json-formatter.runsLocally: Validated and formatted in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.

json-yaml-convert.name: JSON ↔ YAML Converter
json-yaml-convert.blurb: Convert JSON to block-style YAML or YAML back to JSON, entirely in your browser. Parse errors point to the exact line and column, and conversion notes flag the lossy edges like dropped comments and expanded anchors.
json-yaml-convert.inputPlaceholder: Paste JSON or YAML to convert.
json-yaml-convert.runsLocally: Converted in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.

notes.COMMENTS_DROPPED: Comments were dropped. JSON has no way to represent them.
notes.ANCHORS_EXPANDED: Anchors and aliases were expanded inline; the repeated values are written out in full.
notes.MERGE_KEYS_LITERAL: YAML merge keys were kept as literal keys rather than flattened into the surrounding map.
notes.LARGE_NUMBER: This document has a very large number. Conversion uses JavaScript number precision, so values beyond about sixteen digits may be rounded; use the JSON formatter when exact digits matter.

jwks-explainer.name: JWKS explainer + key matcher
jwks-explainer.blurb: Paste a JSON Web Key Set to break down every key, flag any private material, and match a JWT to its key by kid. Nothing leaves your browser.
jwks-explainer.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. No jwks_uri is fetched.
jwks-explainer.jwtInputPlaceholder: Paste a JWT, or just its header
jwks-explainer.privateBanner: This set contains private key material. A JWKS should only ever contain public keys.
jwks-explainer.algMismatch: The JWT alg does not match the matched key alg.

diag.heading: How a key is matched by kid
diag.note: When a JWT arrives, the verifier reads its kid and finds the one key in the set with the same kid, then uses that key to check the signature. Each key needs a distinct kid for this to be unambiguous.

jwt.name: JWT Decoder & Verifier
jwt.blurb: Decode a JSON Web Token's header and claims, read its expiry and timing in plain language, and verify an HS256/384/512 signature with a pasted secret. Runs entirely in your browser.
jwt.runsLocally: Decoding and signature checks run locally. The token and secret never leave your browser.

errors.empty: Paste a JSON Web Token to decode.
errors.format: That does not look like a JWT. A token has three base64url parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature.
errors.header: The header is not valid. It should be base64url-encoded JSON.
errors.payload: The payload is not valid. It should be base64url-encoded JSON.

verify.label: Verify HMAC signature (HS256/384/512)
verify.invalid: Signature does not match this secret.
verify.algNone: This token is unsecured (alg: none), so there is no signature to verify.
verify.unsupportedAlg: In-browser verification covers HS256/384/512 only. {alg} is asymmetric and needs a public key.
verify.hint: The secret is used only to compute an HMAC in your browser. It is never sent anywhere.

struct.heading: Anatomy of a JWT
struct.note: The header and payload are only base64url-encoded, not encrypted, so anyone can read them. The signature is computed over the header and payload joined by a dot, and is what a verifier checks.

tools.lede: Small, fast, privacy-first utilities that run entirely in your browser. Nothing you type is sent anywhere.

nslookup-output-explainer.name: nslookup output explainer
nslookup-output-explainer.blurb: Paste real nslookup output and get a decoded, explained breakdown: the resolver it used, whether the answer is authoritative, every record (with MX / SRV / SOA field breakdowns), and any failures. Parsed entirely in your browser, nothing is resolved or sent anywhere.

input.label: Paste nslookup output
input.placeholder:
Server:	1.1.1.1
Address:	1.1.1.1#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name:	example.com
Address: 93.184.216.34

nslookup-output-explainer.privacy: Parsed entirely in your browser. Nothing is resolved or sent anywhere.
nslookup-output-explainer.notNslookup: This does not look like nslookup output. Paste the full text nslookup prints, including the Server: line.

warnings.title: Worth noticing
warnings.nxdomain: The server reports the name does not exist (NXDOMAIN).
warnings.servfail: The server could not complete the query (SERVFAIL), often a broken delegation, a DNSSEC failure, or an upstream timeout.
warnings.refused: The server refused the query (REFUSED), usually a policy or access-control decision.
warnings.no-servers: No server could be reached (a timeout). Check the resolver address and connectivity.
warnings.non-authoritative: This answer is non-authoritative: it came from the resolver's cache, not directly from a server authoritative for the zone. That is normal for a recursive lookup.

server.title: Resolver used
server.help: This is the DNS server nslookup queried, not the answer. Its address includes the port (#53).

authority.title: Answer authority
authority.nonAuthHelp: The answer came from the resolver's cache. To get an authoritative answer, query a server listed in the zone's NS records directly.

referral.note: nslookup listed the authoritative servers it would refer you to for a definitive answer.

oidc.name: OIDC Decoder
oidc.blurb: Paste an OpenID Connect ID token or a .well-known/openid-configuration document and decode it: the core claims, profile claims, endpoints, and capabilities, with checks for required claims, signing algorithm, nonce, and PKCE.
oidc.inputPlaceholder: Paste an ID token, or an OIDC discovery JSON document
oidc.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally in your browser. It never calls the jwks_uri or any endpoint, and nothing is sent anywhere.
oidc.notVerifiedNote: This tool decodes and explains. It does not verify the token signature, fetch the signing keys, or check expiry against the clock.

errors.empty: Paste an ID token or an openid-configuration document to decode.
errors.malformed-json: This starts like JSON but could not be parsed. Check for a missing brace, comma, or quote.
errors.json-not-oidc: This is JSON, but not an OpenID Connect discovery document. Paste an ID token, or a .well-known/openid-configuration document.
errors.not-jwt: This does not look like a JWT or an OIDC document. Paste an ID token or a discovery document.
errors.jwt-header: The token header could not be decoded. A JWT is three base64url parts separated by dots.
errors.jwt-payload: The token payload could not be decoded as JSON.

reasons.MISSING_REQUIRED_CLAIM: Missing required claim {value}. An ID token must contain iss, sub, aud, exp, and iat.
reasons.ALG_NONE: The token uses alg none, meaning it is unsigned. An unsigned ID token proves nothing and must be rejected.
reasons.ALG_SYMMETRIC: Signed with {value}, a symmetric (HMAC) algorithm. ID tokens from a public provider normally use an asymmetric algorithm like RS256 or ES256 so the relying party can verify with the public key.
reasons.ALG_UNKNOWN: Signed with {value}, an algorithm this tool does not recognize. Confirm it is an expected, strong algorithm.
reasons.SIGNED_ASYMMETRIC: Signed with {value}, an asymmetric algorithm. The relying party verifies it against the provider's public key from the JWKS.
reasons.NO_NONCE: No nonce claim. The nonce binds the token to the authentication request and protects against replay. It should be present when the request sent one.
reasons.MULTI_AUD_NO_AZP: The token has multiple audiences but no azp (authorized party). With more than one audience, azp should name the client the token is for.
reasons.DISCOVERY_ALG_NONE: The provider advertises the none signing algorithm for ID tokens, which would allow unsigned tokens. This should not be offered.
reasons.DISCOVERY_NO_PKCE: No code_challenge_methods_supported. The provider does not advertise PKCE, which is recommended for the authorization code flow.
reasons.DISCOVERY_NO_PKCE_S256: PKCE is advertised but without the S256 method. S256 is the recommended code challenge method.

flow.heading: Authorization code flow
flow.s1: Redirect the user to the provider with scope=openid
flow.s2: Provider authenticates the user and gets consent
flow.s3: Provider redirects back with an authorization code
flow.s4: Relying Party exchanges the code at the token endpoint
flow.s5: Provider returns an ID token plus an access token
flow.s6: Relying Party validates the ID token signature against the JWKS
flow.s7: Relying Party may call UserInfo with the access token

pkce.name: OAuth PKCE Verifier & Challenge
pkce.blurb: Generate an OAuth 2.0 code_verifier and derive its S256 code_challenge, or paste your own and check it against RFC 7636's length and charset rules. The same SHA-256 base64url derivation your authorization server expects. Runs entirely in your browser.
pkce.verifierPlaceholder: Paste a code_verifier, or generate one
pkce.runsLocally: Generation and derivation run locally. Your verifier never leaves your browser.
pkce.plainNote: The plain method is discouraged. Use S256 wherever the client can compute SHA-256.

regex.name: Regex Toolkit
regex.blurb: Test, explain, and debug regular expressions with live matches and a backtracking-risk check.
regex.title: Regex Toolkit
regex.description: Compile a JavaScript regular expression, test it against your own text with every match and capture group highlighted, and read a plain-language breakdown of what the pattern does — all in your browser.
regex.redosBody: This pattern nests an unbounded quantifier inside another (the (a+)+ shape), which can be slow or hang on some inputs. Run it against a short string first.
regex.noMatches: No matches in the test text.
regex.tooLong: The test text is too long to run; shorten it and try again.

saml-decoder.name: SAML Decoder
saml-decoder.blurb: Paste a SAML Response or assertion (raw, base64, or URL-encoded) and decode its issuer, status, subject, conditions, audience, and attributes, with signature and weak-algorithm checks. Hardened against XXE.
saml-decoder.inputPlaceholder: Paste a SAML Response, an assertion, or the base64 SAMLResponse value
saml-decoder.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.
saml-decoder.encryptedNote: {n} encrypted assertion(s) detected. Decrypting requires the service provider's private key and is out of scope for this decoder.
saml-decoder.notVerifiedNote: This tool decodes and explains structure only. It does not verify signatures, validate the message, or decrypt encrypted content.

errors.empty: Paste a SAML message to decode.
errors.not-decodable: This does not look like XML or base64. Paste a SAML Response, an assertion, or its base64 form.
errors.deflated: This decodes to compressed (DEFLATE) bytes, which is the HTTP-Redirect binding. Paste the raw XML or the base64 HTTP-POST form instead.
errors.doctype-forbidden: This message contains a DOCTYPE or entity declaration, which a SAML message must not have. It is rejected to prevent XML external entity (XXE) attacks.
errors.not-xml: The input could not be parsed as XML.
errors.no-saml: This is XML, but not a recognizable SAML message.
errors.malformed: The XML is malformed (an unclosed or mismatched tag).
errors.too-large: The message is too large to decode here.
errors.too-deep: The XML is nested too deeply to decode.
errors.too-many-nodes: The XML has too many elements to decode.

reasons.SIGNED_RESPONSE: The response is signed at the top level.
reasons.SIGNED_ASSERTION: An assertion signature is present ({value} signed).
reasons.UNSIGNED: Nothing in this message is signed. Its authenticity cannot be established, so it must be treated as untrusted until a signature is added and verified.
reasons.WEAK_SIG_ALGO: The signature uses {value}, a weak algorithm. Prefer RSA-SHA256 or stronger.
reasons.WEAK_DIGEST_ALGO: The signature digest uses {value}, a weak hash. Prefer SHA-256 or stronger.
reasons.ENCRYPTED_ASSERTION: {value} assertion(s) are encrypted. This tool reports their presence but does not decrypt them.
reasons.STATUS_NOT_SUCCESS: The response status is {value}, not Success. The login did not succeed.
reasons.NO_CONDITIONS: An assertion has no Conditions element, so it carries no validity window or audience restriction.
reasons.NO_AUDIENCE_RESTRICTION: An assertion has Conditions but no AudienceRestriction, so it is not bound to a specific service provider.
reasons.BEARER_NO_NOTONORAFTER: A bearer SubjectConfirmation has no NotOnOrAfter, so the confirmation does not expire. This weakens replay protection.
reasons.BEARER_NO_RECIPIENT: A bearer SubjectConfirmation has no Recipient, so it is not bound to the intended endpoint.
reasons.MULTIPLE_ASSERTIONS: The message contains {value} assertions. Confirm your service provider processes the one it expects, since multiple assertions can be used in wrapping attacks.

flow.heading: Web browser SSO (SP-initiated)
flow.s1: A user requests a protected resource at the service provider
flow.s2: The SP builds an AuthnRequest and redirects the browser to the IdP
flow.s3: The IdP receives the AuthnRequest over the Redirect binding
flow.s5: The IdP issues a signed Response carrying an Assertion
flow.s6: The browser POSTs the SAMLResponse back to the SP's ACS
flow.s7: The SP checks the signature, conditions, and audience
flow.s8: The SP establishes a session and grants access

secure-headers.name: Secure Headers Analyzer
secure-headers.blurb: Paste an HTTP response and get a graded breakdown of its security headers, cookie flags, and cross-origin policy, checked against OWASP, RFC 6797, CSP Level 3, and RFC 6265bis.
secure-headers.inputPlaceholder:
HTTP/2 200
strict-transport-security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload
content-security-policy: default-src 'self'; object-src 'none'
x-content-type-options: nosniff
set-cookie: id=abc; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax
secure-headers.runsLocally: Analysis runs locally in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.

errors.empty: Paste an HTTP response or a block of headers to analyze.
errors.no-headers: No headers found. Paste a response with header lines like 'Header-Name: value'.

reasons.HSTS_MISSING: No Strict-Transport-Security header. Browsers may use plain HTTP, leaving the first request open to downgrade attacks.
reasons.HSTS_MAXAGE_ZERO: max-age is {value}, which disables HSTS. Set a positive max-age such as 31536000 (one year).
reasons.HSTS_MAXAGE_LOW: max-age is only {value} seconds, under the recommended six-month minimum.
reasons.HSTS_MAXAGE_OK: max-age is {value} seconds, acceptable but below the one-year baseline.
reasons.HSTS_MAXAGE_STRONG: max-age is {value} seconds (one year or more), the recommended baseline.
reasons.HSTS_SUBDOMAINS: includeSubDomains is set, extending HTTPS enforcement to every subdomain.
reasons.HSTS_NO_SUBDOMAINS: includeSubDomains is not set, so subdomains are not covered.
reasons.HSTS_PRELOAD: preload is set, making the host eligible for the browser HSTS preload list.
reasons.CSP_MISSING: No Content-Security-Policy. The main defence-in-depth control against cross-site scripting is absent.
reasons.CSP_HAS_DEFAULT_SRC: A default-src directive is set, giving every resource type a baseline policy.
reasons.CSP_NO_DEFAULT_SRC: No default-src directive, so resource types without their own directive are unrestricted.
reasons.CSP_HAS_OBJECT_SRC: object-src is set, restricting legacy plugin content.
reasons.CSP_HAS_BASE_URI: base-uri is set, preventing base-tag injection.
reasons.CSP_FRAME_ANCESTORS: frame-ancestors is set, the modern control against framing and clickjacking.
reasons.CSP_UNSAFE_INLINE: Uses 'unsafe-inline', which permits inline scripts or styles and largely defeats the XSS protection of CSP.
reasons.CSP_UNSAFE_EVAL: Uses 'unsafe-eval', which permits eval() and similar dynamic code execution.
reasons.CSP_WILDCARD: A wildcard '*' source allows content from any origin, weakening the policy.
reasons.CSP_RO_ABSENT: No report-only policy is set. This is optional and used to stage a CSP before enforcing it.
reasons.CSP_RO_ONLY: A report-only CSP is present but there is no enforced Content-Security-Policy. Report-only does not protect the page.
reasons.CSP_RO_WITH_ENFORCED: A report-only CSP is present alongside the enforced policy, useful for testing changes.
reasons.XCTO_MISSING: No X-Content-Type-Options. Browsers may MIME-sniff responses, risking content being run as script.
reasons.XCTO_NOSNIFF: Set to nosniff, which stops MIME-type sniffing.
reasons.XCTO_INVALID: Unexpected value '{value}'. The only valid value is nosniff.
reasons.XFO_MISSING: No X-Frame-Options. Without it, or CSP frame-ancestors, the page can be framed for clickjacking.
reasons.XFO_DENY: Set to DENY, preventing the page from being framed by any site.
reasons.XFO_SAMEORIGIN: Set to SAMEORIGIN, allowing framing only by same-origin pages.
reasons.XFO_ALLOWFROM_DEPRECATED: Uses ALLOW-FROM, which is obsolete and ignored by modern browsers. Use CSP frame-ancestors instead.
reasons.XFO_INVALID: Unexpected value '{value}'. Use DENY or SAMEORIGIN.
reasons.REFERRER_MISSING: No Referrer-Policy. The browser default applies, which may leak full URLs to other origins.
reasons.REFERRER_WEAK: Set to '{value}', which still sends the origin or more to other sites.
reasons.REFERRER_UNSAFE_URL: Set to unsafe-url, which sends the full URL, including path and query, to every destination.
reasons.PERMISSIONS_MISSING: No Permissions-Policy. Powerful features such as camera, microphone, and geolocation are not restricted.
reasons.PERMISSIONS_PRESENT: Present, restricting access to powerful browser features.
reasons.COOP_MISSING: No Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy. The page shares a browsing-context group with cross-origin openers.
reasons.COOP_SAME_ORIGIN: Set to same-origin, isolating the page from cross-origin windows.
reasons.COOP_ALLOW_POPUPS: Set to same-origin-allow-popups, isolating the page while keeping popups it opens.
reasons.COOP_UNSAFE_NONE: Set to unsafe-none, which provides no opener isolation.
reasons.COEP_ABSENT: No Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy. This is optional and required only for cross-origin isolation.
reasons.COEP_PRESENT: Set to '{value}', requiring embedded resources to opt in.
reasons.CORP_ABSENT: No Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy. This is optional and limits who may embed this resource.
reasons.CORP_SAME_ORIGIN: Set to same-origin, the tightest setting.
reasons.CORP_SAME_SITE: Set to same-site, allowing same-site embedding.
reasons.CORP_CROSS_ORIGIN: Set to cross-origin, allowing any site to embed this resource. Make sure that is intended.
reasons.CACHE_ABSENT: No Cache-Control header. Set no-store on responses that carry sensitive data.
reasons.CACHE_RESTRICTED: Caching is restricted (no-store, no-cache, or private), appropriate for sensitive responses.
reasons.CACHE_PERMISSIVE: Caching is permissive. Make sure the response contains no sensitive data.
reasons.CORS_WILDCARD: Access-Control-Allow-Origin is '*', letting any site read the response. This is safe only for public, credential-free data.
reasons.CORS_WILDCARD_CREDENTIALS: Access-Control-Allow-Origin is '*' together with credentials. This exposes credentialed data to any origin, a serious misconfiguration.
reasons.CORS_NULL_ORIGIN: Access-Control-Allow-Origin is 'null', which several contexts can forge. Avoid the null origin.
reasons.CORS_SPECIFIC_ORIGIN: Access-Control-Allow-Origin allows '{value}'. Confirm that origin is trusted.
reasons.CORS_CREDENTIALS_TRUE: Access-Control-Allow-Credentials is true, so the response may carry cookies cross-origin. Pair this only with a specific, trusted origin.
reasons.XPCDP_ABSENT: No X-Permitted-Cross-Domain-Policies. OWASP recommends setting it to none.
reasons.XPCDP_NONE: Set to none, the hardened value.
reasons.XPCDP_ALL: Set to all, the most permissive value. Prefer none.
reasons.CLEAR_SITE_DATA_PRESENT: Clear-Site-Data is set ('{value}'), telling the browser to clear site data. Use it on logout endpoints.
reasons.FEATURE_POLICY_DEPRECATED: Feature-Policy is deprecated. Migrate to Permissions-Policy.
reasons.HPKP_DEPRECATED: HTTP Public Key Pinning is deprecated and removed from browsers. Remove this header.
reasons.EXPECT_CT_OBSOLETE: Expect-CT is obsolete ('{value}'). Certificate Transparency is enforced by default, so remove it.
reasons.XSS_PROTECTION_LEGACY: X-XSS-Protection is '{value}'. The legacy auditor is gone from modern browsers. Remove it, or set it to 0, and rely on CSP.
reasons.XSS_PROTECTION_DISABLED_OK: X-XSS-Protection is 0, which correctly disables the obsolete auditor.
reasons.SERVER_VERSION_DISCLOSED: Server reveals version detail ('{value}'), helping attackers target known flaws. Minimise it.
reasons.SERVER_PRODUCT_DISCLOSED: Server names the product ('{value}'). Consider removing or minimising it.
reasons.XPOWEREDBY_PRESENT: X-Powered-By reveals the stack ('{value}'). Remove it.
reasons.XASPNETVERSION_PRESENT: X-AspNet-Version reveals the runtime version ('{value}'). Remove it.
reasons.XASPNETMVCVERSION_PRESENT: X-AspNetMvc-Version reveals the framework version ('{value}'). Remove it.
reasons.COOKIE_SECURE: Secure is set, so the cookie is sent only over HTTPS.
reasons.COOKIE_NO_SECURE: Secure is not set, so the cookie can be sent over plain HTTP.
reasons.COOKIE_HTTPONLY: HttpOnly is set, hiding the cookie from JavaScript.
reasons.COOKIE_NO_HTTPONLY: HttpOnly is not set, so script can read the cookie and an XSS flaw could steal it.
reasons.COOKIE_SAMESITE_STRICT: SameSite=Strict, the strongest cross-site request protection.
reasons.COOKIE_SAMESITE_NONE: SameSite=None with Secure, allowing cross-site sending.
reasons.COOKIE_SAMESITE_NONE_INSECURE: SameSite=None without Secure. Browsers reject this and it is insecure. Add Secure.
reasons.COOKIE_NO_SAMESITE: No SameSite attribute. Set Lax or Strict for cross-site request resistance.
reasons.COOKIE_HOST_PREFIX_OK: The __Host- prefix requirements are met (Secure, Path=/, and no Domain).
reasons.COOKIE_HOST_PREFIX_VIOLATION: The __Host- prefix requires Secure, Path=/, and no Domain. Those conditions are not all met.
reasons.COOKIE_SECURE_PREFIX_OK: The __Secure- prefix requirement is met (Secure is set).
reasons.COOKIE_SECURE_PREFIX_VIOLATION: The __Secure- prefix requires Secure, which is not set.
reasons.OVERALL_INFO_LEAK: {value} header(s) disclose server or stack details.
reasons.OVERALL_CORS_RISK: A cross-origin (CORS) policy exposes data to other sites.
reasons.OVERALL_POOR: Several core security headers are missing.

ssrf-url-classifier.name: SSRF URL classifier
ssrf-url-classifier.blurb: Paste a URL and see where it actually points: loopback, a private or link-local range, a cloud metadata endpoint, CGNAT, reserved space, or the public internet. Decimal, octal, and hex IP obfuscation is decoded, dangerous schemes and embedded credentials are flagged, and an SSRF risk level is shown. It never resolves DNS and never sends the request.
ssrf-url-classifier.privacy: Everything is computed in your browser. This tool never resolves DNS and never sends the request; it classifies the address purely from the text you paste.

categoryDesc.loopback: The host itself (127.0.0.0/8 or ::1). A server fetching this reaches its own local services.
categoryDesc.private: An RFC 1918 private range (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) or IPv6 ULA. Typically internal infrastructure.
categoryDesc.link-local: A link-local address (169.254.0.0/16 or fe80::/10), reachable only on the local segment.
categoryDesc.cloud-metadata: A cloud instance metadata endpoint (IMDS). Reaching it can expose credentials and instance data.
categoryDesc.cgnat: Carrier-grade NAT shared address space (100.64.0.0/10). Not public, but not classic private either.
categoryDesc.reserved: A reserved or special-use range (documentation, benchmarking, multicast, broadcast, future use).
categoryDesc.public: A public, routable address on the internet.
categoryDesc.internal-name: A hostname with an internal suffix (.internal, .local, .corp). Not a public name.
categoryDesc.unresolved: A hostname whose address is only known after a DNS lookup, which this tool does not perform.

flags.title: Flags
flags.obfuscated: The host is written in {form} form, which decodes to {ip}. This is a common way to slip an internal address past a filter that only blocks dotted-decimal.
flags.scheme: The {scheme}:// scheme is a non-HTTP fetch that escalates SSRF, for example local-file read or protocol smuggling to another service.
flags.userinfo: Credentials are embedded in the URL (user:pass@host). The real host may ignore them while a naive filter is fooled into reading the wrong part as the host.

defenses.title: Defenses
defenses.allowlist: Prefer an allow-list of exact hosts over a block-list; block-lists miss encodings and new ranges.
defenses.blockRanges: Reject loopback, private, link-local, CGNAT, and metadata ranges for both IPv4 and IPv6.
defenses.resolveThenCheck: Resolve the hostname first, then check every resulting address, to catch DNS that points at an internal IP.
defenses.noRedirects: Disable or re-validate redirects; a public URL can 302 to an internal one.
defenses.schemes: Allow only http and https; reject file, gopher, dict, ftp, and similar schemes.

ssrf-url-classifier.classifierNote: This is a classifier for learning and allow-list design. It never resolves the hostname and never sends the request, so a public hostname is reported as resolving at runtime rather than guessed.

f5.tmos: TMOS - Traffic Management Operating System · F5OS · Platforms
f5.dns-gtm: BIG-IP DNS (formerly GTM - Global Traffic Manager)
f5.asm-awaf: F5 AWAF - Advanced WAF (formerly BIG-IP ASM - Application Security Manager)
f5.zta-apm: BIG-IP Zero Trust Access (formerly BIG-IP APM - Access Policy Manager)

syslog-pri-decoder.name: Syslog PRI decoder + encoder
syslog-pri-decoder.blurb: Decode a syslog PRI such as 134 into its facility and severity, or encode them back, all in your browser.
syslog-pri-decoder.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
syslog-pri-decoder.facilityVaries: Facilities 12 to 15 are not standardized in RFC 5424 and vary by implementation.

struct.heading: How a PRI splits into facility and severity
struct.note: A single PRI integer carries both fields. Dividing by 8 gives the facility, and the remainder gives the severity, so PRI 134 is facility 16 and severity 6. Encoding runs the other way: facility × 8 + severity.

telemetry-streaming-explainer.name: Telemetry Streaming (TS) explainer
telemetry-streaming-explainer.blurb: Paste the JSON you POST to /mgmt/shared/telemetry/declare and it reads it back: it confirms the top-level Telemetry class, reads the optional Controls, and walks every class-object grouped by its role in the telemetry pipeline: the data sources that produce telemetry (system pollers, event listeners), the consumers that forward it out (Splunk, Azure, AWS, DataDog, Prometheus, and the rest of the catalogue), and the grouping and endpoint classes. It flags the pipeline gaps that make a declaration succeed but do nothing. TS is the third F5 Automation Toolchain extension: AS3 and DO configure the box, TS observes it.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.inputLabel: A TS declaration (the JSON you POST to /mgmt/shared/telemetry/declare)
telemetry-streaming-explainer.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.empty: Paste a TS declaration to see it explained.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.scopeNote: This is a structure explainer and sanity checker, not a full JSON-Schema validator. It never contacts a BIG-IP and nothing you paste leaves the browser. A declaration that passes here can still be rejected by TS itself. Note: F5 has placed Telemetry Streaming in maintenance mode; it remains supported with critical and security fixes, with no deprecation planned.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.walkOrderNote: Grouped by each class's role: data sources produce telemetry (system pollers collect statistics, event listeners ingest logs), consumers forward it to an external system, and the supporting classes group and extend the rest. A working declaration needs at least one source and at least one consumer.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.unknownClass: (class not in this tool's catalog, but still reported)

finding.notTelemetry: This is not a TS declaration. The top-level class must be Telemetry.
finding.noSource: No data source. Add a Telemetry_System (with a systemPoller) or a Telemetry_Listener, or the declaration collects nothing to forward.
finding.noConsumer: No consumer. Add a Telemetry_Consumer (push) or Telemetry_Pull_Consumer (pull), or the telemetry has nowhere to go.
finding.systemWithoutPoller: The Telemetry_System "{name}" has no systemPoller (and no iHealthPoller). To collect metrics, a Telemetry_System must define systemPoller.
finding.consumerMissingType: The consumer "{name}" has no type. A Telemetry_Consumer must set a type to select its target.
finding.consumerUnknownType: The consumer "{name}" has type "{type}", which is not in this tool's catalogue of TS consumer types (it may be newer than this build, or a typo).
finding.namespacesPresent: This declaration uses Telemetry_Namespace: pollers, listeners, and consumers inside a namespace operate as an isolated unit and only see each other.
finding.controlsNote: Controls is set with logLevel "{logLevel}" (used for logging and debugging; set debug true to send a test event to your consumers).

tools.title: Tools that compute, never guess.

totp-hotp.name: TOTP / HOTP Generator & Validator
totp-hotp.blurb: Generate and check time-based (TOTP, RFC 6238) and counter-based (HOTP, RFC 4226) one-time passwords - the codes behind authenticator apps and hardware tokens such as FortiToken. SHA-1/256/512, configurable digits and step. Computed locally with Web Crypto; your secret never leaves your browser.
totp-hotp.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Your secret is never sent anywhere.
totp-hotp.secretError: That secret could not be decoded with the selected encoding. Check the value and the Base32 / Hex / ASCII toggle.
totp-hotp.validMatch: Match - this code is valid right now.
totp-hotp.validNoMatch: No match for the current parameters.
totp-hotp.validWindowNote: Real validators usually accept the adjacent steps too; this check allows +/-1 step of clock drift.
totp-hotp.explainNote: HMAC the 8-byte moving factor under your secret, take the low nibble of the last byte as an offset, read 4 bytes there as a 31-bit number, then take that modulo 10^digits. That is RFC 4226 dynamic truncation; TOTP just feeds the time step in as the counter.

url-inspector.name: URL Inspector
url-inspector.blurb: Dissect any URL into its named parts: scheme, host, port, path, query parameters, and fragment. Decodes percent-escapes and internationalized hosts, and flags credentials and other issues. Runs entirely in your browser.
url-inspector.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. The URL is never sent anywhere.
url-inspector.emptyQuery: The query string is present but empty.

reasons.RELATIVE_REFERENCE: This is a relative reference, not an absolute URL: it has no scheme and no host.
reasons.NO_SCHEME: No scheme is present, so this URL is not absolute.
reasons.CREDENTIALS_IN_URL: This URL embeds a username and password. Credentials in a URL are easily logged, cached, and shared, and should be avoided.
reasons.USERINFO_PRESENT: This URL includes a userinfo component (a username before the host).
reasons.PLAINTEXT_SCHEME: The {value} scheme is not encrypted in transit. Anything sent over it can be read on the network.
reasons.NON_DEFAULT_PORT: The port {value} is not the default for this scheme.
reasons.REDUNDANT_DEFAULT_PORT: The port {value} is the default for this scheme and can be omitted.
reasons.IDN_HOST: The host is internationalized and stored in punycode; the Unicode form is shown above. Watch for look-alike characters in untrusted links.
reasons.UPPERCASE_SCHEME: The scheme contains uppercase letters. Schemes are case-insensitive and are normally written in lowercase.
reasons.UPPERCASE_HOST: The host contains uppercase letters. Host names are case-insensitive and are normally written in lowercase.
reasons.PERCENT_ENCODED: This URL contains percent-encoded characters, which have been decoded in the breakdown above.
reasons.PLUS_IN_QUERY: The query contains a plus sign. Under form encoding (application/x-www-form-urlencoded) a plus means a space; under strict RFC 3986 it is a literal plus. The values above keep it literal.

url-inspector.note: Components are shown exactly as written, without normalization. Parsing follows RFC 3986; the query is split on & and =, and each part is percent-decoded.

uuid.name: UUID Generator & Inspector (v4 / v7)
uuid.blurb: Generate random v4 or time-ordered v7 UUIDs, or paste any UUID to read its version, variant, and (for v7) the embedded creation timestamp. Generation uses the browser's secure random source. Runs entirely in your browser.
uuid.runsLocally: Generation and inspection run locally. Nothing is sent anywhere.
uuid.inspectPlaceholder: Paste any UUID to decode it

x509.name: X.509 Certificate Decoder
x509.blurb: Paste a PEM, base64, or hex certificate to read its subject, issuer, validity window, public key, and v3 extensions, with SHA-256 and SHA-1 fingerprints. Runs entirely in your browser.
x509.runsLocally: Decoding and fingerprints run locally. The certificate never leaves your browser.

errors.empty: Paste an X.509 certificate to decode.
errors.format: That does not look like a certificate. Paste PEM (-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----), base64, or hex.
errors.der: The bytes are not valid DER. Check that the whole certificate was pasted, including the END line.
errors.structure: This decodes as ASN.1 but is not an X.509 certificate. A certificate is a SEQUENCE of TBSCertificate, signatureAlgorithm, and signatureValue.

rev.noneDetail: No CRL or OCSP pointers are present in this certificate.
rev.note: These are the revocation endpoints named in the certificate. This tool reads them but never contacts them; the revocation status check happens in your client, not here.

sct.intro: Embedded SCTs: {n}. Each is a CT log's signed proof that it recorded this certificate; browsers require a minimum number for the certificate to be trusted.
sct.note: Signatures are shown but not verified here; verifying an SCT needs the log's public key and the precertificate.

x509.roleIntermediate: Signed by a root; signs leaf certificates
x509.trustNote: Trust flows downward: each certificate is signed by the one above, up to a root your system already trusts.

xml-decoder.name: XML Decoder
xml-decoder.blurb: Paste XML and read its structure: the declaration, the DOCTYPE and any entities, the element tree with namespaces and attributes, plus a security check of the XML attack surface. Nothing is fetched or resolved.
xml-decoder.privacy: Parsed entirely in your browser. No entity is resolved, no external reference is fetched, and nothing is uploaded.
xml-decoder.notXml: This does not look like XML. Paste a document that starts with an element or an XML declaration.

security.title: Security analysis
security.doctype-present: A DOCTYPE is present. The DOCTYPE is the gateway to XML entity attacks; a hardened parser disables it entirely.
security.external-dtd: The DOCTYPE references an external DTD. A parser that fetches it can be steered to attacker-controlled content.
security.external-entity: An external entity is declared (SYSTEM or PUBLIC). Resolving it would read a local file or make a network request: the classic XXE vector.
security.parameter-entity: A parameter entity is declared. Parameter entities are used to build blind and out-of-band XXE payloads.
security.entity-expansion: Entities reference other entities. Chained expansion can grow exponentially and exhaust memory, the billion laughs denial of service.

errors.unexpected-close: Closing tag with no matching open: {detail}
errors.multiple-roots: Multiple root elements ({detail}). XML must have exactly one.
errors.unterminated-cdata: A CDATA section was never closed.

fortios-sniffer-builder.name: FortiOS packet sniffer builder
fortios-sniffer-builder.blurb: Build a FortiGate diagnose sniffer packet command from parts, or paste one to have every argument explained: interface, filter, verbosity 1-6, count, and timestamp format.
fortios-sniffer-builder.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you enter or paste leaves this page.
fortios-sniffer-builder.pasteLabel: Paste a diagnose sniffer packet command
fortios-sniffer-builder.pastePlaceholder: diagnose sniffer packet any 'host 10.1.1.1 and tcp port 443' 4 0 l

pac-file-explainer.name: PAC file explainer and validator
pac-file-explainer.blurb: Paste a Proxy Auto-Config file and it reads back the proxy directives it returns, the PAC helper functions it uses (with the DNS-consulting ones flagged), structural and correctness lints, and whether it is a Netskope explicit-proxy steering file. Never runs the file.
pac-file-explainer.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser and never evaluates the file. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
pac-file-explainer.returnValuesTitle: Return values a PAC can produce

exos-config-explainer.name: ExtremeXOS config explainer
exos-config-explainer.blurb: Paste an ExtremeXOS (EXOS / Switch Engine) configuration and it explains each command in plain English, summarizes the VLANs (tag, tagged and untagged ports, IP addresses), and groups the commands by category. Never touches a switch.
exos-config-explainer.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
exos-config-explainer.inputPlaceholder:
create vlan engineering tag 100
configure vlan engineering add ports 1:1, 1:2 tagged
configure vlan engineering ipaddress 10.1.1.1/24
enable ipforwarding

acme-dns01.name: ACME dns-01 TXT computer
acme-dns01.blurb: Compute the TXT record for an ACME dns-01 challenge, from the token and account key.
acme-dns01.tokenPlaceholder: the token from the dns-01 challenge
acme-dns01.keyPlaceholder: the public account key as a JWK, or its base64url thumbprint
acme-dns01.runsLocally: Runs locally. Only the key's public members are used; nothing leaves your browser.
acme-dns01.recordNote: Publish this as a TXT record, wait for it to propagate, then let your ACME client continue.

public-suffix.name: Registered domain (eTLD+1)
public-suffix.blurb: Find the public suffix (eTLD) and registered domain (eTLD+1) for any hostname, using the Public Suffix List.
public-suffix.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser against a bundled list. Nothing is sent anywhere.
public-suffix.noRegistrable: This host is itself a public suffix, so nothing is registrable at this exact name.
public-suffix.icannViewNote: The interpretation certificate rate limits and same-site checks use.

letsencrypt-rate-limits.name: Let's Encrypt rate-limit planner
letsencrypt-rate-limits.blurb: Plan certificate issuance for a set of hostnames: group them by registered domain and see how they map onto Let's Encrypt's rate limits.
letsencrypt-rate-limits.namesPlaceholder: One hostname per line, e.g. www.example.com
letsencrypt-rate-limits.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.
letsencrypt-rate-limits.exceedsWarning: At least one registered domain would exceed the weekly limit if you issue one certificate per name. Pack names onto fewer certificates or use a wildcard.
letsencrypt-rate-limits.groupExceeds: One certificate per name here would exceed 50 per week. Pack them onto fewer certificates, or cover subdomains with a wildcard.
letsencrypt-rate-limits.ipNote: IP addresses use their own unit: the exact IPv4 address, or the containing IPv6 /64. They are not grouped as domains.
letsencrypt-rate-limits.invalidNote: These were not valid hostnames, or were bare public suffixes with nothing registrable, so they were skipped.
letsencrypt-rate-limits.limitExactSet: Same exact set of names (7 days)
letsencrypt-rate-limits.ariNote: Renewals coordinated by ARI are exempt from all rate limits.

f5-irules-runtime-calculator.name: iRules runtime calculator
f5-irules-runtime-calculator.blurb: Turn iRule timing statistics into real runtime, CPU cost per request, and maximum requests per second.
f5-irules-runtime-calculator.timingLabel: iRule timing output (from tmsh show ltm rule field-fmt)
f5-irules-runtime-calculator.timingPlaceholder: Paste the field-fmt event blocks (avg-cycles, min-cycles, max-cycles, total-executions), or lines of the form: HTTP_REQUEST 729 total 0 fail 0 abort | Cycles (min, avg, max) = (3693, 3959, 53936)
f5-irules-runtime-calculator.overrideHint: Leave blank to use cores x clock, exactly as the spreadsheet does. Enter a platform-specific figure to override it.
f5-irules-runtime-calculator.downloadNote: Prefer a spreadsheet? Download the branded, offline-ready workbook with the same four tables, live formulas, and a guided walkthrough:

f5-irules-performance-linter.name: iRules performance linter
f5-irules-performance-linter.blurb: Paste an iRule; it flags F5-documented performance anti-patterns line by line, with severity, cause, and fix. The big one: global variables demote CMP. Local, no network.
f5-irules-performance-linter.irPlaceholder: Paste an iRule to scan for documented performance anti-patterns
f5-irules-performance-linter.summaryClean: No high-confidence anti-patterns found. This scan is not a substitute for measurement.
f5-irules-performance-linter.catGlobalDetail: Global variables demote the virtual server to a single TMM instance (CMP demotion), so one core handles all of its traffic. They have been deprecated since v10, and the old data-group-as-global form raises a runtime error and resets the client on v11 and later.
f5-irules-performance-linter.catGlobalFix: Use the CMP-safe static:: namespace for shared constants, ordinary local variables for per-connection data, or the class command for data groups. Never prefix a data group name with two colons.
f5-irules-performance-linter.catExprDetail: Without braces, Tcl substitutes and re-parses the expression and can double-substitute; bracing lets the bytecode compiler optimize it.
f5-irules-performance-linter.catExprFix: Wrap the expression in curly braces after expr.
f5-irules-performance-linter.catMatchclassDetail: matchclass and findclass were deprecated in v10; the class command offers better functionality and performance.
f5-irules-performance-linter.catMatchclassFix: Use class match or class search.
f5-irules-performance-linter.catRegexTitle: Regular expression in the data path
f5-irules-performance-linter.catRegexDetail: regexp and regsub cost materially more than fixed string work.
f5-irules-performance-linter.catRegexFix: When the match is a prefix, suffix, exact value, or simple glob, use string, scan, switch -glob, or a data group instead.
f5-irules-performance-linter.profileNote: This is a static scan. For the actual cost of a rule, measure it:

f5-release-cadence-calendar.name: F5 release cadence calendar
f5-release-cadence-calendar.blurb: F5 moved to a monthly security cadence in July 2026. This computes the upcoming hardened-release and security-notification dates so you can plan patch windows. Local, no network.
f5-release-cadence-calendar.note1: Every date is a third Wednesday: that month's hardened release ships, and the previous month's security notification is published one month after the release it covers.
f5-release-cadence-calendar.note2: These are the scheduled dates. F5 also issues out-of-band security alerts and engineering hotfixes for urgent issues at any time, and retains flexibility to adjust notification timing.

f5-bigd-thread-calculator.name: BigD thread calculator
f5-bigd-thread-calculator.blurb: Enter your vCPU count (and whether the system is hyperthreaded) to get the BigD thread count BIG-IP 21.1 derives automatically, per F5's documented formulas, plus the bigd.numprocs override cap and the 15,000-monitor ceiling.
f5-bigd-thread-calculator.inputPlaceholder: 8 r10900, 16 r4800, 22 velos, 8 ht, or just 6
f5-bigd-thread-calculator.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser: two documented formulas, no network, no clock.
f5-bigd-thread-calculator.fractionNote: F5 documents the formula but not a rounding rule for fractional results; the exact value and its whole-thread floor are both shown. The running system's own count is authoritative.
f5-bigd-thread-calculator.zeroNote: At 2 vCPUs the documented formula yields 0; bigd still runs as a single multi-threaded instance, so treat this edge as "minimal threading" rather than "no bigd".

config.heading: Override and ceiling
config.numprocsNote: bigd.numprocs sets the thread count by hand but is capped at the number of available vCPUs; its default 0 means the count is calculated automatically with the formula above.

f5-bigd-thread-calculator.verifyNote: Formulas verbatim from F5's BIG-IP 21.1.0 release notes (BigD enhancements for large-scale configurations). For sizing beyond 5,000 monitor instances, F5's 21.0 notes point to In-TMM monitors.

errors.empty: Enter a vCPU count, optionally followed by ht or normal.
errors.format: Could not read that. Try a count plus a type, like "8 ht" or "16 normal".
errors.range: The vCPU count must be a whole number between 1 and 1024.

platform.heading: Platform:
platform.htNote: This platform is hyperthreaded: each Intel core appears as two vCPUs, so the hyperthreaded formula applies to the vCPU count the tenant sees.
platform.normalNote: This platform does not use hyperthreading: it counts physical CPU cores only, so the normal formula applies.
platform.veNote: Virtual Edition inherits whatever the hypervisor exposes. Check inside the guest (lscpu: "Thread(s) per core", 2 means hyperthreaded) and pick the matching formula; both are shown.
platform.rseriesAmbiguous: rSeries splits down the middle: r5000/r10000/r12000 are hyperthreaded, r2000/r4000 are not. Enter the model (e.g. r10900, r4800) and the right formula is selected.
platform.not21x: Context only: this platform cannot run BIG-IP 21.x, and the multi-threaded BigD with these formulas is a 21.1 feature. It stays on 17.x with the classic bigd.
platform.overridden: Your explicit ht/normal choice overrides this platform's documented default (useful when hyperthreading is disabled in firmware).

tools.devOtherTagline: off the catalogue, still on principle

fault-hypothesis-builder.name: Fault Hypothesis Builder
fault-hypothesis-builder.blurb: Describe a fault in six structured fields; a fixed rule set ranks hypotheses to test - with the evidence to collect, what would support each, and what would weaken it. Advisory structure, never a diagnosis.

form.heading: Describe the fault (quick mode)
form.clues: Layer clues you have observed (all that apply)

options.cannot-reach-new-service: Cannot reach a NEW service (never worked)
options.works-by-ip-not-name: Works by IP, not by name
options.one-path-works-other-not: One path works, another does not

fault-hypothesis-builder.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser. The free-text notes flow only into the exported worksheet - they never influence the ranking, and nothing is uploaded.
fault-hypothesis-builder.emptyState: Pick what you are seeing above and the hypotheses to test appear here - ranked, with evidence, live as you refine.

notes.heading: Optional detail for the export (does not affect the ranking)
notes.hint: These lines are copied verbatim into the worksheet's Situation section so a ticket or TAC case starts complete.

results.heading: Hypotheses to test
results.framing: Ranked by deterministic rules over what you described. Evidence decides - each card shows what would support the hypothesis and what would weaken it.
results.whyIntro: Every rule that fired, with its points. The ranking is the sum - inspectable, never an oracle.

listHead.gvTitle: golden vectors: known-correct outputs pinned in CI
listHead.svTitle: snapshot vectors: exact rule-firing and ranking pinned in CI (advisory tool)

tools.roomsGreenTitle: Off the catalogue, still on principle
tools.roomsGreenDesc: Tools that cannot sign the main floor's contract yet - a manifest, golden vectors, a fixed shape - because their form is still finding itself. Early or odd, not lesser, and labeled as such. Same privacy, same local-first compute.
tools.roomsRedDesc: The one place a tool sends your input out of the browser - to the official service named on screen, and only when you press Ask. Live answers from the real internet, with the rules of engagement stated out loud and the endpoint named before anything is sent.

change-window-runbook-builder.name: Change Window Runbook Builder
change-window-runbook-builder.blurb: Describe a planned change in six structured fields; a fixed rule set assembles an ordered runbook across six phases - with the risks the plan carries and readiness cautions about the input. Advisory structure to review and adapt, never an approval.

form.heading: Describe the change (quick mode)
form.changeType: What kind of change is this?
form.safeguards: What is already in place? (all that apply)

change-window-runbook-builder.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser. The free-text notes flow only into the exported runbook - they never influence which steps appear, and nothing is uploaded.
change-window-runbook-builder.emptyState: Describe the change above and the runbook appears here - phased, with the risks it carries, live as you refine.

notes.heading: Optional detail for the export (does not affect the runbook)
notes.hint: These lines are copied verbatim into the runbook's Change summary so a change ticket starts complete.

results.heading: Your runbook
results.framing: Assembled by deterministic rules over what you described - a proposal to review and adapt, not an approval. Work the phases in order; check off steps as you go.
results.whyIntro: Every rule that fired, with its reason. The runbook is the sum - inspectable, never an oracle.

incident-timeline-rca-builder.name: Incident Timeline & RCA Builder
incident-timeline-rca-builder.blurb: RCA stands for root cause analysis - the disciplined walk from an incident's symptoms back to the conditions that allowed it. Enter an incident's events as a small timeline and mark the contributing-factor domains you observed; a fixed rule set orders the timeline, derives the milestone spans, and structures candidate contributing factors with the evidence for each. It never names a root cause - candidates to investigate, not a verdict.

form.heading: Build the incident timeline
form.timelineHint: Add the incident's events and set an order for each (lower runs earlier). The tool has no clock - you control the sequence; spans are counted in events.
form.factorsHint: Tick the domains you have reason to investigate. Tick "mark confirmed" only for a factor you have confirmed from your own evidence - the tool never confirms one for you.

incident-timeline-rca-builder.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser. The free-text notes flow only into the exported scaffold - they never influence which candidates appear, and nothing is uploaded.
incident-timeline-rca-builder.emptyState: Build the timeline above and the RCA scaffold appears here - the ordered timeline, the milestone spans, and the candidate factors to investigate, live as you refine.

notes.heading: Optional detail for the export (does not affect the scaffold)
notes.hint: These lines are copied verbatim into the exported scaffold so the review write-up starts complete.

results.heading: Your RCA scaffold
results.framing: Structured by deterministic rules over what you described - candidates to investigate with the evidence each would need, not a conclusion. A root cause is confirmed by people from evidence, in a real review.
results.candidatesIntro: Each is a domain to test, not a conclusion. Confirm or rule out each against your own evidence; the tool never confirms one for you.
results.ruleOutHeading: Evidence that would rule it out
results.whyIntro: Every rule that fired, with its reason. The scaffold is the sum - inspectable, never an oracle.

change-blast-radius-mapper.name: Change Blast-Radius Mapper
change-blast-radius-mapper.blurb: Describe what you are changing and its structure; a fixed rule set maps the blast radius as concentric tiers (target, co-located, downstream, human) populated with what could be affected, plus severity-tagged risks and containment measures. It maps what could be affected, never asserts what will break.

form.heading: Describe the change (quick mode)
form.colocation: Does it share hardware or a platform?
form.trafficPath: Is it in the traffic path?
form.dependents: How many things depend on it?
form.userReach: How far does the user reach extend?

change-blast-radius-mapper.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser. It maps categories from what you describe - it has no view of your real topology - and nothing is uploaded.
change-blast-radius-mapper.emptyState: Describe the change above and the blast-radius map appears here - concentric tiers with what could be affected, the risks, and containment measures, live as you refine.

notes.heading: Optional detail for the export (does not affect the map)
notes.hint: These lines are copied verbatim into the exported assessment.

results.heading: Blast-radius map
results.framing: Mapped by deterministic rules over what you described - the categories of thing in each tier that could be affected, not an enumeration of your topology. Confirm the specifics against your real environment.
results.whyIntro: Every rule that fired, with its reason. The map is the sum - inspectable, never an oracle.

tac-escalation-packet-builder.name: TAC Escalation Packet Builder
tac-escalation-packet-builder.blurb: Describe the issue you are about to escalate; a fixed rule set assembles a complete vendor-support packet and a checklist of the artifacts still to collect before opening the case, dropping what you already have. It structures the hand-off; it does not open a case or contact any vendor.

form.heading: Describe the escalation (quick mode)
form.severity: Severity (as the vendor would grade it)
form.collected: What have you already collected? (all that apply)
form.tried: What have you already tried? (all that apply)

tac-escalation-packet-builder.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser. It does not open a case, contact any vendor, or collect diagnostics - it names which to attach - and nothing is uploaded.
tac-escalation-packet-builder.emptyState: Describe the escalation above and the packet appears here - the assembled sections and the checklist of what to collect before opening the case, live as you refine.

notes.heading: Optional detail for the export (does not affect the packet)
notes.hint: These lines are copied into the exported packet where they belong.

sections.to-collect: To collect before opening the case

results.heading: Your escalation packet
results.framing: Assembled by deterministic rules over what you described - a hand-off to review before you open the case, not a diagnosis. The checklist is the gap: what to attach that you do not have yet.
results.toCollectHeading: To collect before opening the case
results.toCollectIntro: The single biggest cause of a slow case is a first contact missing the diagnostic the vendor will ask for. Gather these first.
results.whyIntro: Every rule that fired, with its reason. The packet is the sum - inspectable, never an oracle.

ja4-fingerprint-decoder.name: JA4 / JA3 TLS fingerprint decoder
ja4-fingerprint-decoder.blurb: Decode a JA4 TLS client fingerprint or a JA3 fingerprint into its fields, compute the hashed JA4 from raw values, or compute a JA3 MD5. It auto-detects which you paste. All in your browser.
ja4-fingerprint-decoder.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
ja4-fingerprint-decoder.extCountDesc: Extensions offered including SNI and ALPN, ignoring GREASE.
ja4-fingerprint-decoder.cipherHashDesc: First 12 hex of the SHA-256 of the sorted cipher list. One-way; it cannot be reversed to the ciphers.
ja4-fingerprint-decoder.extHashDesc: First 12 hex of the SHA-256 of the sorted extensions plus signature algorithms. One-way.
ja4-fingerprint-decoder.anatomyNote: A JA4 has three parts joined by underscores: JA4_a is human-readable (transport, TLS version, SNI, cipher count, extension count, ALPN); JA4_b is a truncated SHA-256 of the sorted cipher list; JA4_c is a truncated SHA-256 of the sorted extensions plus the signature algorithms. GREASE values are ignored throughout.
ja4-fingerprint-decoder.ja3ExtCountDesc: Extensions, in the order the client sent them.

oui-lookup.name: OUI / MAC vendor lookup
oui-lookup.blurb: Look up the manufacturer behind a MAC address, and read whether it is unicast or multicast and universally or locally administered. All in your browser.
oui-lookup.runsLocally: Looked up in your browser against an embedded IEEE snapshot. Nothing is uploaded.
oui-lookup.ouiDesc: The first 24 bits identify the manufacturer.
oui-lookup.ouiOnlyDesc: You entered a bare OUI (the manufacturer prefix).
oui-lookup.multicastDesc: A group address (the I/G bit is set).
oui-lookup.universalDesc: Globally unique, assigned from an OUI.
oui-lookup.localDesc: Locally administered (the U/L bit is set); no vendor.

voss-fabric-id.name: VOSS fabric identifier decoder
voss-fabric-id.blurb: Decode an Extreme SPBM fabric identifier - a 24-bit I-SID, a 20-bit nickname (X.XX.XX), or a system-id / B-MAC. Auto-detected, all in your browser.
voss-fabric-id.runsLocally: Decoded in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.

voss-exos-translator.name: VOSS / EXOS command translator
voss-exos-translator.blurb: A side-by-side reference between VOSS (Fabric Connect / SPBM) and EXOS. Search a task or command to see how each CLI expresses it - and where EXOS has no equivalent.
voss-exos-translator.searchPlaceholder: e.g. i-sid, nickname, vlan, fabric attach
voss-exos-translator.hint: A reference, not a config generator. EXOS does not run SPBM; it joins a fabric as a Fabric Attach edge.
voss-exos-translator.noEquiv: No direct equivalent - EXOS does not run SPBM.
voss-exos-translator.noResults: No matching task. Try i-sid, vlan, nickname, or fabric attach.

f5xc-rate-limit-calculator.name: F5XC rate-limit calculator
f5xc-rate-limit-calculator.blurb: Turn an F5 Distributed Cloud rate-limiter configuration into its effective rate, burst ceiling, and exact leaky-bucket behavior - including why Disabled is not a bypass and how a Block lockout holds.
f5xc-rate-limit-calculator.runsLocally: Calculated in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
f5xc-rate-limit-calculator.behaviorLeakyBucket: Enforcement is a leaky bucket: when the bucket overflows, the load balancer returns HTTP 429.
f5xc-rate-limit-calculator.behaviorDisabled: Mitigation Action Disabled still returns 429 on overflow. It only means no extra lockout timer is applied - Disabled is not a bypass.
f5xc-rate-limit-calculator.behaviorBlock: Mitigation Action Block adds a lockout of {secs} seconds: after the bucket overflows the user is blocked for the full lockout even if the bucket drains, and can send again only once the timer expires.
f5xc-rate-limit-calculator.behaviorOverMax: This lockout exceeds F5's maximum blocking time of 48 hours (172800 seconds).
f5xc-rate-limit-calculator.overshootCaveat: Counting is distributed per Regional Edge / proxy, so you may briefly see more requests than the limit before enforcement converges (K000157944). Layered Server URL and API endpoint rules are evaluated first-match, in the order configured.

errors.min-number: Number must be an integer of at least 1.
errors.min-periods: Periods must be an integer of at least 1.
errors.per-period: Per Period must be Seconds, Minutes, or Hours.
errors.min-burst: Burst Multiplier must be at least 1 (default 1).
errors.block-lockout: Block mitigation needs a lockout duration of at least 1.

f5xc-tls-security-level-mapper.name: F5XC TLS security-level cipher mapper
f5xc-tls-security-level-mapper.blurb: Map an F5 Distributed Cloud TLS security level to its exact TLS versions and cipher suites, or paste a cipher to see which levels include it - from F5's verbatim TLS Reference.

summary.High: TLS 1.2 with PFS ciphers and strong algorithms. This is the Default level, used by an HTTPS load balancer with automatic certificate.
summary.Medium: TLS 1.0 with PFS ciphers and medium-strength algorithms. Includes every High cipher plus four ECDHE-CBC suites.
summary.Low: TLS 1.0 with non-PFS ciphers and weak algorithms. Includes every High and Medium cipher plus four static-RSA suites.

f5xc-tls-security-level-mapper.cumulativeNote: Includes every higher level's ciphers, plus {n} added at this level.
f5xc-tls-security-level-mapper.gotchaVersions: Because the minimum is TLS 1.0, a scanner will report TLS 1.0/1.1 enabled on this level. The Default/High level is minimum TLS 1.2 (K000148226).
f5xc-tls-security-level-mapper.gotchaWeak: The static-RSA suites here have no forward secrecy and are what scanners flag as weak ciphers (K000148079).
f5xc-tls-security-level-mapper.reversePlaceholder: e.g. ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA, or paste a scanner line
f5xc-tls-security-level-mapper.runsLocally: Mapped in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
f5xc-tls-security-level-mapper.noMatch: No F5XC predefined-level cipher recognized in that input. It may be a Custom-level suite or one XC does not offer.

f5xc-ce-egress-checklist.name: F5XC CE egress checklist & verifier
f5xc-ce-egress-checklist.blurb: Paste F5's published Customer Edge IP/domain reference file and get a purpose-organized, site-type-filtered allowlist, the port matrix, a firewall-request text, and a curl-host verifier - parsed from what you paste, never a hardcoded list.
f5xc-ce-egress-checklist.filePlaceholder: Paste the contents of F5's downloadable ips-domains.txt
f5xc-ce-egress-checklist.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
f5xc-ce-egress-checklist.emptyError: Paste F5's downloadable CE IP/domain reference file to begin.
f5xc-ce-egress-checklist.provenance: Parsed {lines} non-empty lines from the file you pasted: {domains} domains and {ips} IP entries. This is your file, not a stored list.
f5xc-ce-egress-checklist.portNote: IPsec (UDP 4500) is optional - SSL tunneling to the global network is supported. Port 65500 is local UI/API only, not an egress rule.
f5xc-ce-egress-checklist.registrationFlow: When a CE registers, it calls home with its token; the Global Controller selects two Regional Edges by GeoIP proximity of the CE's public IP and returns them with the CE's PKI certificates; the CE then negotiates tunnels to those REs, preferring IPsec and falling back to SSL.

f5xc-http-lb-route-explainer.name: F5XC HTTP LB route explainer
f5xc-http-lb-route-explainer.blurb: Paste the routes of an F5XC HTTP load balancer and get each route decoded in order - type, path/method/header match, action, header mutations, and per-route WAF - plus a first-match simulator for a test request.
f5xc-http-lb-route-explainer.inputLabel: HTTP load balancer spec or routes array (JSON)
f5xc-http-lb-route-explainer.placeholder: Paste the http_loadbalancer object (JSON view) or just its routes array
f5xc-http-lb-route-explainer.runsLocally: Decoded in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
f5xc-http-lb-route-explainer.noRoutes: No routes array found. Paste the http_loadbalancer spec or its routes array.
f5xc-http-lb-route-explainer.firstMatchNote: {n} routes, evaluated top to bottom - the first route that matches wins.
f5xc-http-lb-route-explainer.customRoute: Custom route object points to {ref}. Its match and action are defined in the referenced route, not inline here.
f5xc-http-lb-route-explainer.noPool: no origin pool (falls through to default)

f5xc-lb-algorithm-chooser.name: F5XC load-balancing algorithm chooser
f5xc-lb-algorithm-chooser.blurb: A short questionnaire recommends an F5XC origin-pool load-balancing algorithm - with the BIG-IP equivalent, caveats, and where to set it - and explains the XC model where consistent hashing IS the persistence method.
f5xc-lb-algorithm-chooser.title: Load-balancing algorithm chooser
f5xc-lb-algorithm-chooser.q1: Do sessions need to stick to the same origin server?
f5xc-lb-algorithm-chooser.q3dyn: Is the origin pool dynamic (autoscaling / Kubernetes pods)?
f5xc-lb-algorithm-chooser.prompt: Answer the questions above to see a recommended algorithm.

round-robin.label: Round Robin
round-robin.rationale: Sends requests to origins in turn, spreading load evenly. The simplest choice when origins have similar capacity and requests are short.
round-robin.config: Origin pool > Load Balancing Algorithm > Round Robin.

least-active-request.label: Least Active Request
least-active-request.rationale: Sends each request to the origin with the fewest in-flight requests. Better than Round Robin when request costs vary or connections are long-lived.
least-active-request.config: Origin pool > Load Balancing Algorithm > Least Active Request.

random.label: Random
random.rationale: Picks an origin at random. Statistically even across very large pools and avoids the synchronized stepping Round Robin can show.
random.bigip: (no direct equivalent; approximates Round Robin at scale)
random.config: Origin pool > Load Balancing Algorithm > Random.

source-ip-stickiness.label: Source IP Stickiness
source-ip-stickiness.rationale: Consistent-hashing on the client source IP, so a given client keeps landing on the same origin. In XC this hashing algorithm IS the persistence - there is no separate profile.
source-ip-stickiness.config: Origin pool > Load Balancing Algorithm > Source IP Stickiness.

cookie-stickiness.label: Cookie Based Stickiness
cookie-stickiness.rationale: Consistent-hashing on a cookie value, so a client carrying that cookie keeps landing on the same origin. The hashing algorithm is itself the persistence.
cookie-stickiness.config: Origin pool > Load Balancing Algorithm > Cookie Based Stickiness (or use Load Balancer Override to set the cookie name, TTL, and path per route).

ring-hash-custom.label: Ring Hash (custom hash policy)
ring-hash-custom.rationale: Consistent-hashing on a hash policy you define - typically an application header carrying a session ID. Ideal for microservices that pass their own session token.
ring-hash-custom.config: Origin pool > Load Balancing Algorithm > Ring Hash, then define the hash policy (header / cookie / source IP).

lb-override.label: Load Balancer Override
lb-override.rationale: Defers the choice to the HTTP load balancer so you can set stickiness per route or per domain, rather than one setting for the whole pool.
lb-override.config: Origin pool > Load Balancing Algorithm > Load Balancer Override, then set Advanced Options > Load Balancing Control per route (Cookie or Source IP).

caveat.no-persistence: This algorithm does not keep a client on the same origin. If the app needs sticky sessions, choose a consistent-hashing option instead.
caveat.least-uneven-cost: Best when requests differ in cost or run long; for uniform, short requests Round Robin is simpler.
caveat.random-large-pool: Evenness is statistical, so it shows best across large pools; small pools may look lumpy.
caveat.dynamic-no-persistence: Your pool is dynamic, but this algorithm does not persist - a scaling event will freely reshuffle clients, which is fine only if the app is stateless.
caveat.low-client-diversity: If many clients share a source IP (behind NAT or a proxy), hashing on source IP concentrates them on one origin and load becomes uneven.
caveat.cookie-params: You will set the cookie name, TTL, and path; the client must return that cookie for stickiness to hold.
caveat.custom-header-session: Pick a header whose value is stable per session (a session ID), not something that changes per request.
caveat.per-route-control: Set the stickiness under each route's Advanced Options > Load Balancing Control - different routes or domains can differ.
caveat.dynamic-hash-good: Good fit for a dynamic pool: consistent hashing remaps the fewest clients when origins are added or removed.

f5xc-lb-algorithm-chooser.teachingNote: On BIG-IP the load-balancing method and the persistence profile are two separate settings on the virtual server. XC folds them together: the consistent-hashing algorithms (Source IP Stickiness, Cookie Based Stickiness, Ring Hash) ARE the persistence method, and Round Robin, Least Active Request, and Random do not persist at all. There is no separate persistence profile object - to vary persistence per route, use Load Balancer Override.

f5xc-origin-pool-explainer.name: F5XC origin pool explainer
f5xc-origin-pool-explainer.blurb: Paste an F5XC origin_pool spec and get it decoded - origin-server types and addresses, the pool port, the load-balancing algorithm and endpoint selection, health checks, and the TLS-to-origin settings.
f5xc-origin-pool-explainer.placeholder: Paste the origin_pool object (Console JSON view) or its spec body
f5xc-origin-pool-explainer.runsLocally: Decoded in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
f5xc-origin-pool-explainer.unrecognized: No origin_servers found. Paste an origin_pool spec, its spec body, or a create/get envelope.

f5xc-domain-sni-match-resolver.name: F5XC domain / SNI match resolver
f5xc-domain-sni-match-resolver.blurb: Paste the domain lists of one or more F5XC HTTP load balancers and a test hostname, and see which load balancer and domain entry wins - exact beats wildcard, with the Default LB catching the rest.
f5xc-domain-sni-match-resolver.lbsPlaceholder: A JSON array of load balancers, each with a name and a domains list (and optional default). Use the Example button to see the shape.
f5xc-domain-sni-match-resolver.hostnamePlaceholder: app.example.com (from SNI or the Host header)
f5xc-domain-sni-match-resolver.runsLocally: Resolved in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
f5xc-domain-sni-match-resolver.parseError: That is not a valid JSON array of load balancers. Expected a list of objects with a name, a domains list, and an optional default flag.

resolution.exact: {host} is served by {lb} via an exact match on {domain}.
resolution.wildcard: {host} is served by {lb} via a wildcard match on {domain}.
resolution.default: No specific domain matches {host}; the Default load balancer {lb} catches it.
resolution.none: No load balancer domain matches {host}, and there is no Default load balancer - this hostname would not be picked up.

warn.auto-cert-conflict: Both *.{base} and the apex {base} are configured - F5 advises against mixing a wildcard and its apex across load balancers when using automatic certificates.
warn.dup-exact: The exact domain {domain} is claimed by more than one load balancer ({lbs}) - only one can win.
warn.multi-default: More than one Default load balancer is configured ({lbs}) - only one is allowed per advertise policy (IP + port).
warn.ambiguity: {host} matches {a} and {b} with equal specificity - this is ambiguous and should be avoided.
warn.wildcard-cert-multilabel: {host} routes via the wildcard {domain}, but a wildcard TLS certificate covers a single label - a multi-label host like this may route yet fail certificate validation.

f5xc-domain-sni-match-resolver.teachingNote: XC matches the hostname (from SNI on HTTPS, or the Host header on HTTP) to the most specific domain across all load balancers sharing an IP and port. An exact FQDN wins over a wildcard, and a wildcard like *.foo.com covers subdomains but not the apex foo.com - to serve the apex, add it as its own domain. One load balancer per advertise policy can be the Default, catching anything unmatched.

f5xc-security-event-explainer.name: F5XC security event explainer
f5xc-security-event-explainer.blurb: Paste an F5XC security event (WAF, Bot Defense, Service Policy, or API) and it decodes the type, the action and disposition, the request context, and the specific reason it fired.
f5xc-security-event-explainer.placeholder: Paste the security event from the Console (JSON view) or the API
f5xc-security-event-explainer.runsLocally: Decoded in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
f5xc-security-event-explainer.unrecognized: No security-event object found. Paste a single event, an events-page export, or a log-message envelope.

f5xc-object-linter.name: F5XC object linter
f5xc-object-linter.blurb: Paste an F5XC origin_pool, http_loadbalancer, or app_firewall object and it flags risky or surprising settings, each with a severity and a grounded explanation.
f5xc-object-linter.placeholder: Paste an origin_pool, http_loadbalancer, or app_firewall object (Console JSON view)
f5xc-object-linter.runsLocally: Linted in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
f5xc-object-linter.unrecognized: Not an origin_pool, http_loadbalancer, or app_firewall object. Paste one of those from the Console or the API.
f5xc-object-linter.servicePolicyNote: This is a service policy. The linter does not have rules for service policies yet - use the service policy explainer to decode it.

finding.op-skip-verify: TLS to the origin skips server verification - the connection is encrypted but the origin certificate is not validated, so a man-in-the-middle could impersonate the origin.
finding.op-no-sni: SNI is disabled on the connection to the origin - origins that host multiple names may return the wrong certificate or site.
finding.op-cleartext: TLS to the origin is disabled - traffic from XC to the origin is sent in cleartext.
finding.op-no-healthcheck: No health check is referenced - the pool relies on implicit health only, with no active probe.
finding.op-single-origin: The pool has a single origin server - there is no redundancy if it fails.
finding.lb-no-waf: No WAF (app_firewall) is attached to this load balancer - application-layer attacks are not inspected.
finding.lb-cleartext: This load balancer has a plain-HTTP listener with no HTTPS - client traffic is unencrypted.
finding.lb-no-redirect: HTTPS is configured but HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect is off - clients arriving over HTTP are not upgraded.
finding.lb-route-waf-off: Route {index} disables the WAF - requests matching it skip application-firewall inspection.
finding.lb-route-shadow: Route {index} is a catch-all but is not last - later routes after it can never match (first-match wins).
finding.lb-wildcard-apex: Both *.{base} and the apex {base} are configured - F5 advises against mixing a wildcard and its apex when using automatic certificates.
finding.waf-monitoring: The WAF is in monitoring mode - it detects and logs attacks but does not block them.
finding.waf-tc-off: Threat campaigns are disabled - known active-exploit campaigns are not detected.

f5xc-api-path-explainer.name: F5XC API path explainer
f5xc-api-path-explainer.blurb: Paste the OpenAPI / Swagger spec you import into (or download from) F5XC API Protection and it lists every path and operation with its method, parameters, request body, responses, and authentication.
f5xc-api-path-explainer.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
f5xc-api-path-explainer.unrecognized: Not a recognizable OpenAPI 2.0 or 3.0.x spec with paths. Paste a Swagger/OpenAPI JSON document.
f5xc-api-path-explainer.summaryLine: {paths} paths, {operations} operations. {unauthenticated} unauthenticated, {pathParams} object-level (path parameter), {deprecated} deprecated.

flag.no-security-schemes: No security schemes are defined in this spec - the API declares no authentication mechanisms.
flag.all-unauthenticated: Every operation is unauthenticated - no operation requires a security scheme.

bigip-ltm-lb-simulator.name: BIG-IP LTM load balancing simulator
bigip-ltm-lb-simulator.blurb: Configure pool members, pick a load balancing method and a request count, and see where the next N connections land.
bigip-ltm-lb-simulator.runsLocally: Simulated in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.

reason.fastest: Fastest routes to the member with the quickest response time. That depends on live latency measurements, so it cannot be simulated deterministically from configuration alone.
reason.observed: Observed assigns a ratio every second based on current connection counts. It depends on live, changing metrics, so it cannot be simulated deterministically from configuration alone.
reason.predictive: Predictive extends Observed with performance trends (whether a member is improving or declining). It depends on live trend data, so it cannot be simulated deterministically.
reason.dynamic-ratio: Dynamic Ratio sets ratios from real-time performance metrics, often gathered by SNMP. It depends on live metrics, so it cannot be simulated deterministically.

bigip-ltm-lb-simulator.resultTitle: Where the next N connections land
bigip-ltm-lb-simulator.summaryLine: {requests} requests across {active} active members ({standby} on standby).

note.standby: Members in a lower priority group are on standby and receive no traffic while the active group meets the priority group activation threshold.
note.lc-fresh: Least Connections is simulated from a fresh connection table (this tool takes no current-connection count), so it starts even, like Round Robin. On a live system it favors members with fewer active connections.
note.cookie-fallback: Least Sessions uses the persistence-record count as its metric. Note: with cookie-based persistence, BIG-IP falls back to Round Robin.

ja3-tls-fingerprint.name: JA3 / JA3N passive TLS fingerprint
ja3-tls-fingerprint.blurb: Paste a JA3 string and it recomputes the JA3 hash, computes the permutation-stable JA3N, decodes the fields, and flags GREASE values.
ja3-tls-fingerprint.runsLocally: Hashed in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
ja3-tls-fingerprint.permutedNote: The extensions are not in ascending order, so this JA3 will change if the client permutes its extension order (JA3 churn). Use JA3N, which sorts them, or JA4 for a stable fingerprint.
ja3-tls-fingerprint.stableNote: The extensions are already in ascending order, so JA3 and JA3N match here. Modern browsers that randomize extension order would still churn the JA3.
ja3-tls-fingerprint.greaseNote: GREASE values were found and excluded before hashing (RFC 8701): {values}.

packet-capture-plan-builder.intro: Describe the path and the symptom; a fixed {rules}-rule registry designs the capture plan: where to observe, in what order, and what each observation would establish. It plans collection - it never receives captures, and it never diagnoses.

form.heading: Describe the path and the symptom

archetype.outbound-sse: Outbound via SSE / secure web gateway

notes.hint: Free text here goes ONLY into the exported plan - it never influences the rules.

results.phase-2: Phase 2 - expansion if phase 1 is inconclusive
results.phaseEmpty: No scored points - establish the path and access first.
results.matrix: Interpretation matrix - decide in advance what it would mean
results.whyIntro: Every point, phase, and warning traces to a fired rule below - nothing else influences the plan.
results.privacy: Everything runs in your browser. The plan is advisory: capture authorization, retention, and interpretation remain yours.

packet-capture-plan-builder.name: Packet Capture Plan Builder
packet-capture-plan-builder.blurb: Command builders answer how to capture; this answers where, why, and what it would mean. Describe the path and the symptom; a 35-rule original registry designs a phased capture plan: ranked points on named boundaries, expected observations, an interpretation matrix with supports and weakens decided before collection, and sync/authorization discipline. It plans captures - it never ingests them.
