packet-capture-plan-builder.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser - nothing you select or type leaves this page.

flow-path-reasoner.intro: Describe the path as you believe it runs; a fixed {rules}-rule registry builds the model: the hop chain, side-flows, TLS segments, transformation points - and the failure-domain candidates the ambiguities imply. The map is a proposed model, never discovered topology, and unknowns stay visibly unknown.

form.heading: Describe the path as you believe it runs

archetype.load-balancer: Client to load balancer to pool

notes.hint: Free text here goes ONLY into the exported map - it never influences the rules.
notes.title: Map title

results.mapDesc: The forward hop chain as modeled from the selections, with transformation and TLS markers on each node.
results.mapModel: Canonical assumed order — a proposed model to verify with the hop owners, not discovered topology.
results.whyIntro: Every domain, warning, and checklist line traces to a fired rule below — nothing else influences the model.
results.privacy: Everything runs in your browser. The map describes internal topology — keep it local and share deliberately.

flow-path-reasoner.name: Flow Path Reasoner
flow-path-reasoner.blurb: The senior skill is refusing to troubleshoot until the path is understood. Seven selections build a canonical hop map with side-flows, TLS segments, rewrite points, and ranked failure-domain candidates - and unknowns stay visibly unknown.
flow-path-reasoner.emptyState: Describe the path above and the modeled map appears here - hops, side-flows, TLS segments, and the failure domains the ambiguities imply.
flow-path-reasoner.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser. The map is a proposed model from your selections, never discovered topology - and free-text labels flow only into the export.

health-snapshot-comparator.name: Before/After Health Snapshot Comparator
health-snapshot-comparator.blurb: You declare the states, the tool gates the conclusion: seven selections produce a tiered comparison report - baseline quality, a churn-classed dimension catalog, delta-interpretation guidance, and the continue / observe / investigate / hold-rollback-ready gate. Never labels a change successful on green components alone.
health-snapshot-comparator.emptyState: Declare the comparison above - context, target, baseline confidence, after-state, window, churn - and the gated report appears here.
health-snapshot-comparator.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser. This tool never ingests state data and never diffs anything: you declare the states, the tool gates the conclusion - and free text flows only into the export.
health-snapshot-comparator.intro: Declare the comparison you are making - never the data itself. A fixed {rules}-rule registry gates what conclusion those declarations can honestly carry: you declare the states, the tool gates the conclusion. Green components alone never earn a pass.

notes.hint: Free text here goes ONLY into the exported report - it never influences the rules.
notes.title: Report title

verdict.investigate: INVESTIGATE - close the gaps before any conclusion
verdict.hold-rollback-ready: HOLD ROLLBACK READY - keep it armed while the gaps close

results.whyIntro: Every reason, warning, and checklist line traces to a fired rule below — nothing else influences the gate.
results.privacy: Everything runs in your browser. The report describes your change and environment — keep it local and share deliberately.

roman-numerals.name: Roman numerals converter
roman-numerals.blurb: Numbers to numerals and back, with the canonical subtractive form, the place-by-place construction, and honest validation: IIII is accepted and explained, IL is refused with the rule it breaks.

roman-numerals.nonCanonical: {input} is a valid additive spelling; the canonical form is {canonical}.

greek-alphabet.alphabetIntro: The full alphabet, with the modern transliteration (classical in parentheses where it differs) and the place you have most likely met each letter.

time-calculator.name: Time calculator
time-calculator.blurb: Exact time arithmetic: the span between two instants, or an instant plus or minus a duration, in ISO 8601 or plain shorthand. Months and years are refused honestly: they are calendar units with no single exact length.

timezone-meeting-planner.blurb: One instant, read in every attendee's IANA time zone: local time, UTC offset, day shift, and a working-hours flag. DST is whatever the zone database says for that date, which is exactly why you plan with a real one.
timezone-meeting-planner.instantLabel: Meeting instant (with Z or an offset)

p0f-signature-explainer.runsLocally: Runs locally. The signature you paste never leaves your browser.

user-agent-entropy-analyzer.blurb: Paste a User-Agent string and see the identifying tokens it exposes, the distinguishing bits each contributes, and the Client Hints migration.

user-agent-entropy-analyzer.runsLocally: Runs locally. The string you paste never leaves your browser; nothing is read from your own browser.
user-agent-entropy-analyzer.reducedTag: This is the reduced / frozen UA form - the minor version is fixed and platform detail is capped.
user-agent-entropy-analyzer.bitsDisclaimer: Bit estimates are illustrative, from published fingerprinting research - not a live measurement of any population.

http-header-order-fingerprint.blurb: Paste a request header block and see how header order and casing fingerprint the client - the passive-HTTP analog of JA3.
http-header-order-fingerprint.runsLocally: Runs locally. The headers you paste never leave your browser.
http-header-order-fingerprint.missingIntro: A browser would normally send these:

bigip-dns-gslb-simulator.name: BIG-IP DNS (GTM) GSLB simulator
bigip-dns-gslb-simulator.blurb: Model two-tier global server load balancing: a wide IP picks a pool, then the pool picks a virtual server. See how N DNS requests resolve across pools and members.
bigip-dns-gslb-simulator.runsLocally: Runs locally. Your topology and requests never leave your browser.
bigip-dns-gslb-simulator.notSimulableGeneric: This method depends on live big3d performance metrics collected from each data center, so its outcome cannot be reproduced offline. The tool states this instead of inventing a distribution.
bigip-dns-gslb-simulator.memberTierTitle: Member tier — which virtual server resolves
bigip-dns-gslb-simulator.memberDynamicNote: One or more pools use a dynamic member method; those members show 0 because their selection needs live metrics.

reason.qos: Quality of Service scores each virtual server from live RTT, hops, and hit-ratio metrics.
reason.completion-rate: Completion Rate compares the percentage of packets successfully exchanged, a live metric.
reason.lowest-round-trip-time: Lowest Round-Trip Time picks the resource with the least measured RTT from the local DNS.
reason.fewest-hops: Fewest Hops picks the resource with the least router hops from the local DNS, measured by big3d.
reason.packet-rate: Packet Rate routes by live packets-per-second.
reason.virtual-server-score: Virtual Server Score uses a live per-VS performance score.
reason.least-connections: Least Connections routes by current live connection counts.
reason.cpu: CPU routes by live processor utilization.

note.pool:global-availability: Wide-IP Global Availability sends all requests to the first available pool; the rest stand by.
note.pool:topology-match: Wide-IP Topology matched the client region to a pool (score 10).
note.pool:topology-nomatch: No pool region matched the client region, so the wide-IP fell back to round-robin across available pools.
note.global-availability: Global Availability sends all requests to the first available resource.
note.topology-nomatch: No region matched; fell back to round-robin.

mtu-mss.name: MTU / MSS calculator
mtu-mss.blurb: Enter a link MTU and your encapsulation stack (vxlan, gre, pppoe, vlan, mpls, +N custom) to get the inner MTU and TCP MSS, the Ethernet frame sizes, the underlay MTU an overlay needs, and the wire efficiency of standard versus jumbo frames.
mtu-mss.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser: fixed RFC header arithmetic, no network, no clock.

errors.format: Start with an MTU number, then optional tokens: v6, pppoe, gre, ipip, 6in4, vxlan, geneve, wireguard, vlan, qinq, mpls or mplsN, and +N for custom overhead.
errors.custom-range: Custom overhead +N must be between 1 and 2000 bytes.
errors.mpls-range: MPLS label count must be between 1 and 8 (mpls1 to mpls8).
errors.duplicate: A token was given twice; each encapsulation or shim appears once.
errors.too-small: That stack eats the whole MTU: the inner packet would be smaller than the 68-byte IPv4 minimum.

inner.heading: Inside the MTU
inner.encapNote: Encapsulation consumes {total} bytes inside the link MTU (outer header: {outer}). This is the number to clamp MSS against.
inner.sixInFourNote: 6in4 carries IPv6 inside IPv4, so the inner packet is IPv6: read the IPv6 MSS.
inner.variableNote: GENEVE's 50 bytes is the optionless base; variable-length options add more. Check your platform's actual header usage.

stack.heading: The stack, layer by layer
stack.insideMtu: — inside the MTU (shrinks the inner packet)
stack.lesson: Encapsulations spend bytes inside the MTU, so the inner MTU and MSS shrink. L2 shims (VLAN, QinQ, MPLS) ride in the frame header, so the IP MTU does not move; the frame grows instead, which is why switches need frame headroom, not a smaller MTU.

frame.heading: At the link
frame.maxFrame: Largest Ethernet frame (header + shims + MTU + FCS)
frame.onWire: On the wire per frame (adds preamble + inter-frame gap)
frame.shimNote: With {shims} bytes of shims, an untagged-size payload already makes a baby giant: the switch must accept frames beyond 1518.

underlay.heading: To carry a full 1500-byte inner packet
underlay.note: The classic overlay design answer: raise the underlay MTU to this value (or clamp MSS down) so full-sized inner packets cross without fragmentation or path-MTU black holes.

efficiency.heading: Wire efficiency, standard vs jumbo
efficiency.note: Share of on-wire bytes that are TCP payload for full-sized IPv4 segments: (MTU − 40) ÷ (MTU + 38), counting Ethernet header, FCS, preamble and inter-frame gap. The classic pair: 94.93% at 1500, 99.14% at 9000.

http-methods-comparison.name: HTTP methods comparison
http-methods-comparison.blurb: Enter one to four HTTP methods (try "get vs query") to see each one's registry facts: safe, idempotent, cacheable, request-body semantics, CORS safelist membership, HTML-form support, and the defining RFC - plus exactly which properties differ. Includes QUERY, the RFC 10008 method registered in June 2026.
http-methods-comparison.inputPlaceholder: query · get vs query · post,query · get post put delete
http-methods-comparison.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser: a fixed registry-fact table, no network, no clock.

errors.format: Enter one to four HTTP method names, separated by spaces, commas, or "vs" - for example "get vs query".
errors.unknown-method: "{token}" is not in the table. Known methods: GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, DELETE, CONNECT, OPTIONS, TRACE, PATCH, QUERY, PROPFIND, REPORT, SEARCH.
errors.too-many: Compare at most four methods at a time.

GET.story: The read: safe, idempotent, cacheable - and the query must live in the URL, with every length limit, log file, and Referer header that implies.

HEAD.story: GET's metadata twin: identical semantics, headers only in the response. What caches and monitors use to check without transferring.

POST.story: The everything verb: create, submit, trigger - or secretly just search. The protocol cannot tell, so intermediaries must assume the worst: unsafe, non-idempotent, effectively uncacheable.

PUT.story: Full replacement at a known URI. Not safe, but idempotent by design: putting the same representation twice leaves the same state, which is why retries are legal.

DELETE.story: Idempotent but not safe: deleting twice leaves the world in the same state as deleting once, even though the second attempt may answer 404.

CONNECT.story: Not a resource operation at all: it asks a proxy to open a tunnel (the way HTTPS traverses forward proxies). No body, no caching, its own rules.

OPTIONS.story: The capability probe - and the vehicle of every CORS preflight. Asks what the target supports without touching it.

TRACE.story: The diagnostic echo: the server reflects the request back. Safe and idempotent, forbidden a body, and disabled almost everywhere for header-leak reasons.

PATCH.story: Partial modification (RFC 5789, 2010). Deliberately registered as neither safe nor idempotent: applying the same delta twice may not land in the same state.

QUERY.story: The June 2026 arrival (RFC 10008): a read that carries a body. Safe and idempotent like GET, body like POST, cacheable with the cache key incorporating the request content - and the first new method registration since PATCH, sixteen years earlier. Not CORS-safelisted (preflight required); HTML forms fall back to GET today.

PROPFIND.story: WebDAV's property reader (RFC 4918): safe, idempotent, XML body. One of the three registry precedents RFC 10008's appendix credits before giving QUERY a clean, generic start.

REPORT.story: WebDAV versioning's extensible query (RFC 3253): safe and idempotent with a body, but tied to its XML report vocabulary - part of why a generic QUERY was still needed.

SEARCH.story: The WebDAV search method (RFC 5323) - and QUERY's former name. Early drafts of the new method were called SEARCH until the 2021 rename gave it a fresh identity, free of the WebDAV baggage.

diff.heading: Where they differ
diff.none: These methods share every property in the table.
diff.note: Safe and idempotent are IANA-registered promises about intent, mechanically usable by caches, retries, and proxies - never guarantees about payload content. A QUERY body deserves the same inspection as a POST body.

privacy_page.title: Your data, and what this site does with it
privacy_page.lede: A plain-language account of exactly what this website does and does not do with your information. It is written to be accurate rather than merely reassuring, because under regulations like the GDPR and Brazil's LGPD it is the accurate disclosure that matters.
privacy_page.short1: This site sets no cookies, runs no analytics, uses no advertising, and contains no third-party trackers or fingerprinting. Nothing follows you across the web.
privacy_page.short2: It does not sell, share, or build profiles of its visitors, and it asks you for nothing in order to read it.
privacy_page.short3: The interactive tools run entirely in your browser. What you paste or type into them stays on your device and is not transmitted anywhere.
privacy_page.short4: Only three things involve data at all, and each is explained below: a single theme setting saved in your own browser, the standard connection logs that every website visit creates at the hosting layer, and any message you choose to send by email.
privacy_page.controllerBody: This is the personal website of Rodolfo Nützmann (ronutz.com). For any privacy question, or to exercise any of the rights described below, write to {email}. Enquiries are handled directly by Rodolfo Nützmann, as the data controller.
privacy_page.noTrackTitle: No cookies, no tracking, no analytics
privacy_page.noTrackBody: This site sets no cookies of any kind. It uses no analytics service (such as Google Analytics or Plausible), no advertising network, no social-media tracking pixels, and no device fingerprinting. Your visit is not measured, profiled, or shared with anyone for those purposes.
privacy_page.browserTitle: What is stored in your browser
privacy_page.browserBody: One small thing: your chosen visual theme, saved in your browser's local storage so the site can remember it the next time you visit. It is a convenience setting that stays on your own device, is never sent to this site or to anyone else, and can be cleared at any time through your browser. No other data about you is stored on your device.
privacy_page.hostingBody: The site is delivered through Cloudflare, which provides its hosting and content delivery. As with any website, loading a page means your device connects to a server, and Cloudflare, acting as the hosting provider, processes basic connection data, including your IP address and standard request details, in order to deliver the pages to you and to protect the site against abuse and attack. This processing is necessary to operate and secure the site (a legitimate interest under GDPR Article 6(1)(f), and the corresponding bases under LGPD Articles 7 and 10). It is retained only briefly by the provider and is not used here to identify or track you.
privacy_page.toolsTitle: The tools run in your browser
privacy_page.toolsBody: The decoders and calculators on this site, for JWTs, IP addresses, certificates and the rest, compute entirely within your browser. The values you enter are processed on your own device and are never sent to a server. A separate programmatic interface (an API) is offered for automation and integrations; by design it is stateless and logs none of the queries or request bodies sent to it.
privacy_page.contactBody: The contact form does not submit to any server. Instead it opens a pre-filled message in your own email application, which you then send yourself from your own account, so nothing passes through a third party. If you do write, whether through the form or directly, I receive the information you include, such as your name, email address and message, and use it only to reply to you. I keep it only for as long as needed to deal with your enquiry, do not use it for marketing, and do not share it.
privacy_page.intlBody: Because Cloudflare operates a global network, the connection-level processing described above may take place on servers located outside your own country, including outside Brazil and the European Economic Area. Where personal data is handled internationally in this way, it is processed under the safeguards offered by the provider concerned.
privacy_page.childrenBody: This site is intended for a professional, general audience. It is not directed at children and does not knowingly collect personal data from them.
privacy_page.rightsBody: Depending on where you live, you have rights over your personal data, including to access it, to have it corrected or deleted, to object to or restrict its processing, to request a copy in a portable form, and to be told how it is handled. To exercise any of these, simply write to {email}. Because so little personal data is ever processed here, in practice there is usually very little to act on, but the channel is always open.
privacy_page.rightsGdpr: If you are in the European Economic Area or the United Kingdom, you also have the right to lodge a complaint with your local data protection supervisory authority.
privacy_page.rightsLgpd: If you are in Brazil, you hold the rights set out in Article 18 of the LGPD, and may also contact the national data protection authority (the ANPD).
privacy_page.changesBody: If this notice changes, the revised version will be published on this page and the date below will be updated. Material changes will not be applied retroactively.
privacy_page.prefsTitle: Theme, language, and what this site remembers
privacy_page.prefsBody: Your theme choice is stored only in this browser's localStorage, on your device, and applied before first paint on your next visit; there is no cookie, no account, and no server that ever sees it, which also means it cannot follow you: a private window, cleared site data, or a different browser or device starts you back at the default theme by design. Your language is not stored anywhere at all, because it lives in the address itself: /en/ and /pt-BR/ are different pages, so bookmarking or sharing a URL in your language keeps it, while visiting the bare domain serves the default. That is the whole trade: with no accounts and no tracking, preferences can only live where you can see and delete them.
privacy_page.shortcutsBody: The site has a handful of single-key shortcuts for quick navigation, active on every page. Press b for the boss key, which hides whatever you are reading behind a convincing 1980s work application until you press any key or click; t jumps to the Tools index; l to the Learn index; m opens the Mega Brain console; and z opens Buzzword Bingo. These are deliberately inert whenever you are typing: if the focus is in a text field, a paste box, a search input, or any editable area, the keystroke belongs to that field and the shortcut does nothing, so nothing you type is ever swallowed. They are also ignored when a modifier key is held, so your browser's own combinations such as Ctrl+T for a new tab are never shadowed. The shortcuts run entirely in your browser, add no tracking, and are purely a convenience you can ignore.

footer.redEducation: Book official vendor training with Red Education, the multi-award-winning global training powerhouse
footer.builtWith: Built by Rodolfo Nützmann with CONCORD

search.filterEmpty: No results of the selected types.
