about.lede: Network and security training delivered by a practitioner who has spent his career on the implementation side, not just the slides. Based in São Paulo, teaching globally, in Portuguese and English.

now.title: What I do now
now.body: I deliver official, certified instructor-led training across security and networking platforms. There are hands-on labs on real systems, and for people who already know the fundamentals and came to practice, that is exactly the point: real environments to work in. For groups newer to a technology, the time goes first into building the foundations the topic rests on, so the hands-on work makes sense and people leave understanding why things behave the way they do, not just which buttons to press. Both are equally valuable, and the balance shifts with who is in the room. Teaching and technical enablement have been part of my work since the late 1990s, on a career that began on the implementation side in 1996.
now.teachesValue: Virtual and on-site instructor-led training, globally

platforms.title: The platforms, taught in depth
platforms.body: Being an authorized instructor for every vendor here means I can teach each one properly and compare them honestly. These are the platforms I am authorized to teach.
platforms.f5: BIG-IP across the full instructor-led curriculum: LTM, DNS, Advanced WAF (ASM), APM, AFM, SSL Orchestrator, automation, and troubleshooting.
platforms.fortinet: Fortinet certified training, including the FCP track.
platforms.extreme: Extreme Networks switching, SD-WAN, and API and automation tracks, across installation, configuration, management, and troubleshooting.
platforms.ping: Ping Identity access management and federation, instructor authorization cleared in 2026.
platforms.zscaler: Zscaler Zero Trust Exchange, instructor authorization cleared in 2026.

path.title: The path here
path.intro: My career began on the vendor and implementation side in 1996, and stayed there for two decades before I focused fully on training. That implementation background is the reason the training lands: I have built, broken, and fixed these systems in production.
path.cabletron: Network engineer and certified instructor. Non-Ethernet and Ethernet networks, campus networks, IP routing.
path.riverstone: Product support engineer and knowledge-base coordinator in California. Gigabit Ethernet, metropolitan area networks, IP routing, BGP. Third-level escalation, customer scenario recreation, and regression testing.
path.cisco: Network consulting engineer, acting as the single point of contact for key customers including SERPRO and Correios. Escalation and customer-satisfaction management.
path.enterasys: Network engineer and instructor. Enterprise networks, IP routing, user access control and NAC, IDS and IPS.
path.juniper: Channel support engineer and instructor for enterprise switches and firewalls. Technical sales enablement and Junos-SRX operations training.
path.f5channel: Channel-facing sales and pre-sales engineering for F5, providing technical enablement, proof-of-concept execution, and partner training across distribution.
path.rededucation: Authorized training instructor delivering official certified courses across F5, Fortinet, Extreme Networks, and Netskope to organizations worldwide.

origins.title: Where it started
origins.body: Before the formal career, there was the curiosity. Through the early 1990s I was importing and assembling computers, running and using bulletin board systems, and working across DOS, Unix, Linux, Netware, early Ethernet, X.25, and dial-up internet. By 1995 that hands-on work had become a first formal role building an electronic-data-interchange system. The thread from then to now is the same: take something genuinely complex and make it work, then make it make sense to someone else.

approach.title: How I teach
approach.body: I chose to specialize in training because it combines deep technical knowledge with the part of the work I enjoy most: explaining complex concepts simply. The strongest sessions connect each concept to the job the learner actually has to do, so the content has somewhere to land. Real-world examples, real labs, and a focus on understanding over memorization.

cta.title: Start with the concepts
cta.body: The Learn section explains the ideas behind the tools, and the tools let you work with them directly, all in your browser.

recognition.title: Recognition
recognition.body: Recognized as an F5 DevCentral MVP for three consecutive years, in 2022, 2023, and 2024, for contributions to the F5 technical community.

credibility.certsDesc: The full list of current F5, Fortinet, Extreme Networks, and Netskope credentials, each with its issuing body and date.
credibility.endorsementsDesc: Recommendations from colleagues, partners, and students across two decades of training, quoted verbatim.

endorsements.title: What people say, in their own words.
endorsements.intro: {count} verbatim recommendations and reviews, spanning two decades of teaching and consulting. Nothing here is paraphrased or edited.
endorsements.provenance: Sourced from LinkedIn recommendations, Google reviews, and verified Red Education student reviews. Original wording and spelling are preserved exactly as written.

testimonials.machineDisclaimer: Machine-generated translation. It may not accurately reflect the original wording.

history.indexLede: Three eras, one through-line: take something genuinely complex, make it work, then make it make sense to someone else.

pre1996.title: The curiosity
pre1996.subtitle: Ham radio, a cloned Sinclair, bulletin boards, a stolen dial tone, and the first academic internet — the whole apprenticeship, before any of it was a job.
pre1996.intro: Every technical career has a before. Before the vendor badges and the certifications, there was a teenager who needed to know how everything worked — and who chased that need through radio waves, cloned micros, phone lines, and the earliest internet. This is where the instinct that drives all the rest was formed. The techniques on this page belong to a world that no longer exists: the analog phone network, the token payphone, and the single-line bulletin board are gone, which is exactly why the story can be told plainly.
pre1996.mapNote: Roughly a decade, from the first machine to the first payslip.

tl.what: Rodolfo is born to a Brazilian father — a civil engineer, grandson of Portuguese, Italian, and Arab immigrants — and a German-born mother, a Bachelor of Fine Arts who had moved to Brazil with her parents and sister at the age of twelve. His maternal grandmother, especially, taught him to look at the universe and at nature with curiosity and awe; had computers not claimed him, he would probably have gone the scientist's way.
A Brazilian clone of Sinclair's ZX81, first loaded from cassette by his father onto the family TV — and soon programmed in BASIC by Rodolfo himself.

tl.where: Amateur radio · his father's call sign & QSL cards
tl.what: His father's world: an engineer and avid ham operator working the local and long-distance bands, with QSL postcards arriving from strangers a continent away to prove a contact made on the airwaves.
Running a bulletin board on a single phone line; importing components from the United States and assembling custom PCs to sell.
Through a university, over a modem, into a text prompt on a machine with no center and no owner. The walled dial-up world opened onto the planet.
The token payphone and the water-in-the-slot trick; blue-boxing operator trunks all the way to Europe's "scene" boards; and war-dialed corporate PBXs left deliberately untouched.

tl.where: The World Wide Web · SLIP/PPP & NCSA Mosaic
tl.what: A program compiled on the shell account turned the dial-up session itself into a SLIP/PPP tunnel — and through it, NCSA Mosaic: the first glimpse of the World Wide Web.
An electronic-data-interchange system on Novell NetWare and PCBoard. The point where hobby, trade, and profession became one thing.

mv.title: Curiosity and awe
body: Before any machine, there was a teacher. His grandmother — his mother's mother, who had crossed an ocean from Germany to Brazil with her family and started the world over — talked with the boy, from his earliest age, about everything her world contained. The garden of her house, with its small insects and earthworms, its flowers and weeds, its tomatoes, carrots, and strawberries. The night sky full of stars, the vastness of the Universe, and the human consciousness there to behold it. She taught him that all of it answers curiosity with more curiosity, and that awe is not a childish thing to outgrow but a way of paying attention.

It took. Had computers not claimed him a few years later, he would almost certainly have gone the scientist's way — the pull toward how things work was already there, installed early and installed deep. The machines simply gave that curiosity a keyboard.

mv.title: Signals in the air
body: Before the computers, there was radio — and the radio was his father's. An engineer drawn to every new piece of technology and, just as much, to the people it connected, Rodolfo's father was an avid amateur operator who worked the bands both local and long-distance. Amateur radio — "ham" radio — meant a licensed call sign, the clipped discipline of the phonetic alphabet, and the small ceremony of the QSL card: an actual postcard that would arrive in the mail from a stranger on the far side of the world, sent to confirm that yes, on this frequency, at this hour, two people had met on the airwaves.

The lesson landed early and never left. Someone with the right gear could reach a stranger a continent away, and a real, physical card would come back to prove it — and there was already a whole worldwide fellowship of people who cared, deeply, about how a signal travels from here to there. Curiosity about the medium itself, and a global community reachable from a room in São Paulo — every later chapter is a variation on that first one, and on the man who showed it to him first.

mv.title: The national micros
body: The first machine came home the same way the radio had: through his father. A nine-year-old Rodolfo would watch, rapt, as his father fed a program into the computer from an ordinary audio cassette and then made things happen on the screen of the television set — which, in that era, was the monitor every home micro borrowed from the living room. It looked like conjuring, and it was not long before the watching turned into doing.

The machine itself was a product of its moment. In 1984 Brazil was under the reserva de mercado, a market reserve that banned imported computers to shelter a domestic industry, so the machines were local clones — the first one here a Microdigital TK-82C, a Brazilian clone of Sinclair's ZX81, later joined by the TK90X, a clone of the ZX Spectrum 48K.

There was no hard drive and no network. Soon Rodolfo was writing his own programs in BASIC, typed by hand from the back pages of magazines and saved to that same screeching cassette; a membrane keyboard under his fingers, the television still standing in for a monitor. He learned BASIC because there was nothing else to do with the thing, and in the process learned the truth the rest of this site is built on: a computer is not magic. It is a machine that does exactly, and only, what you tell it.

mv.title: The bulletin-board years
body: By 1991 the phone line had become the frontier. A bulletin board system — a BBS — was a computer in someone's bedroom with a modem answering a telephone. To visit one you told your own modem to dial its number, then listened to the two machines negotiate a connection in a burst of static that every modem owner could hum from memory.

One caller at a time. If someone else was already on, you got a busy signal and tried again later. Inside were message boards, libraries of files, screens drawn in colored ANSI text, and door games you played turn by turn against everyone else in town. Running one — the software here was Oblivion/2 — meant being host, moderator, and mechanic all at once.

And the boards were not islands. FidoNet stitched thousands of them together: in the small hours each board would dial its neighbors and hand off a sack of messages that then hopped, board to board, across the country and the world by morning. It was electronic mail before electronic mail — and it had exactly the shape the QSL cards had taught: a message finding a stranger, confirmed a little later.

mv.title: The young hacker's way
body: If a BBS taught how the machines spoke to each other, phreaking asked a bolder question: how does the telephone system itself work — and can it be made to do things it was never meant to?

In Brazil the payphone was the orelhão, the "big ear," and it ran on fichas, little tokens you bought and fed into a slot. Schoolyard folklore held that a splash of water in the coin slot would short something inside and hand you a free dial tone; it was tried, more than once, and sometimes it worked. But the real doorway was the one the old legends described: a single tone — the famous 2600 Hz — that the long-distance network listened for as its own internal signal, and the blue box built to speak the network's language back to it.

That doorway got used, and often. A blue box could coax the network into handing over a long-distance trunk as if the call were its own, and the destination that mattered was Europe — the "scene" boards, the tight international circuit of crackers, artists, and traders whose bulletin boards were unreachable on any budget a teenager in São Paulo had. War-dialing mapped the quieter half of the network: a modem set loose to sweep whole ranges of numbers overnight and log what answered — a carrier, a fax, or the distinctive tone of a corporate PBX. Some of those PBXs could be talked into handing out an outside line.

Those were left alone, and the reason was the whole ethic in miniature. Seizing a trunk from the phone company's own signaling put the cost nowhere — no account, no invoice, no one billed. Making a real company's PBX place the call would have landed on that company's bill, and that was the line that stayed uncrossed. The one scheme that was only ever dreamed — a modem spliced straight into a payphone's wiring — stayed a dream. None of the rest was for money; it was the plain intoxication of understanding a system well enough to see its seams, and being young enough to lean on them. The analog network all of this lived on is decades gone now, its signaling tones long retired, which is the honest reason it can be set down here as history rather than instruction.

That one dream kept its own schedule. In the teenager's version it had a shape: a bulky 386DX40, a 14-inch CRT monitor, a modem, and a phone line — which is to say, access to the knowledge of the whole world, as long as the world stayed within reach of the desk. Seventeen years later, holding one of the first iPhones shortly after its launch — jailbroken so it could run the apps a community was building for itself — he felt it at once: the recurring dream of the fourteen-year-old of 1990, made real — and made portable, the whole of it now possible anywhere, in the palm of the hand.

mv.title: The academic internet
body: The BBS world was a town. The internet was the planet. In 1991 it arrived through a university, over a modem, as a plain text prompt on a UNIX machine that was itself in conversation with thousands of others — no center, no owner, no closing time.

The account was a shell account — no SLIP or PPP carrying real internet traffic down the phone line, just a prompt and a menu of tools with strange names: Gopher, Archie, IRC. It was enough. The first time a command typed in São Paulo reached across and pulled a file off a machine on another continent, the walls of the dial-up garden simply fell away.

The door had a name, too: aandrade, an account on the UNIX servers of USP — the Universidade de São Paulo — whose access, as was the custom among enthusiasts then, was traded hand to hand like a key quietly copied for friends. Only years later did he learn how many of the people he had met in that era had stepped through the very same account. So, three decades late and with a grin: thank you, aandrade, for unknowingly holding open, for a whole generation of young explorers, one of the first doors to the world.

Then, in 1993, the shell account became a doorway of its own. On it he compiled a program that, when run, turned the dial-up session itself into a SLIP/PPP tunnel — the terminal line quietly becoming a real internet connection — and through that tunnel he opened NCSA Mosaic for his first glimpse of the World Wide Web. The shape of the next thirty years came into view all at once.

mv.title: From hobby to trade
body: Somewhere in those same years the curiosity started to pay. From 1991 the work was hands-on and autonomous: importing components from the United States, assembling custom PCs and selling them, then installing and troubleshooting them for whoever needed help — DOS and CP/M, Turbo Pascal, and the Clipper and dBase databases that quietly ran small businesses then.

In 1995 it converged into a first formal role at INTELECTA, a company incubated by SEBRAE-SP. The project was an electronic-data-interchange system for trading medical and hospital supplies, built on Novell NetWare servers and the PCBoard bulletin-board system. A real system solving a real commercial problem — the exact point where the hobby, the trade, and the profession finally became a single thing.

pre1996.fieldTitle: A field guide to a vanished world
pre1996.fieldIntro: The vocabulary of the era, for anyone who arrived after it. Two of these open into the glossary, where the phone-phreaking lineage is documented in full.

bbs.desc: A computer with a modem answering a phone line, one visitor at a time. The community internet before the internet: message boards, file libraries, and games, all reached by dialing a phone number.

sysop.desc: System operator — the person who ran a BBS from their own home: host, moderator, librarian, and repair technician for a little world with a phone number instead of an address.

baud.desc: The modem's speed, from 300 baud upward. The screech you heard when connecting was two modems negotiating in real time — a ritual so familiar that owners could tell a good connection from a bad one by ear.

ansi.desc: Pictures and menus drawn entirely in colored text characters, using ANSI escape codes. The visual language of the BBS: logos, borders, and full-screen art, all made of letters and symbols.

door.desc: A game that ran on the BBS itself, played turn by turn against the other callers — you took your moves, hung up, and someone else took theirs. The whole town competing on the same board, a day at a time.

fidonet.desc: A store-and-forward network linking BBSs worldwide. Each board dialed its neighbors overnight to relay messages that hopped board to board across the globe — hobbyist email that moved while everyone slept.

qsl.desc: A postcard mailed between amateur-radio operators to confirm a contact actually happened. Proof, by post, that two strangers had spoken on a given frequency — the same message-finds-a-stranger shape the BBS world would later repeat.

orelhao.desc: Brazil's public payphone — the "big ear" — and the tokens (fichas) you fed it to make a call. The everyday hardware the phreaking folklore of the schoolyard tried, endlessly, to outwit.

phreaking.desc: Exploring and bending the telephone network out of pure curiosity about how it worked. The phone-system ancestor of computer hacking, born a decade before most people had a computer to hack.

bluebox.desc: A device that generated the control tones the old long-distance network trusted — most famously the 2600 Hz tone — to seize a line and route calls for free. In-band signaling meant the network believed any caller who could make the right sound.

shell.desc: Early internet access over a phone line: a text login on a remote UNIX machine, sometimes upgraded with SLIP or PPP to carry real internet traffic. The doorway from the walled BBS world onto the open network.

slipppp.desc: Serial Line Internet Protocol and Point-to-Point Protocol — the two encapsulations that turn a plain dial-up line into a real internet link, carrying IP packets over the phone wire: the difference between typing on someone else's UNIX machine and your own computer being on the internet. SLIP came first and stayed minimal; PPP succeeded it with negotiation and error detection. The program compiled on the shell account in 1993 spoke exactly this.

mosaic.desc: The browser that made the Web visible: released in 1993 by NCSA — the National Center for Supercomputing Applications at the University of Illinois — it was the first widely used browser to put images inline with the text and to run on the ordinary home systems of the day. One glimpse through it and the text-only internet acquired a face; its young team soon left to build Netscape.

wardialing.desc: A modem set to dial automatically through whole ranges of phone numbers, logging which ones answered — a carrier, a fax, or a PBX. Named for the 1983 film WarGames, it was the reconnaissance half of phreaking: mapping the network to find what was out there.

pre1996.closer: By the end of 1995 the foundation was set, and it was never really about the tricks. It was about the habit underneath them — taking a thing apart until it is genuinely understood, then explaining it to whoever comes next. The vendor badges, the certifications, the classrooms on four continents: all of it was built on a teenager with a cloned Sinclair, a soldering iron, a stolen dial tone, and an unreasonable need to know how everything worked.

era19962020.title: The practitioner
era19962020.subtitle: Two decades inside the networking and security industry, building, breaking, and fixing the systems that later became the curriculum.
era19962020.intro: This is the long middle of the story, and the reason the teaching that came later carries weight. For roughly two decades, the work was implementation: designing networks, standing them up, troubleshooting them under pressure, and being the person called when something critical broke. The journey ran through some of the defining names in networking, on two continents.
era19962020.s1Body: The formal career began in 1996 at Cabletron Systems, then a leading networking equipment vendor based in Rochester, New Hampshire. Over four and a half years, the role spanned field engineering and post-sales support, systems engineering and pre-sales, and, from 1997, certified instruction. The subject matter was the enterprise LAN and WAN of the era: switches, routers, Wi-Fi and WLAN, network management, NAC and UAC, and stateful-inspection firewalls. In 2000, Cabletron reorganized into four companies, and the part this career touched became Enterasys Networks.
era19962020.s2Body: The next chapter was in California. Riverstone Networks, a Cabletron spin-off building metropolitan-area network equipment, brought a move to Santa Clara on an H1-B1 work visa. The role was Tier-III product support engineer and knowledge-management coordinator: third-level technical escalation, recreating customer scenarios in the lab, regression testing and bug verification, and building the knowledge base that the rest of support relied on. The technologies were the backbone of the early-2000s carrier and metro world: Gigabit Ethernet, MPLS, metropolitan-area switching and routing, and BGP. This period also produced a formal milestone: as part of the visa process, a United States evaluation in 2001 recognized an educational background equivalent to a bachelor's degree in computer science and a bachelor's degree in business administration.
era19962020.s3Body: Back in Brazil, and contracted through Cisco Professional Services, the role shifted toward the customer relationship at its most demanding. As a high-touch operations manager and single point of contact, the work was post-sales customer-satisfaction management for two of Brazil's largest federal entities, SERPRO, the government's data-processing agency, and ECT-Correios, the national postal service. The technical surface was Catalyst switches and routers, PIX firewalls, and CSS, CSM, and ACE load balancers, but the real work was escalation management and keeping critical national infrastructure running smoothly.
era19962020.s4Body: The second half of the decade returned to the vendor side as an enterprise LAN subject-matter expert. At Enterasys from 2005, the focus was switches and routers, Wi-Fi and WLAN, network management, NAC and UAC, and intrusion detection and prevention, across solution design, implementation, auditing, and training. From 2009, at Juniper Networks through Professional Services for LATAM, the role was new-product advocacy for the strategic partner Telefónica Empresas in Brazil, centered on EX switches and SRX firewalls, with operations training on Junos-SRX delivered at Level 3 and Impsat, now Lumen.
era19962020.s5Title: The channel and consulting years, 2010 to 2020
era19962020.s5Body: The final decade of this era moved fluidly between roles, all of them building toward the instructor the story arrives at. There were stints as a network and security engineer through resellers and distributors, deepening expertise in enterprise switching and routing, firewalls and next-generation firewalls, SSL-VPN, user access control, WAN acceleration, and internet load-balancing across Juniper and Cisco solutions. Crucially, this period included the pivot toward F5, beginning work with F5 BIG-IP that would define the years to come. Across all of it, technical training was a constant thread, never absent from any role.
era19962020.closer: By 2020, the picture was complete: someone who had not just studied these systems but lived inside them for twenty years, on the vendor side and the customer side, in design and in crisis. That is the difference an implementer brings to a classroom. When this person explains why a configuration behaves a certain way, it is because they have seen it behave that way, at three in the morning, with a critical service waiting.

era2020present.title: The instructor
era2020present.subtitle: Full-time technical training, delivered globally, expanding platform by platform.
era2020present.intro: In 2020, a career-long thread became the whole cloth. After two decades where teaching accompanied every role, training became the work itself: full-time, official, certified instruction delivered to professionals and teams across the world. What makes this era distinct is not just the focus, but the deliberate, dated expansion across platforms, each one added on top of deep practitioner experience rather than in place of it.
era2020present.s1Body: Since 2020, the work has been delivering official instructor-led training, remotely and in person, in Portuguese and English, to audiences around the globe. The delivery reaches Australia, Singapore, India, Central Europe, the United States, and Brazil. Brazilian and German citizenship, together with travel clearance to the United States and the United Kingdom, make that global reach practical: unrestricted eligibility to work across the European Union and Mercosur, and the freedom to go where the classroom is.
era2020present.s2Body: F5 has been the anchor since the beginning of this era, building on work with BIG-IP that reaches back over a decade. The authorized curriculum covers the full BIG-IP instructor-led catalog: Local Traffic Manager, DNS, Advanced WAF, Access Policy Manager, Advanced Firewall Manager, SSL Orchestrator, BIG-IQ, iRules development, automation, and troubleshooting, ranging from one-day to four-day courses. Alongside the teaching runs a practical complement: managed F5 BIG-IP lab environments, so that hands-on practice always has somewhere real to happen.
era2020present.s3Body: What distinguishes recent years is a clear, dated progression. Extreme Networks training was added from 2021, covering EXOS switching, SD-WAN, and automation. Fortinet followed from 2024, centered on the FCP FortiGate Administrator track. Netskope joined from 2025, covering Netskope Security Cloud operation, administration, implementation, and integration. Each platform was added deliberately, each resting on the same two decades of network and security implementation underneath.
era2020present.s4Title: Through Red Education, to the world
era2020present.s4Body: The primary vehicle for this global delivery is Red Education, an authorized training center through which official certified courses reach organizations across regions. The role is global technical training instructor, delivering to the kind of international audiences that a São Paulo base, two passports, and a genuine love of travel make possible. The standing relationship turns individual expertise into something organizations worldwide can book and rely on.
era2020present.closer: This is where the through-line of the whole history pays off. The curiosity of the early years became the implementation depth of the practitioner years, and that depth is now what every student receives: not a presenter reading slides, but someone who built these systems explaining how they actually work, and why.

vendors.indexLede: Every vendor of a career since 1996, told chapter by chapter - from the hub wars to the platforms taught today. The wider industry family tree, including the partners taught alongside them, lives under Industry.

cabletron.name: Cabletron and Enterasys
cabletron.tagline: Where the career began, and the enterprise LAN was learned from the ground up.
cabletron.intro: The longest single thread in this history runs through one company and its successor. Cabletron Systems, a leading networking equipment vendor based in Rochester, New Hampshire, was the first employer, starting in 1996. When Cabletron reorganized into four companies in 2000, the line carried forward as Enterasys Networks, and the relationship resumed there from 2005. Together these span the formative decade of enterprise networking expertise.
cabletron.s1Body: Over four and a half years, the role moved through the full arc of vendor engineering: field engineering and post-sales support, systems engineering and pre-sales, and, from 1997, certified instruction. The daily work was hands-on and physical: installing, configuring, troubleshooting, upgrading, migrating, and sometimes physically relocating the secure, redundant, resilient, managed networks that ran São Paulo's enterprises, LAN, campus LAN, and WAN alike. The WAN side was built on Frame Relay and fractional-E1 leased lines, the LP circuits of the Brazilian carriers, reaching up to ATM and Packet over SONET on the faster links, and leaned mostly on Cisco routers, from the period when Cabletron partnered with Cisco on routing before it built its own. This was where the discipline of solution design, implementation, auditing, troubleshooting, and escalation management was first practised, and where teaching first entered the work.
cabletron.s2Body: Returning to the lineage as a services and support manager, the focus was the Secure Routing product line, with Enterasys as the regional focal point for Brazil. The work combined high-level customer support, complex troubleshooting, and escalation management, including the demanding escalations that came in from a key account, Petrobras in São Paulo, with localized product management alongside. The technical surface had grown to include network management, NAC and UAC, intrusion detection and prevention, and SIEM, across enterprise switching and routing. Enterasys was later acquired by Extreme Networks in 2013, closing a circle that connects this early work to a platform still taught today.
cabletron.certs: Cabletron Systems Engineer (CSE), 1999. Enterasys Systems Engineer (ESE), 2000 and 2007. Enterasys Certified Internetworking Engineer (ECIE), 2007.
cabletron.lineageDesc: Cabletron Systems, based in Rochester New Hampshire, split into four companies in 2000: Enterasys, Riverstone, Aprisma, and GNTS. Enterasys was acquired by Extreme Networks in 2013. Riverstone was acquired by Alcatel-Lucent in 2006.
cabletron.s3Body: One thread from the Enterasys years deserves recording precisely, because it is easy to overstate. This was the height of policy-based networking, Enterasys built its Secure Networks architecture around role-based access and 802.1X, and the work sat in close, constant contact with the demanding world of contact-center networks and the Avaya and Cisco IP-telephony systems running on top of them. The familiarity ran deep, but it was familiarity from the network side. The role was never unified-communications engineering; it carried no telephony certification and no hands-on UC work. What it delivered instead was the layer all of that depends on: a secure, redundant, resilient, monitored, guaranteed always-on superhighway for voice and mission-critical traffic. Being clear about where that line sits is part of describing the work honestly.
cabletron.ssrBody: One product from those years changed the trajectory. Cabletron's SmartSwitch Router, the SSR, came out of its 1998 acquisition of Yago Systems, an early Layer-3 switch that put wire-speed routing where the enterprise needed it. Becoming one of the world's top SSR specialists, and teaching it across Brazil to partners and customers, was the expertise that would carry the career out of Brazil entirely: the SSR line became Riverstone Networks, based in Santa Clara, and that is where it led next.
cabletron.fieldBody: None of this happened at a desk. It meant pulling cable under the petrol-ship pier at São Sebastião for Petrobras, alongside the country manager and nearly the entire local team, because the deadline and the late-fee clause left no other option. It meant climbing metal structures to mount antennas at the top, and crawling through tight, dusty spaces, always in full business attire, designer tie included. It meant constant travel by car, plane, and taxi, and waking up more than once not sure which city it was. It also meant meeting a great many people and making friends and acquaintances along the way. The work was physical, relentless, and, in its own way, a privilege.
cabletron.customersBody: Where the work went is its own kind of résumé. The customer list ran across the Brazilian economy of the era: the datacentres of national and international banks and credit-card processors, the BM&F futures exchange, and investment and stock-trading brokers; Rede Globo across many sites in Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo, Editora Abril, and print and cable-TV media; Petrobras throughout both states, Votorantim and VCP, and chemical and pharmaceutical industry; airlines, national bus-line operators, insurers, call centres, retail and fast-food chains, construction and development firms; mobile-phone and cable operators; federal, state, and municipal government; and large, geographically distributed university campuses. Few weeks looked alike.
cabletron.multiprotoBody: The enterprise network of the late 1990s was not the Ethernet-and-IP monoculture it would later become. The wire itself was as likely to be Token Ring, or an FDDI or CDDI fibre-and-copper ring backbone, as it was to be Ethernet. And IP was only one protocol among many, sharing the same segments with IBM's SNA, DEC's DECnet, Xerox's XNS, AppleTalk, and Novell's IPX. IPX, a descendant of XNS, was a notorious source of broadcast storms: it ran in any of four different Ethernet frame types, 802.3 “raw”, 802.2, SNAP, and Ethernet II, and a segment where those were mismatched, or where its SAP service broadcasts went unchecked, could drown itself in overhead while stations on the wrong frame type sat unable to talk at all. Making these protocols coexist on one infrastructure, and keeping it stable, was the daily reality.

juniper.name: NetScreen and Juniper
juniper.tagline: Enterprise switching and the security gateways that grew from NetScreen into the Juniper SRX line.
juniper.intro: Juniper Networks acquired NetScreen, the firewall company, in 2004, and NetScreen's security technology became the foundation of Juniper's secure-gateway line. Working with both reflects one continuous lineage: the NetScreen SSG firewalls and the SRX gateways that succeeded them. The relationship ran directly through Juniper from 2009, and continued through the reseller channel afterward.
juniper.s1Body: Contracted through Juniper Professional Services for Brazil, the role was new-product advocacy for the newly introduced enterprise lines: the SRX-series secure gateways and EX-series switches. The primary engagement was with the strategic partner Telefónica Empresas in Brazil. Beyond pre-sales advocacy, this period delivered real instruction: JUNOS networking-operating-system and SRX secure-gateway operations training for channel partners and customers, including operations training for the Level 3 Communications network operations center staff in Brazil and Argentina.
juniper.s2Body: The Juniper and NetScreen relationship continued through systems integrators and resellers. At CYLK, the work spanned configuration development, proof-of-concept and interoperability testing, and live implementation and troubleshooting across Juniper, NetScreen, and F5. At TDec, Juniper joined a multi-vendor consulting and training practice. The hands-on product range across these years was broad: EX switches, SRX and NetScreen SSG firewalls, SA SSL-VPN, J-series routers, and AX managed Wi-Fi.
juniper.certs: Juniper Networks Sales Specialist, Enterprise Networking (JNSS-EN), 2010. Juniper Networks Sales Associate, Enterprise Networking (JNSA-EN), 2010.
juniper.lineageDesc: NetScreen, maker of the SSG firewalls, was acquired by Juniper Networks in 2004 and became the foundation of Juniper's SRX secure-gateway line.

riverstone.name: Riverstone Networks
riverstone.tagline: Two years in Santa Clara, at the carrier and metropolitan-network edge.
riverstone.intro: The one chapter of this career set entirely outside Brazil. Riverstone Networks, a metropolitan-area-network equipment vendor based in Santa Clara, California, brought a move to the United States on an H1-B1 work visa. Riverstone had an unusual lineage of its own: originally a company called Yago, acquired by Cabletron around 1998, and re-emerged as an independent company through a spin-off in 2001. It was later acquired by Alcatel-Lucent in 2006.
riverstone.s1Body: The role carried two titles across two years: product support engineer and knowledge-management coordinator. As a Tier-III technical-support specialist, the work was third-level escalation, recreating customer scenarios in the lab, regression testing, and bug verification. As knowledge-management coordinator, it was building and organizing the knowledge base the rest of support relied on, alongside product-marketing support and internal training. This was the deepest technical-support work of the career, at the level where the hardest problems escalate.
riverstone.s2Body: The technologies were the backbone of the early-2000s carrier and metro era: Gigabit Ethernet, MPLS, metropolitan-area switching and routing, and wide-area IP routing including BGP. Working at the vendor's third-level support meant seeing how these systems behaved at the edges, under the conditions that only surface in production at scale. It also delivered formal instruction: Riverstone concepts, features, and configuration training, and customer-support issue-identification and escalation-procedure training.
riverstone.certs: Riverstone Certified Networking Professional (RCNP), 2001. United States educational-equivalency evaluation recognizing a background equivalent to a bachelor's degree in computer science and a bachelor's degree in business administration, completed in 2001 as part of the H1-B1 visa process.
riverstone.lineageDesc: Riverstone Networks began as a company called Yago, was acquired by Cabletron Systems around 1998, re-emerged as an independent company through a spin-off in 2001, and was acquired by Alcatel-Lucent in 2006.

cisco.name: Cisco Systems
cisco.tagline: Keeping critical national infrastructure running, as the single point of contact for Brazil's federal customers.
cisco.intro: Contracted through Cisco Professional Services in Brazil, this was the career at its most operationally demanding, where the work was less about any single technology and more about keeping critical systems running for customers who could not afford downtime.
cisco.s1Body: As a high-touch operations manager and single point of contact, the role was post-sales customer-satisfaction management for two of Brazil's largest federal entities: SERPRO, the government's data-processing agency, and ECT-Correios, the national postal service, across Brasília and São Paulo. The technical surface included Catalyst switches and routers, PIX firewalls, and CSS, CSM, and ACE content and load-balancing appliances. The real discipline, though, was escalation management and crisis handling, maintaining high satisfaction for accounts where the stakes were national.
cisco.s2Body: The Cisco relationship also produced formal training delivery: IOS and CatOS concepts and operations, and Layer 2 and Layer 3 networking concepts, operations, and troubleshooting, in multi-day courses. This complemented the deep operational experience with the structured instruction that would later become the whole focus of the career.
cisco.certs: Cisco Certified Network Associate (CCNA), 2000 and 2005. Cisco Internetwork Troubleshooting (CIT), 2003.

paloalto.name: Palo Alto Networks
paloalto.tagline: Next-generation firewalls, through the reseller channel and the classroom.
paloalto.intro: A focused relationship centered on next-generation firewall technology, carried through the reseller and integrator channel. This is a past engagement; Palo Alto is not among the platforms taught today, but the work was real and certified.
paloalto.s1Body: At TDec Network Group, Palo Alto joined a multi-vendor consulting and training practice spanning systems engineering, solutions architecture, application enablement, implementation, audits, and troubleshooting. The relationship continued through Cipher Security in 2015 for pre-sales and post-sales work. The technical focus was next-generation firewalls and the Panorama management platform, the core of Palo Alto's enterprise security offering at the time.
paloalto.s2Body: This period included formal training delivery: a PAN-OS 6.0 bootcamp, an early instance of the same practitioner-led teaching that continues today. Combined with the certifications earned in 2014, this established genuine depth in the platform, even as the career's focus later concentrated on other technologies.
paloalto.certs: Palo Alto Certified Network Security Engineer (CNSE 5.1), 2014. Palo Alto Networks Accredited Configuration Engineer (ACE), 2014. Palo Alto Networks Accredited Sales Expert (ASE), 2014. Note: these certifications are historical and are not maintained as current.

ironport.name: IronPort Systems
ironport.tagline: A brief chapter with an email-security startup, years before it became part of Cisco.
ironport.intro: Worth recording on its own rather than as a footnote. IronPort Systems was an independent email-security-appliance startup based in San Bruno, California, years before Cisco acquired it in 2007. This engagement predates that acquisition entirely, so it was a relationship with an independent company and its own technology, not with Cisco.
ironport.s1Body: Channel development and pre-sales technical consulting for IronPort's C-Series email-security appliances, across 2004 and 2005. It was the early-stage mix a young security vendor needs in a new market: building channel interest, and giving the technical pre-sales support that turns interest into deployments.
ironport.s2Body: The C-Series were purpose-built email-security appliances, known for reputation-based filtering at a time when spam and email-borne threats were escalating fast. Cisco acquired the company in 2007 and folded the technology into what became its Email Security Appliance line. That later history is why the name is familiar today, but the engagement here was with the independent company that came before it.
ironport.certs: No certification track. This was a short channel and pre-sales engagement.

extreme.name: Extreme Networks
extreme.tagline: Building RNP's metropolitan research networks, and the one vendor here that is also a platform taught today.
extreme.intro: This one closes a circle. Enterasys, the second half of the very first chapter of this career, was acquired by Extreme Networks in 2013, and the same year brought a return to that lineage from the other side: deploying Extreme in the field. Working through one of Brazil's first and best Extreme Networks partners, the work built metropolitan research networks for RNP, Brazil's National Education and Research Network.
extreme.s1Body: The project was the physical build-out of RNP's RedeComep program, Redes Comunitárias de Educação e Pesquisa, the initiative that gave Brazilian cities community-run metropolitan networks for education and research. Extreme nodes went in across Campinas and its region in São Paulo, the twin cities of Petrolina in Pernambuco and Juazeiro in Bahia, Marabá in Pará, and Castanhal in Pará. Each city's network was a long-distance metropolitan-area optical ring running EAPS, Extreme's Ethernet Automatic Protection Switching, the ring-resiliency protocol that keeps a fibre ring converged when a span fails, connecting RNP's point of presence to the federal and state higher-education and scientific-research institutions it served.
extreme.s2Body: Installing the nodes was only half of it. The other half was making the local teams self-sufficient: delivering ExtremeXOS (EXOS) Switching and Routing training, and the knowledge transfer that goes with it, how their systems had been configured, how the whole thing worked, and how to manage, troubleshoot, and expand it once the deployment team had moved on. Handing over the ability to run and grow the network was the point.
extreme.certs: Extreme Networks Certified Instructor. Extreme is one of the vendors in the instructor portfolio; the current authorizations are listed under Training. The work here, in 2013 to 2014, was field deployment and training delivery.

distribution.name: FireEye, McAfee and Ixia
distribution.tagline: A portfolio of security and test-and-measurement vendors, carried into the Brazilian channel through value-added distribution.
distribution.intro: A different kind of relationship from the rest of this list. For several years the work sat on the distribution side of the market, carrying vendors' technology into the Brazilian channel as a value-added distribution engineer, rather than working for any one of them directly. Three names from that portfolio are worth recording: FireEye, McAfee, and Ixia.
distribution.s1Body: Each covered a different corner of the market. FireEye was advanced threat detection, sandboxing and analysis aimed at the targeted, evasive attacks that signature-based tools miss. McAfee brought a broad endpoint- and network-security line. Ixia was the outlier and the interesting one: network test, measurement, and traffic generation, the equipment used to prove how networks and security devices behave under load, later acquired by Keysight. Together they spanned defence and the tools that verify it.
distribution.s2Body: Value-added distribution is its own skill. The role was pre-sales technical: architecting solutions, running demonstrations and proofs of concept, and enabling the resellers and integrators who took these products to end customers, translating each vendor's roadmap into something a partner could actually deploy and support. It is a vantage point that sees a lot of the market at once, across many vendors and many customer problems at the same time. The ScanSource line brought Pulse Secure into the portfolio - the secure-access lineage whose full story now has its own page in this record.
distribution.certs: Vendor and distribution enablement across the portfolio, rather than a single certification track.

f5.name: F5
f5.tagline: The application delivery company, and the platform taught most on this site.
f5.intro: F5 is the deepest chapter of the whole record: a decade that runs from a first administrator certification through the full specialist ladder to the instructor's chair, three DevCentral MVP years, and the flagship hub of this site.
f5.s1Body: The chapter opens in 2015 with the F5 Certified Administrator, earned while distributing the platform commercially. The specialist ladder followed: Certified Technology Specialist in LTM, DNS, ASM, and APM, then the two solution-expert peaks, F5-CSE Cloud (2021) and F5-CSE Security (2022). Climbing every module of BIG-IP one exam at a time is exactly the preparation a full-time instructor needs, and that is where it led.
f5.s2Body: F5 Authorized Instructor since 2020, delivering twelve official BIG-IP and TMOS courses, from administration fundamentals through LTM, DNS, ASM/AWAF, APM, iRules, and troubleshooting. F5's DevCentral community named Rodolfo an MVP three consecutive years, 2022 through 2024. On this site, F5 is the flagship: the largest vendor hub, with calculators, decoders, and explainers built for the same engineers who sit in those classes.
f5.certs: F5 Authorized Instructor (since 2020). Current certifications: F5-CSE Security, F5-CSE Cloud, F5-CTS LTM / DNS / ASM / APM, F5-CA. DevCentral MVP 2022, 2023, 2024. Full detail on the credentials page.

fortinet.name: Fortinet
fortinet.tagline: From the NSE ladder to the trainer's chair, through a certification program reborn.
fortinet.intro: The Fortinet chapter is the fastest climb on this record: from the first NSE exams in 2022 to Fortinet Certified Trainer in 2024, five official FCP courses in the teaching portfolio, and a front-row seat to the program's 2026 rebirth.
fortinet.s1Body: The ladder was climbed once in 2022, NSE 1 through NSE 4 under the legacy program, then again as Fortinet reorganized certification into the FCF, FCA, and FCP tracks: Fundamentals and Associate in Cybersecurity, Professional in Network Security, with the FortiGate, FortiAnalyzer, FortiManager, and FortiAuthenticator administrator badges alongside. In 2024 came the credential that matters most to a teacher: Fortinet Certified Trainer.
fortinet.s2Title: Five courses and a program transition
fortinet.s2Body: As FCT, Rodolfo delivers five official FCP courses across the FortiGate operating stack and its management plane. On July 15, 2026, Fortinet retired the FCP and FCSS era names and restored the eight-level NSE progression, converting active certifications forward, so this chapter's credentials now read NSE 1 through NSE 4 again, exactly where they began, one program generation later. The credentials page records that transition first-hand.
fortinet.certs: Fortinet Certified Trainer (2025 – 2027). Current certifications: Fortinet NSE 4 in FortiOS, NSE 3 / NSE 2 / NSE 1 in Cybersecurity (2026 program, converted from FCP-NS, FCA, and FCF). Full detail on the credentials page.

netskope.name: Netskope
netskope.tagline: The SSE teaching pillar: taught from inside the category's defining vendor.
netskope.intro: The Netskope chapter is the accreditation sprint of this record: administrator to integrator to architect in under two years, the instructor authorization in between, and the SSE category itself as the subject matter.
netskope.s1Body: It began with the Administrator accreditation in late 2024, followed by Integrator in 2025 and Architect in 2026, with the sales-side NCSSA and NCSSE alongside, a deliberate run through every angle of the platform: operating it, deploying it, designing for it, and positioning it. The hands-on implementation and operation labs were part of the same push.
netskope.s2Body: Netskope Certified Cloud Security Instructor since 2025, with the Trainer Accreditation Administrator License current through 2027, delivering the official operation and administration and implementation and integration courses. Teaching SSE from inside one of the vendors that defined the category closes a neat loop with this site's own glossary and tooling around SASE and SSE.
netskope.certs: Netskope Certified Cloud Security Instructor (since 2025); Trainer Accreditation, Administrator License (2026 – 2027). Current accreditations: Cloud Security Architect (2026 – 2028), Integrator (2025 – 2027), Administrator (2024 – 2026), plus NCSSA and NCSSE. Full detail on the credentials page.

ping.name: Ping Identity and ForgeRock
ping.tagline: The identity chapter: two bloodlines, one platform - federation from Denver, Sun's rescued open source from Oslo.
ping.intro: Ping Identity opens the identity era of this record - and it carries two heritages. The Denver federation pioneer behind PingFederate, and ForgeRock, the Norwegian fork that saved Sun Microsystems' open-source identity stack, were combined by Thoma Bravo in August 2023 into the platform this page profiles. In 2025 that alignment became formal training.
ping.s1Body: In 2025 Rodolfo completed Ping Identity's official PingFederate training and earned the PingFederate Practitioner credential, deep-diving the federation server that turned SAML from a specification into enterprise plumbing. For someone who teaches access management on other platforms and builds SAML and OIDC tooling for this site, it was the natural next protocol stack to master from the inside.
ping.s2Body: The identity toolset on this site decodes the same artifacts PingFederate and PingOne move in production: the SAML decoder, the JWT and OIDC inspectors, and the PKCE and TOTP tools all read the assertions, tokens, and flows federation runs on - locally, in the browser. The chapter's coursework runs through PingFederate practitioner training in 2025, and the federation patterns it teaches are exactly what those tools make visible.
ping.certs: Authorized Ping Identity instructor (cleared 2026). PingFederate Practitioner (2025), earned through Ping Identity Training. Full detail on the credentials page.
ping.lineageTitle: How two identity bloodlines became one
ping.lineageDesc: Ping was founded on federation in 2002. Sun open-sourced its access stack as OpenSSO in 2005; when Oracle shelved it in 2010, five Sun engineers forked it as ForgeRock (OpenAM, OpenDJ) and took it to the NYSE. Thoma Bravo took both private in 2022 and combined them on August 23, 2023.

zscaler.name: Zscaler
zscaler.tagline: Zero trust at cloud scale - the company that bet the perimeter would dissolve, and built the exchange that replaced it.
zscaler.intro: Zscaler enters this record carrying the thesis every earlier chapter kept testing: that the perimeter those firewalls and load balancers defended would dissolve, and that inspection would move into a cloud sitting between every user and every application. The Zero Trust Exchange is that bet built at planetary scale - one of the largest security clouds running - and its platform is now part of the curriculum this record teaches.
zscaler.s1Body: Two decades of this record run through firewalls, load balancers, and the perimeters they defended; Zscaler is the company that bet earliest that the perimeter would dissolve into a global inspection cloud. Studying the Zero Trust Exchange, ZIA, and ZPA architectures is the natural continuation of the SSE work already underway in the Netskope chapter, from the other side of the same market.
zscaler.s2Body: The JA4 and JA3 decoders on this site read the same TLS surface the Zero Trust Exchange inspects at scale: the handshake a client offers before any policy fires, and everything it reveals. It is a natural pairing - a cloud that terminates and inspects the world's TLS, and a toolbox that teaches what those handshakes say - with more of that tooling to follow.
zscaler.certs: Authorized Zscaler instructor (cleared 2026). Platform certifications will be recorded on the credentials page as they land.

pulse.name: Pulse Secure
pulse.tagline: The distribution chapter's secure-access thread: Neoteris DNA, carried through five flags.
pulse.intro: Pulse Secure entered this record from the distribution side: in the ScanSource year its secure-access line sat in the portfolio Rodolfo architected and demonstrated across Brazil - the SSL VPN he had first known from inside Juniper, in the very years Junos Pulse took shape.
pulse.s1Body: The work was value-added distribution at its most technical: architecting remote-access designs around Pulse Connect Secure, running demonstrations and proof-of-concepts, and enabling channel partners on a product that sold into every vertical with a workforce outside the office. Policy Secure added the NAC conversation to the same platform, and the distributor's job was making both make sense in Brazilian enterprise networks - sizing, integration points, and the honest limits.
pulse.s2Body: The product Rodolfo distributed carried more history than most companies: born as Neoteris's Instant Virtual Extranet, the appliance that defined clientless SSL VPN; acquired by NetScreen in 2003; carried into Juniper with the 2004 NetScreen acquisition, where it became the SA series and then Junos Pulse - the chapter that overlapped Rodolfo's own years inside Juniper. Siris Capital carved the business out in 2014 as Pulse Secure, and Ivanti closed its acquisition on December 1, 2020, alongside MobileIron - the full genealogy is on this page below, and the NetScreen chapter has its own page in this record.
pulse.certs: No Pulse-specific certifications; the work was distributor-side pre-sales and enablement. The secure-access lineage continues today as Ivanti Connect Secure.

vendors.industryPointerBody: Deep-research lineages of the Red Education training partners and the pioneers that shaped this industry - Check Point to Nortel, Symantec to Madge - live on the Industry page.

training.title: Platforms taught by someone who has run them.
training.intro: Official, certified instructor-led training across {courses} courses on the platforms at the center of modern networking and security. Every course is delivered by an authorized instructor with decades of hands-on implementation behind the teaching.
training.representativeNote: This catalog is representative, not exhaustive. Course names, durations, and content reflect current public information and are refined from official datasheets.
training.agendaPending: A detailed day-by-day agenda will be added from the official course datasheet.

teach.title: An instructor who built these systems before teaching them.
teach.lede: Most technical training is delivered by people who learned the material to teach it. This is the opposite: three decades of building, breaking, and fixing real networks and security systems, distilled into training that connects every concept to how it actually behaves in production.
teach.s1Title: Why this matters in a classroom
teach.s1Body: There is a difference between explaining how a feature is supposed to work and explaining why it behaves the way it does at three in the morning when something critical is down. The first comes from a manual. The second comes from having been there. Since 1996, the work was implementation: designing networks and standing them up, then troubleshooting them under pressure and being the person called to resolve the critical availability and security incidents that halt a business, for vendors and for some of the largest organizations in Brazil. That depth is what every student receives, not a presenter reading slides, but an engineer who has lived inside these systems explaining how they truly work.
teach.s2Body: Deep technical knowledge is necessary but not sufficient. The rarer skill is translating it: taking something genuinely complex, web application firewalls, identity federation, traffic management, SD-WAN, and making it click for someone encountering it for the first time. Students consistently single out the real-world examples and the clear, structured didactics, the ability to put each concept in the context of the work they actually do. Teaching has been a thread through this entire career, present in nearly every role since 1997, and the full-time focus since 2020.
teach.s3Body: Technical skill is built by doing, so every course has hands-on labs on real systems, backed by managed lab environments where practice always has somewhere real to happen. For people who already know the fundamentals and came to get their hands on the technology, that is exactly what they are here for, and it is time well spent. For those newer to it, the time goes first into the foundations the labs rest on, so the hands-on work makes sense rather than being followed by rote. Both are equally valuable, and the balance simply shifts with the room. The format adapts to the audience: virtual instructor-led training delivered globally, in-person when that serves better, in English or Portuguese. Either way, people should leave with something real and useful in hand and a real understanding of why it works.
teach.s4Body: The teaching rests on a foundation of formal recognition. F5 DevCentral MVP for three consecutive years, in 2022, 2023, and 2024. F5 certifications held since 2015, and instructor authorization across F5, Extreme Networks, Fortinet, Netskope, Ping Identity, and Zscaler. Delivery reaches Australia, Singapore, India, Central Europe, the United States, and Brazil, through Red Education, an authorized training center. The credentials matter, but they are shorthand for the thing underneath: a genuine, current command of the technology.
teach.platformsBody: Official, certified instructor-led training across the platforms at the center of modern networking and security. Each links to its full course catalog.
teach.s5Body: The same depth that makes for good teaching makes for good counsel. Thirty years across application delivery, network security, identity, and infrastructure, on both the vendor and the integrator side, with a consistent record of customer advocacy, is a perspective that travels well beyond a training room, into architecture decisions, technology selection, and the hard problems that do not fit a syllabus. The teaching is the focus; the experience behind it is available to teams that need more than a course.
teach.toolsBody: Alongside the training sits a growing set of free, privacy-first network and security tools, deterministic utilities that run entirely in your browser and never send your input anywhere. Subnet and CIDR math, IPv6, certificate and token inspection, and more, the kind of everyday tools a working engineer reaches for. They are built in the same spirit as the teaching: precise, practical, and genuinely useful. Try them, no sign-up, no tracking.
teach.historyBody: From building computers as a teenager in 1991, through two decades inside the networking and security industry, to full-time global instruction since 2020. The whole story is worth reading if you want to understand the experience behind the teaching.
teach.ctaBody: Whether you are looking to book official training, build a custom program for your team, or bring in experienced counsel on a hard problem, the door is open.

instructor.body: These courses are taught by Rodolfo Nützmann, a senior technical instructor delivering authorized F5, Fortinet, Extreme Networks, and Netskope training worldwide, built on a networking and security career that began in 1996 on the implementation side. The credentials, and what past students say, are a click away.

teach.envBody1: Live online classes (vILT) run on Zoom by preference, with Google Meet and Microsoft Teams equally supported when a customer's platform policy calls for it.
teach.envBody2: Hands-on labs are delivered through self-hosted lab environments that Rodolfo builds and maintains, through Red Education's lab infrastructure, and through platforms such as CloudShare, Skytap, ReadyTech Axis, and Amazon WorkSpaces - whichever fits the course and the customer.

certs.fortinetTransitionNote: On 15 July 2026, Fortinet retired the FCF / FCA / FCP / FCSS certification names and restored the eight-level NSE 1-8 progression. Certifications that were active on that date converted forward from the exams already passed: the cybersecurity fundamentals became NSE 1, NSE 2, and NSE 3, and the FortiGate / FortiOS exam behind FCP became NSE 4. Those NSE certifications are listed above as the current program; the FCP & FCSS credentials they replaced remain on record below. Each NSE certification inherits the expiry date of the certification it replaced.

colophon_page.title: How this was built
colophon_page.lede: Most websites do not explain themselves. This one does, because how it was made is part of what it is: a deliberate experiment in building well, with unusual collaborators, and a record worth keeping.
colophon_page.concordBody1: This site was designed and built through a protocol called CONCORD: a structured collaboration between one person and three different AI systems, each in a distinct role. It is a working method, with its own governance, its own decision record, and a single human accountable for every choice that shipped.
colophon_page.concordBody2: The premise is simple. Different AI models have different strengths, and a hard problem benefits from more than one perspective held in tension. So the work was divided by role, and one person sat at the center, relaying between them, ratifying what was sound, and rejecting what was not.
colophon_page.concordBody3: CONCORD was not adopted from anywhere. It was designed by Rodolfo Nützmann for this project, out of a practical need: how to draw on several AI systems at once, each genuinely strong at something different, without surrendering the single thread of human accountability that real work requires. The answer was to give each system a defined seat, keep them from negotiating with one another, and route every exchange through one person who held the whole picture. That arrangement has an older name. The AI systems are agents: they act on direction and on someone else's behalf. PRIME is the principal: the party who actually decides, who exercises the judgment, and who carries both the consequences and the name.
colophon_page.concordBody4: It began informally, as a way of dividing the work, and hardened over the build into a named method: fixed seats, a single overriding rule that nothing ships without PRIME ratifying it, and a written record of why each choice was made. The name states the aim: concord, agreement reached on purpose through a process, not whatever an unsupervised tool happens to produce.
colophon_page.concordPrincipal: The leverage is the agents'. The accountability is the principal's, and it does not transfer.
colophon_page.mech1Gloss: Each seat puts forward options within its own remit.
colophon_page.mech2Gloss: PRIME carries proposals between seats; they never negotiate directly.
colophon_page.mech3Gloss: PRIME accepts what is sound and rejects what is not. Nothing ships otherwise.
colophon_page.mech4Gloss: A standing set of house rules bounds every output, in every seat.
colophon_page.mech5Gloss: A written decision log keeps the reasoning behind each choice.
colophon_page.mech6Gloss: Context, the house rules, and that log persist as files, carried from one session to the next, so the method outlives any single conversation.
colophon_page.rolePrimeBody: The principal, and the sole ratifier. Every decision, every line that shipped, passed through one human who held the full picture and bore final responsibility. The agents proposed; PRIME disposed.
colophon_page.roleAnvilBody: The chief engineer seat. Architecture, code, content structure, and the build itself, turned from intention into a working, tested, deployable site.
colophon_page.roleScoutBody: The strategy and positioning seat. The questions of what this is, who it is for, and how it should present itself to the world.
colophon_page.rolePrismBody: The design seat. The visual language, the typography, the colour, and the feel of the thing, shaped into a coherent system.
colophon_page.seatsModelNote: AI model versions as of June 2026.
colophon_page.principlesBody: A few principles held throughout, and they are visible if you know where to look.
colophon_page.p1Body: The tools on this site calculate answers locally and deterministically. They do not call a server with your input, and they do not approximate. What runs in your browser, stays in your browser.
colophon_page.p2Body: The deterministic logic each tool runs is the whole of the tool. There is no hidden server step, no account, and no telemetry. Everything runs in your browser.
colophon_page.p3Body: Every part of the codebase is commented and documented, not as an afterthought but as a standing rule. The build is meant to be legible, to its maintainer and to anyone who inherits it.
colophon_page.p4Title: Built to last and to travel
colophon_page.p4Body: The site is a static export: fast, cacheable, and dependent on nothing at runtime. It is structured for many languages from the ground up, so it can speak to a global audience without being rebuilt.
colophon_page.stackBody: For those who care about such things, the technical foundation, stated plainly.
colophon_page.stackFrameworkV: Next.js 15 and React 19, exported as a fully static site
colophon_page.stackI18nV: next-intl, with {count} locales and right-to-left support
colophon_page.stackDesignV: A skinnable, token-based theme engine; the default theme is Obsidian
colophon_page.stackTypeV: Inter for prose, JetBrains Mono for data and codes
colophon_page.stackEngineV: A deterministic compute layer that runs entirely in the browser
colophon_page.stackSearchV: Static, client-side full-text search; no search server
colophon_page.codeSynth: The code is 100% synthesised by Claude.
colophon_page.vibeBody1: It is a fair question, and worth answering plainly. Vibe coding is a term the AI researcher Andrej Karpathy coined in early 2025 for a way of building software where you describe what you want to a language model, accept what it writes without reading it closely, and steer by results rather than by the code itself. He framed it as giving in to the vibes and forgetting that the code even exists, and was clear that it suited quick, throwaway projects more than systems people depend on.
colophon_page.vibeBody2: By that definition, part of this site was built that way, and it is worth owning rather than hiding. The application's surface, the framework wiring, the components, the styling, the plumbing that holds the pages together, was produced quickly with an AI engineer and steered by outcome and by a fixed set of house rules, not typed out by hand line by line. For that layer, where a mistake is visible and easily corrected, speed was the point.
colophon_page.vibeBody3: The parts that matter most are held to a different standard. Everything that computes your data is verified, not vibed: each tool's core is checked against the published standard it implements, the relevant RFCs and specifications, and its output is confirmed against independent references before it ships. As a widely cited line from the programmer Simon Willison puts it, code you have reviewed, tested, and understood is not vibe coding at all. Karpathy himself now calls the disciplined version agentic engineering: keeping the leverage of AI without conceding the quality of the result. That is the line this project draws. Fast where speed is free, rigorous where it counts, and one human accountable for all of it.
colophon_page.closingBody: Building software with AI collaborators is new enough that the honest thing is to be transparent about it. Nothing here was published without a human deciding it should be. The AIs were instruments, capable ones, but instruments. The judgment, the accountability, and the name on the work are human.
colophon_page.standardsLede: Every tool here implements a published specification, not a guess. The decoders and calculators are built against the documents that define their formats, and pinned to the test vectors those documents publish, so each answer is checked against the source of truth rather than against itself.
colophon_page.specsBody: JSON Web Tokens follow RFC 7519, with signatures and algorithms in RFC 7515 and 7518; PKCE is RFC 7636; Base64 and its siblings are RFC 4648; UUIDs are RFC 9562 (which obsoleted RFC 4122 in 2024 and ships its own test vectors); HMAC is RFC 2104, over the SHA family standardized in FIPS 180-4 and FIPS 202; X.509 certificates are RFC 5280; IPv4 and CIDR notation are RFC 4632; IPv6 addressing and its canonical text form are RFC 4291 and RFC 5952; and the cipher-suite decoder is driven by the official IANA TLS Cipher Suites registry, cross-referenced with the TLS 1.3 and 1.2 specifications (RFC 8446 and 5246), the registry-update rules that set the "Recommended" column (RFC 8447), and the RC4 prohibition (RFC 7465). Where a registry is the authority, its data is vendored directly rather than retyped.
colophon_page.vectorsBody: Each tool ships with a set of golden vectors: known inputs paired with known-correct outputs, taken from the relevant RFCs and standards bodies. They run on every build, so a refactor that quietly changes an answer fails the build instead of shipping.
colophon_page.owaspBody: The security tools are defined against OWASP's frameworks rather than assembled ad hoc. The cryptographic and TLS tools map onto the Cryptographic Failures and Security Misconfiguration areas of the OWASP Top 10 and the matching checks in the Application Security Verification Standard; the token tooling follows OWASP's guidance for inspecting and validating JWTs. OWASP's prevention cheat sheets also set hard rules for what gets built next: any XML or SAML handling added here is required to be hardened against XXE before it ships.
colophon_page.redblueBody: The same decode-and-explain that lets a red-teamer read a captured token lets a blue-teamer understand what their own stack is emitting. The platform deliberately sits on the analysis side of that line: it identifies, decodes, converts, and explains, and it stops short of forging, injecting, or defeating controls. That boundary is a design decision, not an oversight; these tools exist to teach and to diagnose, not to weaponize.
colophon_page.localBody: Everything runs in the browser. The tool calls a pure function: given the same input it returns the same output, holds no state, and sends nothing to a server. No cookies, no analytics, as the Privacy page sets out in full.
colophon_page.limitsBody1: This site runs as a single Cloudflare Worker serving static assets, and that platform has hard, published limits worth being honest about. A Worker version may carry at most 20,000 static files on the free plan and 100,000 on the paid plan (raised from 20,000 in September 2025, and only when deploying with Wrangler 4.34 or newer), with no single file over 25 MiB. Requests for static assets themselves are free and unlimited; only invocations of the Worker script, such as the API, are metered.
colophon_page.limitsBody2: Those numbers matter because this architecture multiplies content deliberately. Every rendered page ships as roughly three files: its HTML, the framework's navigation payload, and a search-index fragment, across all sixteen languages, and the English and Portuguese pages add machine-readable Markdown twins. In practice one tool costs about fifty files and a tool with its companion article about a hundred, so the current site stands at a little over eighteen thousand files. Under the paid ceiling that leaves room for hundreds more tools; the build is measured against these limits, and if the toolbox ever truly outgrows a single Worker, the expansion path is already mapped: splitting routes across additional Workers, each with its own allowance, and ultimately object storage with no count limit at all.
colophon_page.wordDef: 1. A note, traditionally at the end of a book, recording how it was made: who produced it, where, and with which tools and typefaces.
colophon_page.wordExt: 2. By modern extension, a page on a website that explains how the site was built. In other words: this page.
colophon_page.wordEty: From the Greek kolophōn, via Latin: summit, finishing touch.
colophon_page.fableNote: A note on Fable 5. It is the seat's intended engine, but only for a limited window on the current plan; after that it becomes pay-per-use, and the added cost means it will see little use here. In practice it also falls back to Claude Opus 4.8 for most of this work: ARSENAL's subject is network and application security, and the safeguards on Fable 5 route security-related requests to Opus 4.8, catching this content (sometimes as a false positive) because Fable 5 is currently gated from handling those subjects. So most of what stands here was built on Opus 4.8.

machineTranslation.notice: This page was translated by machine and may contain mistakes.

contribute.title: Help improve the translations
contribute.lede: Every language here other than English is a machine-made first draft. If you speak one of them and notice something wrong or clumsy, your corrections are genuinely welcome. Here is how to send them.
contribute.howBody: English is the source of truth, and every other language is translated from it, so mistakes are possible. Each language pack is a single text file of labelled phrases. Download the one you want to improve, change only the text after each label, and leave the labels and anything inside curly braces exactly as they are. Then email the edited file and mention the language. Every submission is reviewed by hand.
