api.title: API
api.lede: The tools on this site run in your browser and keep your data on your device. Each one is also built to run as a small, deterministic HTTP API, the programmatic counterpart to the browser tools. That API is fully implemented and documented here, but this site does not serve it. The reference below is a specification browser, not a live endpoint.
api.privacyTitle: What this means for your data
api.privacyBody: By design, the API would receive only the input you send it, compute a result, and return it: stateless, logging no query values or request bodies. Since the API is not served here, nothing you enter on this page is sent anywhere; the try-it panels below only show you the request you would make.
api.engineTitle: The same engine as the browser
api.engineBody: Every endpoint runs the same pure function the in-browser tool runs, so the API and the browser return byte-identical results.
api.specBody: The full contract is published as OpenAPI 3.1. Download it, point your own tooling at it, or read the reference below.
api.authValue: None by design. The API is specified as a public, read-only interface. It is not currently served from this site (see below).
api.loadError: The specification could not be loaded.
api.tryItHint: This builds the exact request URL you would call, but sends nothing: the API is not served from this site.
api.swaggerLoadError: Swagger UI could not be loaded.
api.servedTitle: Why the API is documented but not served here
api.servedBody: Running a public API means paying for compute on every call, with a bill that grows with usage in ways a single maintainer cannot cap safely. This site is deliberately free and predictable to run, so it serves the browser tools (which compute on your own device, at no cost to the host) and keeps the API dormant rather than risk unbounded cost. The endpoints are real and the contract is stable; what is switched off is the hosted execution. If you need the API today, the engine is open and can be run yourself.
api.swaggerCaveat: This is the standard Swagger UI rendering of the specification. It is a documentation browser only: there are no "try it out" controls, because the API is not served from this site.
api.servedOnTitle: The API is served locally on this origin
api.servedOnBody: Local API processing is switched on. The same-origin Worker serves each endpoint using the in-house engines, returning the same deterministic, byte-identical results as the browser tools. It is stateless and logs no query values or request bodies. If you would rather keep everything on your own device, the browser tools remain the zero-egress default.
api.selfHostTitle: Self-hosting: turn the toolbox into your API
api.selfHostBody: Everything needed to consume these tools as an Application Programming Interface (API) ships in the open-source repository, and the path from download to serving is deliberately short. One: download the source - github.com/ronutz/arsenal, Apache-2.0 licensed. Two: switch the API on - open src/config/apiSurface.ts and flip the single API_PROCESSING switch from 0 to 1; that one value drives both the Worker that serves the endpoints and every API badge in this interface, so the two can never disagree. Three: compile and deploy - npm install, npm run build, and publish to your own Cloudflare Workers account with the adapter already wired into the repository. Four: call the endpoints documented in the reference below - now answered by your deployment, on your domain, under your control.
api.selfHostBody2: That is the whole design: self-hosting plus a flip of a switch makes this an API toolbox. The same deterministic engines that power these pages answer the endpoints - tools that compute, never guess - and the switch works in both directions: set API_PROCESSING back to 0, redeploy, and the surface returns to documented-but-not-served, endpoints answering 404, badges neutral.

contributeIdeas.title: Share an idea
contributeIdeas.lede: This toolbox is built to grow, and the clearest signal for where it should go comes from the people using it. Found a bug, a mistake, or an inaccuracy? Want a tool that isn't here yet? See a better way to handle something, or a result you'd word differently? Send it, every kind of input is welcome.
contributeIdeas.sendBody: Bugs, mistakes, and inaccuracies of any kind: a tool that misbehaves, a wrong result, an error in a Learn article, or anything that simply looks off. Feature requests for tools that already exist. Ideas for new tools the toolbox should have. Corrections and additions to the Learn articles, like a clearer explanation, a better source, or a topic that is missing. Or simply a different angle on a problem. Rough is fine; a sentence is enough to start a conversation.
contributeIdeas.toolTitle: If you're proposing a new tool
contributeIdeas.toolBody: Tools here are small, self-describing modules: a manifest that says what the tool is and where its correctness comes from, one pure function that does the work, and a set of golden vectors, the fixed input-and-output pairs that prove it. A good fit computes locally and deterministically (the same input always yields the same output, with no clock, network, or randomness in the result), keeps anything sensitive on the device, and pins its correctness to a cited source such as an RFC rather than to opinion. You don't need to build any of that to propose one: just describe what it should compute, an example, and the source it rests on.
contributeIdeas.fitRule: One simple test decides it. Every tool here runs entirely in your browser and sends nothing anywhere, so a new tool has to be something a computer can work out just from what you type, by following a fixed, published rule. If it needs to go online, look something up live, sign you in, or remember you, it does not belong here.
contributeIdeas.fitYesBody: decoding or explaining something you paste in (a token, a certificate, a config, command output), converting between formats, calculating from a standard or formula, or generating from a rule, like a UUID, a hash, or a command line.
contributeIdeas.fitNoBody: anything that has to go online or check something live (testing a real website, querying a live DNS server, scanning an address), anything that needs an account, a login, or saved data, or anything whose answer is not fixed by a published standard.
contributeIdeas.fitUnsure: Not sure which side your idea falls on? Send it anyway and say what it should do. I will tell you honestly whether it fits, and why.
contributeIdeas.emailBody: Email is the channel. Tell me what you found or what you'd like, with enough detail to act on: an example, a link, the exact wording, whatever fits. If it makes the toolbox better, it gets built.
contributeIdeas.roadmapCheck: It's worth checking the roadmap first, to see what's already planned or shipped and avoid duplicates:
contributeIdeas.disclaimerIntro: In the interest of total honesty: these tools are built with CONCORD, my AI-assisted build process, and it runs on real, recurring costs. Three paid AI subscriptions carry it, one for each working seat.
contributeIdeas.disclaimerSeatAnvil: ANVIL, the engineering seat, runs on Claude from Anthropic, and this is the load-bearing one: it does the actual building, so it has to sit on one of Claude's Max tiers for there to be enough capacity to design, write, and test new tools at all.
contributeIdeas.disclaimerSeatScout: SCOUT, the strategy and brand seat, runs on ChatGPT Plus from OpenAI.
contributeIdeas.disclaimerSeatPrism: PRISM, the design seat, runs on Google AI Pro.
contributeIdeas.disclaimerInfra: On top of the three seats, a Cloudflare Workers plan keeps the site served, and the domains renew once a year for a fee of their own.
contributeIdeas.disclaimerTotal: All in, it comes to roughly USD 150 to USD 250 a month, depending mostly on which Claude tier the engineering seat is running that month.
contributeIdeas.disclaimerCadence: When the tooling is funded, tools get built. When it is not, building simply pauses until it is, which is why new tools tend to arrive in bursts rather than on a fixed schedule. You can read how the seats work, and who sits in each, on <colophon>the colophon</colophon>.
contributeIdeas.disclaimerBody2: If you would like to help it along, <coffee>buy me a coffee</coffee>. Every contribution goes into a single reserve spent on nothing but keeping that toolchain running: the three subscriptions, the hosting, and the domains. No cut, no surprises.
contributeIdeas.disclaimerStatus: Right now: the tooling is funded and new tools are actively being built.

license_page.title: License
license_page.lede: ronutz.com is open source. Its code is licensed under the Apache License 2.0, and its written content under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0). Both require attribution: reuse it, but credit the source. The site is also built on other open-source software, credited below.
license_page.closedHeading: Open source: Apache-2.0 (code) and CC BY 4.0 (content)
license_page.closedBody: The application code is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. The written content -- every Learn article, the tool copy and explanatory prose, and the documentation -- is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0). You may use, modify, and redistribute both, including commercially, provided you give appropriate credit to Rodolfo Nützmann (ronutz.com), link to the license, and indicate any changes. ronutz.com remains the canonical, maintained source.
license_page.thirdBody: This site stands on open-source work. The site itself is built with Next.js and React and shipped as a static export; internationalization uses next-intl; the Learn content is rendered with next-mdx-remote, gray-matter, remark-gfm, and js-yaml; and search is powered by Pagefind. These are provided under the MIT License, except next-mdx-remote (Mozilla Public License 2.0). The API reference embeds Swagger UI, under the Apache License 2.0. Each component stays under its own license, and the full notices ship alongside the source.
license_page.sourceBody: The full source is on GitHub. Issues and pull requests are welcome; ronutz.com remains the canonical, maintained deployment.

languageStatus.title: Translation status
languageStatus.explainBody: English is written and reviewed by a person. Brazilian Portuguese and the other languages are machine-translated, and marked by how far along they are: blue once a language covers the whole site, yellow while newer content is still in English and catching up. Languages marked red have no translation yet and are shown in English for now. Machine-translated pages also carry a short notice, and you are welcome to help improve any of them.

changelog.title: What is new
changelog.lede: A running record of new tools, new Learn articles, and significant changes to this site. There are {count} tools live today.
changelog.roadmapNote: Looking for what comes next instead? The <roadmap>roadmap</roadmap> tracks what is planned and queued; this page records what has already shipped.
changelog.metaDescription: A dated record of new tools, articles, and significant changes to ronutz.com.

roadmap.title: What's on the roadmap
roadmap.lede: The tools we're planning and considering for the toolbox. This page is generated from the live build catalogue, so it's always current: a tool appears here the moment it's queued and moves into the shipped count the moment it ships. No dates, just direction and rough priority.
roadmap.intentTitle: Built for more than a browser tab
roadmap.intentIntro: The code behind this toolbox is already open source. Two directions build on that, both intentions rather than promises.
roadmap.intentApi: The tools already run behind a small, versioned internal API. Opening that up as a hosted service, so the same deterministic engines answer a request and not just fill a browser tab, is a direction rather than a dated plan.
roadmap.intentOpen: Each tool is a self-contained, documented module, and the source is public. A packaged self-host path, dropped straight into your own build and CI pipelines and running inside your own environment with nothing leaving it, is the direction that openness makes possible.
roadmap.intentClose: What makes them possible is that the tooling has been built from the ground up expecting them: deterministic cores, no hidden state, a clean API surface, machine-readable docs.
roadmap.ideaBody: Every tool here started the same way: someone needed a small, specific thing and could not find it at the worst possible moment, the digital cousin of being one console cable short at 2am with the maintenance window closing. If the one you are reaching for is not here yet, propose it. Worth a scroll through the list first, though, in case that exact adapter is already on my bench half-built.
roadmap.metaDescription: The tools planned and under consideration for the ronutz.com toolbox, generated from the live build catalogue.
roadmap.ceiling: Growth here has a measured ceiling, not a vague one. The hosting platform caps how many static files one Worker may carry, and this site's own arithmetic — roughly a hundred files per tool-and-article pack, across sixteen languages — is checked against that cap on every build. Today there is room for hundreds more tools, and the expansion path beyond a single Worker is already designed, so the roadmap below is constrained by judgment, not by hosting.

meetingBingo.tagline: Every meeting has a script. Now it has a scorecard.
meetingBingo.intro: Pick the meeting type, get a fresh card of the cliches you are statistically guaranteed to hear, and mark each one as it lands. Five in a row, in any direction, is a bingo. Play discreetly.
meetingBingo.bingoSub: Tradition says you must now work the word bingo into the meeting, casually.
meetingBingo.creditRest: is a proud office tradition: invented at Silicon Graphics in 1993 by Tom Davis and immortalized by a Dilbert strip in 1994.

Friday deploy: the brain rests, production fends for itself.
If it compiled, it is done. If it ran, it shipped.
A bug in production is just a test run by the customer.
A workaround that works on the first try becomes the official architecture.
The documentation is the code. Good luck with that.
Rollback is for people who planned on coming back.
“Works on my machine” is now an SLA.

settings_page.title: Settings
settings_page.lede: Personalize how the site behaves. Everything here is saved on this device only — there are no accounts and no server, so your choices never leave your browser.
settings_page.shortcutsBody: Rebind any shortcut key to a different action. Pick an action from each menu; the change applies immediately. Some keys may be fixed by the site administrator, in which case they are shown locked. Press ? anywhere on the site to see the current shortcuts at a glance.
settings_page.persistNote: Saved on this device only. Clearing site data or using another browser or device restores the defaults.
settings_page.themeLangBody: Theme and language are set from the controls in the header. Your theme is remembered on this device; your language is remembered too, and a return visit to the site's home sends you to your preferred language. An explicit link in a specific language is always honored as-is.
settings_page.readingBody: Inline glossary hints add a subtle dashed underline to the first mention of a technical term in an article. Hover or tap it for a short definition, with a link to the full glossary entry. This is a device-only preference.
settings_page.hintToggleDesc: Underline glossary terms in articles and tool docs: the first mention of each term, every mention, or none.

bossScreens.metaDescription: Browse the vintage work-app screens the boss key can summon, and open any one fullscreen.
bossScreens.title: Boss-Key Screens
bossScreens.intro: Press the boss key anywhere (or B) and the page hides behind one of these vintage work apps. Here they all are — click any screen to see it fullscreen, then use the left and right arrows to browse the rest.

blurb.amiga: Kickstart's insert-disk screen: the animated hand offering a Workbench floppy, the friendliest boot in computing.
blurb.amipro: Samna's Amí was the first fully functional Windows word processor, a year ahead of Word for Windows. Lotus bought Samna in 1990; the colourful SmartIcons were its signature.
blurb.aol: For millions the internet WAS the blue screen and the friendly voice: You've Got Mail. Quantum's Q-Link became AOL in 1991 and mailed the world a floppy.
blurb.apple1: Wozniak's first: no OS, just the 256-byte WozMon. Press Reset and it prints a backslash and a blinking cursor, ready for hex.
blurb.appleii: The enhanced //e that ran a generation of US schools. Its ROM shows Apple //e, then the Applesoft ] prompt.
blurb.appleii1977: The original. No autostart: it comes up in Wozniak's monitor at a * prompt; Ctrl-B enters Integer BASIC and its > prompt.
blurb.appleiic: Apple's compact, portable II. With no disk it beeps, shows Apple //c, then Check Disk Drive; Ctrl-Reset drops to BASIC.
blurb.appleiigs: The 16-bit II: colour, sound, and the friendly Check Startup Device screen where the Apple logo slides side to side.
blurb.appleiiplus: The Apple II Plus with Applesoft in ROM: the plain Apple ][ banner and the ] prompt where countless first programs ran.
blurb.at286: The AT and its POST: a memory count marching upward before the operating system loads.
blurb.atari800xl: Atari's 8-bit workhorse: a deep blue screen, Atari BASIC's READY, and colours that slowly cycle to spare your TV.
blurb.bbcmicro: Acorn's machine that taught Britain to code, from its terse grey banner and BASIC prompt.
blurb.bsod: The Blue Screen of Death: Windows' STOP error, arguably the most recognized screen in PC history.
blurb.c64: The best-selling home computer ever. Boots to a blue BASIC V2 prompt; here it types a little program by itself.
blurb.clipper: Nantucket's dBASE compiler, the small-business workhorse: millions of DOS apps between 1985 and 1992, and Summer '87 stayed in use for a decade.
blurb.coco: The CoCo: Microsoft Color BASIC in black-on-green, the OK prompt waiting on a TV set.
blurb.commander: The twin-panel DOS file manager that defined orthodox file management, in unmistakable blue.
blurb.compuserve: The first big consumer online service, from Columbus, Ohio: forums, email and stock quotes behind a numbered menu and a lone ! prompt, long before the web.
blurb.cp300: Brazil's compact TRS-80 Model III clone. No disks, straight to cassette BASIC: Cass?, Memory Size?, then READY.
blurb.cp400: Brazil's TRS-80 Color clone (a CoCo 2 inside): Extended Color BASIC in black-on-green, the OK prompt waiting.
blurb.cp500: Brazil's TRS-80 Model III clone, in green phosphor. A reserva-de-mercado icon that ran BASIC and DOS 500.
blurb.cpc464: Unmistakable bright yellow on blue: Locomotive BASIC greeting you with Ready on a built-in cassette machine.
blurb.dbase: The database that ran 1980s business, driven from its famous dot prompt.
blurb.dbase2: Ratliff's Vulcan from JPL, renamed by Ashton-Tate marketing (there was never a dBASE I). The dot prompt that started the xBase world, on CP/M first.
blurb.esxi: The bare-metal hypervisor's yellow-and-grey console (the DCUI): host version, CPU and memory, and just two keys, F2 to configure and F12 to shut down. Released April 17, 2018.
blurb.expert: Gradiente's Brazilian MSX. Standard MSX BASIC, a detached keyboard, and a soap-opera-famous look.
blurb.harvard: SPC's Harvard Presentation Graphics let a DOS PC mix text, charts and clip art into slide shows; version 2.0 gave it the famous name.
blurb.hotbit: Sharp/Epcom's Brazilian MSX. The other national MSX, booting MSX BASIC with Portuguese accents.
blurb.ibmpc: The machine that set the standard. Cassette BASIC in ROM: the IBM Personal Computer BASIC banner.
blurb.lotus: The IBM PC's killer app and the archetypal boss-key screen: a spreadsheet that looks like real work.
blurb.macfinder: The desktop that taught the world the metaphor: menu bar, icons, and a Trash in the corner. System 7 (1991) finally let the Trash keep what you dropped in it.
blurb.macintosh: Susan Kare's friendly boot: a blinking floppy with a question mark, then the smiling Happy Mac and Welcome to Macintosh.
blurb.mosaic: The browser that showed the masses a picture and a link on the same page. Built at NCSA by Andreessen and Bina; its team went on to make Netscape.
blurb.msworks: Unveiled September 14, 1987 for DOS: word processor, spreadsheet, database and communications in one program that ran in 256 KB.
blurb.msx1: The Microsoft/ASCII home standard: the logo scrolls up, then MSX BASIC and its Ok prompt.
blurb.msxturbor: The last, fastest MSX (Japan only). The logo assembles from the sides, then MSX BASIC 4.0 on its R800 CPU.
blurb.multimate: The Wang word processor reborn on a PC, keyboard template and all. Ashton-Tate paid about USD 20 million for it in December 1985.
blurb.netware: The LAN era's heartbeat: MONITOR.NLM on the server console, utilization ticking about once a second, cache buffers healthy above Novell's 40% rule.
blurb.norton: Peter Norton's blue toolkit; the Disk Doctor rescued many a floppy.
blurb.oblivion: The ANSI art scene's darling: OBV/2's one-of-a-kind interface made underground boards beautiful. Freeware from 1997; v2.40 closed out the decade.
blurb.os2warp: IBM's object-oriented Workplace Shell, better DOS than DOS and a rock-solid multitasker. Warp 3 (1994) was its best shot at the desktop, LaunchPad and all.
blurb.pc386: A tricked-out 386 clone: the AMIBIOS POST counting memory, cache and turbo lit, Hit DEL for SETUP.
blurb.pcboard: Clark Development's high-end DOS BBS. The modem dials Salt Air, the carrier locks at 2400 bps, and the v15.22 node banner answers a caller.
blurb.profwrite: SPC's friendlier answer to WordPerfect: pfs:Write's successor with pull-down menus and context help, beloved by offices that just wanted letters typed.
blurb.remoteaccess: Andrew Milner's shareware giant from Australia, running multiple nodes under DESQview when few could. Its FrontDoor-styled sysop console sits waiting for a caller.
blurb.sidekick: Kahn's USD 49.95 TSR that popped over whatever you were doing on Ctrl-Alt: notepad, calculator, calendar, dialer, ASCII table. A million copies in three years.
blurb.sniffer: The DOS protocol analyzer that named a whole category, and Wireshark's ancestor. Three-panel Miller-column menus over a blue screen; first sold December 1986.
blurb.snifferconficker: A decoded capture in the Sniffer's three-pane view: a TCP SYN scan to port 445, the SMB flow Conficker rode via MS08-067. Header bytes only, no exploit payload.
blurb.snifferhttp420: A full HTTP decode in the Sniffer: Twitter's non-standard 420 Enhance your calm rate-limit response, headers and JSON body and all, the way the analyzer would show it.
blurb.snifferiloveyou: An SMTP decode of the LoveLetter worm's mail envelope: the ILOVEYOU subject line and its infamous attachment name. Mail headers only, no script.
blurb.sniffermirai: A decoded Telnet session to port 23, the credential-guessing vector the Mirai botnet used against IoT devices. Connection metadata only, no credential list.
blurb.sniffermorris: A decoded capture showing the fingerd (port 79) and sendmail (port 25) flows the 1988 Internet Worm rode. Protocol headers only, no exploit code.
blurb.snifferpetya: A decoded SMB flow to port 445, the EternalBlue / MS17-010 vector NotPetya shared with WannaCry. Header bytes only, no exploit payload.
blurb.snifferstuxnet: A decoded SMB print-spooler flow to port 445, one of the vectors (MS10-061) Stuxnet used to reach Siemens WinCC systems. Headers only, no payload.
blurb.snifferwannacry: A decoded SMB flow to port 445, the EternalBlue / MS17-010 vector WannaCry rode across flat networks in May 2017. Header bytes only, no exploit payload.
blurb.telegard: Born of leaked WWIV code and ancestor of Renegade: the hobbyist sysop's free workhorse, shown in its final Telegard 3.09/g2 form.
blurb.ti99: Texas Instruments' 16-bit home computer: the cyan title screen, TEXAS INSTRUMENTS HOME COMPUTER, press any key to begin.
blurb.tk82c: Microdigital's ZX81 clone, where many Brazilians (this one's author included) first learned to program: a blank screen and one inverse K.
blurb.tk90x: Brazil's ZX Spectrum clone, where many Brazilians first typed real programs in colour. It boots paper-white with its own name, TK90X - Color Computer, over a colour test bar.
blurb.tk95: The TK90X's successor in a Plus/4-style case with a better keyboard. Its power-on screen shows a colour band field and its name in full: Microdigital TK95.
blurb.trs80: Radio Shack's all-in-one. Cass? then Memory Size?, then Level II BASIC and its READY prompt.
blurb.turbopascal: Borland's beloved blue IDE that taught a generation to program, compiler and editor in one.
blurb.unitron: Brazil's faithful Apple II Plus clone with a Portuguese ROM: the Apple ][ banner and ] prompt, made under market reserve.
blurb.vic20: The friendly computer and the C64's older sibling: CBM BASIC V2 with 3583 bytes free.
blurb.videotexto: Brazil's videotex: TELESP's ANTIOPE-based service in São Paulo, 1982, home micros as terminals at 1200/75 bps, the index painting line by line in eight colours.
blurb.visicalc: The first spreadsheet, on the Apple II. The original boss screens imitated exactly this budget grid.
blurb.wfw311: The grey-chrome Program Manager that ran offices worldwide, now with peer networking baked in. Codenamed Snowball, it shipped November 8, 1993.
blurb.wordperfect: The near-blank blue writing screen that ruled DOS word processing, with just a status line in the corner.
blurb.wordstar: The word processor of the CP/M and early DOS era, remembered for its Ctrl-key command menus.
blurb.xtreegold: The gilded XTree: pull-down menus, Norton-style split panes and, from 2.0, ZIP archives handled in place.
blurb.xtreepro: Executive Systems' tree view grew up: multiple drives, more speed, and the single-letter command rows that made hard disks navigable.
blurb.zx81: Minimalism itself: a blank screen and a single inverse K cursor waiting in the corner.
blurb.zxspectrum: The machine that built the UK bedroom-coder scene. A blank white screen and one line: © 1982 Sinclair Research Ltd.
blurb.zxspectrum128: The 128K's start menu: Tape Loader, 128 BASIC, Calculator, 48 BASIC, over the AY chip's three-channel sound and that rainbow ident.
blurb.zxspectrum2a: Amstrad's black-cased +2A: a +3 with a cassette deck instead of the disk, so its menu still reads +3 BASIC. The last Spectrum made.
blurb.zxspectrum3: Amstrad's +3 added a 3-inch floppy and +3DOS to the Spectrum line. Loader, +3 BASIC, Calculator, 48 BASIC, and drive M: for the RAMdisk.
blurb.defrag: Microsoft's DOS 6 defragmenter, a cut-down Norton SpeedDisk: the whole drive as a grid of blocks, shuffled before your eyes.
blurb.copyiipc: Central Point's legendary disk backup utility that copied what others could not, track by patient track.

copyEditor.title: Copy editor
copyEditor.lede: View and edit every UI string on the site, one language at a time. Pick a language, search for the text you want, edit it in place, then export the result.
copyEditor.caveat: This is a workbench, not a live editor. The site is static with no server, so changes here cannot be saved back to the live site. Export the updated file (or just the changed keys), commit it into src/i18n/messages/, and redeploy to publish.

toolRequirements.heading: What this page needs
toolRequirements.browserNote: This tool computes entirely on your device, so nothing you type is sent anywhere. That local computation needs JavaScript; CSS is used for layout and theming.
toolRequirements.noscriptHeading: JavaScript is off, so the live tool will not run.
toolRequirements.noscriptBody: You can still read this page's explanation, open the Markdown reference and the Learn article, and follow the sources below. Turn JavaScript on to use the interactive tool.
toolRequirements.apiNoteOff: This tool is built to also work over an HTTP API. Processing is currently off, so the API is documented but not served; when it is switched on, you will be able to call it without a browser.
toolRequirements.apiNoteOn: This tool also works over an HTTP API, served locally on this origin. You can call it without a browser; the same deterministic engine returns byte-identical results.

toolApiEndpoint.heading: API endpoint
toolApiEndpoint.linkAria: Open the API reference for this tool
toolApiEndpoint.noteOff: Documented, not served. Opens the specification.
toolApiEndpoint.noteOn: Served locally on this origin. Opens the specification.

licenseBadges.caption: Code under Apache-2.0 · content under CC BY 4.0 · attribution required

guide.title: How to use this site
guide.lede: A practical guide to the site: what is here, how each tool works, which tools to reach for, the learning material that surrounds them, and how the site handles privacy, languages, and offline use. Everything computes locally in your browser.
guide.datasheetIntro: The essentials in one panel. These figures are generated from the site itself at build time, so they always match what is actually published.

ds.computeValue: In your browser, on your device
ds.techValue: Next.js, a statically exported progressive web app
ds.costValue: Free to use; the first useful answer is never behind a paywall

guide.quickrefIntro: Every general-purpose tool, grouped by category. This list is generated from the live registry, so it is always complete. Tools tied to a specific vendor live on that vendor's page.
guide.usageIntro: Common tasks, and the tools that get them done. Follow the tools in order for each task.

inspect-a-token.title: Inspect a token you received
inspect-a-token.desc: Decode a JSON Web Token to read its claims, then decode any Base64 fields inside it.

plan-a-subnet.title: Plan an IP subnet
plan-a-subnet.desc: Work out network ranges, masks, and host counts for IPv4, then do the same for IPv6.

work-with-a-certificate.title: Work with a TLS certificate
work-with-a-certificate.desc: Decode a certificate to see its fields, check a signing request, and plan the renewal date.

set-up-app-login.title: Set up sign-in for an app
set-up-app-login.desc: Understand an OpenID Connect flow, add PKCE for a public client, and add a one-time-password second factor.

read-a-dns-answer.title: Read a DNS answer
read-a-dns-answer.desc: Make sense of the output from dig, and from nslookup, line by line.

verify-data-integrity.title: Verify data integrity
verify-data-integrity.desc: Compute a hash of some data, or an HMAC when a shared secret is involved.

debug-a-saml-login.title: Debug a SAML single sign-on
debug-a-saml-login.desc: Decode a SAML response to read its assertions and conditions, then inspect the X.509 certificate that signed it.

map-a-jwks-to-tokens.title: Match a token to its signing key
map-a-jwks-to-tokens.desc: Read a JWKS key set to see the keys it publishes, then decode a JSON Web Token to find the key that signed it.

understand-a-tls-connection.title: Understand a TLS connection
understand-a-tls-connection.desc: Decode the negotiated cipher suite, inspect the server certificate, and review the response security headers.

harden-a-web-response.title: Harden a web response
harden-a-web-response.desc: Audit a site's HTTP security headers, then inspect the URL those headers protect.

assess-ssrf-risk.title: Assess a URL for SSRF risk
assess-ssrf-risk.desc: Classify whether a URL could reach an internal target, then inspect its parts in detail.

score-a-vulnerability.title: Score a vulnerability
score-a-vulnerability.desc: Decode a CVSS vector string into its metrics to understand how severe a finding really is.

convert-and-tidy-config.title: Convert and tidy a config
convert-and-tidy-config.desc: Convert a config between JSON and YAML, then format the result so it is easy to read.

compare-two-files.title: Compare two files
compare-two-files.desc: Normalize two JSON blobs with the formatter, then diff them to see exactly what changed.

reproduce-an-http-request.title: Reproduce an HTTP request
reproduce-an-http-request.desc: Break a URL into its parts, then translate the call into curl, fetch, or another client.

identify-an-unknown-hash.title: Identify an unknown hash
identify-an-unknown-hash.desc: Look up a digest against known values, then recompute a hash to confirm the match.

read-a-syslog-line.title: Read a syslog line
read-a-syslog-line.desc: Decode a syslog PRI value into its facility and severity.

build-and-test-a-regex.title: Build and test a regex
build-and-test-a-regex.desc: Write a regular expression and test it against sample text as you go.

start.b: Open any tool, paste or type your input, and the result appears immediately. There is no sign-in and nothing to install. Most tools include an Example button that fills in sample input, and a Clear button that resets the fields. Below each tool you will find related Learn articles that explain the underlying concept.

privacy.b: Tools run entirely in your browser, so the data you enter is not transmitted anywhere by default. Your theme choice is remembered on your device only. Your language is carried in the page address rather than stored. The Privacy page explains exactly what is and is not kept.

api.b: Each tool's logic is also documented as an API, with a machine-readable specification you can read on the API page. The API is documented for reference; whether requests are served depends on the site's current configuration, which the API page states plainly.

languages.b: The interface is available in several languages; switch using the language control in the header. English and Brazilian Portuguese are authored directly. Where a phrase has not yet been translated for a given language, the English text is shown for that phrase so nothing is ever missing.

offline.b: The site is a progressive web app. Once you have visited it, your browser can keep it available, and because the tools compute locally they keep working without a connection. You can add the site to your home screen or desktop like an app.

source.b: The project is open source. The code is licensed under Apache 2.0 and the written content under CC BY 4.0. You can read the source, report an issue, or suggest a tool from the repository, which is linked in the footer and on the License page.

learning.b: Two learning aids sit alongside the tools. The Glossary defines the acronyms, terms, and jargon the tools and articles use, from protocol names to attack backronyms, each with a short definition and a fuller explanation. And in the Learn articles and tool documentation, the first mention of a term carries a subtle dashed underline: hover or tap it for a quick definition without leaving the page, with a link to the full glossary entry. If you find the underlines distracting, turn them off under Settings, in the Reading section.

beyond.b: The site is more than the calculators. The Learn library holds articles that explain the concepts the tools operate on. The Vendors pages gather the vendor-specific tools and reference material for platforms like F5, along with the corporate history behind the names. And the Certifications area offers study guides and reading paths for people working toward a certification. All of it is reachable from the header.

disclaimer_page.metaDescription: No warranties, no guarantees, no liability. Everything on ronutz.com is provided as is, built from publicly available information, for use at your own risk and judgement.
disclaimer_page.title: Disclaimer and limitation of liability
disclaimer_page.lede: Please read this before relying on anything this site tells you. It is short, it is plain, and it applies to every page, every tool, every result, and every line of the open-source code.
disclaimer_page.short1: Use this site, its tools, and its code at your own risk and your own judgement. Nothing here is guaranteed: not the correctness of tool results, not the accuracy of articles or documentation, not the availability of the site itself.
disclaimer_page.short2: Everything here is built in good faith from publicly available information, and it can be incomplete, outdated, or simply wrong. Always verify against official vendor documentation and your own testing before you act on anything.
disclaimer_page.short3: If you do not agree with any part of this notice, do not use the site or the code.
disclaimer_page.asIsBody: The site, its tools, its content, and the associated open-source code are provided on an as is and as available basis, without warranties or conditions of any kind, whether express, implied, statutory, or otherwise, including but not limited to any implied warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, title, non-infringement, accuracy, completeness, reliability, or uninterrupted availability. No advice or information obtained from this site creates any warranty not expressly stated here, and nothing here is expressly stated.
disclaimer_page.accuracyTitle: Accuracy: public information, best effort, no guarantee
disclaimer_page.accuracyBody: Every tool, article, and document on this site is built from publicly available information such as vendor documentation, standards, and RFCs, interpreted in good faith. Sources can change, interpretations can be mistaken, software can have bugs, and content can go stale. Tool outputs are informational aids, not authoritative answers. Before you configure, change, buy, certify, or troubleshoot anything based on what you read or compute here, verify it against the official documentation of the vendor or standard in question and against your own testing. The responsibility for any decision you make, and for its consequences, is entirely yours.
disclaimer_page.adviceBody: Nothing on this site is professional, legal, security, compliance, or certification advice, and nothing here creates a professional relationship of any kind. This is an independent site. Product and company names that appear here, including F5, Fortinet, Extreme Networks, and Netskope, are trademarks of their respective owners, are used only to identify the technologies being discussed, and do not imply any affiliation with, sponsorship by, or endorsement from those companies.
disclaimer_page.liabilityBody: To the maximum extent permitted by applicable law, the site owner, contributors, and anyone else involved in creating, producing, hosting, or delivering this site or its code shall not be liable for any damages or losses of any kind arising out of or in connection with your use of, or inability to use, the site, its tools, its results, its content, or its code. This exclusion covers direct, indirect, incidental, special, consequential, punitive, and exemplary damages, and includes, without limitation, loss of data, loss of profits, loss of business, loss of goodwill, business interruption, procurement of substitute services, and personal or professional consequences of decisions taken in reliance on anything found here, even if advised of the possibility of such damages, and regardless of the theory of liability. Where applicable law does not allow the full exclusion of liability, liability is limited to the smallest amount and narrowest scope that law permits.
disclaimer_page.securityTitle: Security: best effort, no responsibility for breaches
disclaimer_page.securityBody: This site is designed to minimize risk: it is statically generated, its tools compute locally in your browser, and it avoids collecting data it does not need. A best effort is made to keep the site and its dependencies secure. No system is guaranteed to be secure, however, and no such guarantee is given here. The site owner cannot be held responsible for security breaches, vulnerabilities, or incidents, whether in this site, its dependencies, its hosting or delivery infrastructure, or its software supply chain, that might impact users of this site or users of the open-source code maintained at github.com/ronutz. If you find a security issue, a report through the contact page is genuinely appreciated.
disclaimer_page.openSourceBody: The code and written content published under github.com/ronutz are open source: the code under the Apache License 2.0 and the content under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0). Those licenses govern that material and provide it as is, each with its own disclaimer of warranty and limitation of liability, which apply in full to anyone who uses, copies, modifies, or redistributes it. Nothing on this page reduces or replaces the terms of those licenses.
disclaimer_page.changesBody: The site, its tools, its content, its code, and this notice may change, break, or disappear at any time, without notice and without obligation to anyone. The current version of this page is the one that applies.
disclaimer_page.acceptBody: By using this site or its code you accept this notice in full. If you do not accept it, your only remedy is to stop using the site and the code.

notFound.bofhAgainTitle: Reopening produces a new excuse. It does not produce a fix.
notFound.lostTitle: You seem lost. Here is one, plucked out of digital randomness:

dev.metaDescription: The developer wing of ronutz.com: /dev/fun, the not-serious shelf; /dev/other, the green room for shapes the catalogue cannot hold yet; and /dev/out, the red room where tools ask the live internet.
dev.title: /dev
dev.tagline: Three rooms off the main floor.
dev.intro: Everything on the main site computes locally and deterministically. This wing holds what does not fit that promise: one room for play, one, in green, for shapes the catalogue cannot hold yet, and one, in red, for tools that talk to the outside world.

fun.title: /dev/fun
fun.tagline: The not-serious shelf. Toys, easter eggs, and one very dramatic brain.

other.title: /dev/other
other.tagline: Off the catalogue, still on principle. Shapes the definitions cannot hold yet.

out.title: /dev/out
out.tagline: Tools that ask, not guess. Live lookups against the real internet, in a clearly marked room.

devOther.metaTitle: /dev/other - off the catalogue, still on principle
devOther.metaDescription: The green room of ronutz.com: tools whose shapes the main catalogue's definitions cannot hold yet - experimental contracts, odd forms - while the house principles (local-first, no accounts, no tracking, open source) keep holding.
devOther.title: /dev/other
devOther.tagline: Off the catalogue, still on principle.
devOther.intro: Every tool on the main floor signs the same contract: a manifest, golden vectors, a documented shape. This room is for tools that cannot sign it yet - not because they talk to the internet (those live next door in /dev/out, in red), but because their shape fits no drawer. The principles still hold; the paperwork differs.

notice.title: What 'other' means here
notice.p1: Tools in this room live outside the main catalogue's definitions: experimental input contracts, unusual output shapes, ideas still finding their form. They are not lesser - they are early or odd, and labeled as such, honestly.
notice.p2: The house rules keep holding. Local-first unless a tool states otherwise on its own door, no accounts, no tracking by us, no ads, open source. Tools whose whole job is asking the live internet live next door in /dev/out, behind red walls.

devOther.empty: No residents yet. The first tool that defies the definitions for a reason other than network egress moves in here - green walls, honest label.

subnetDrill.metaDescription: Timed subnetting practice: network and broadcast addresses, usable hosts, masks, prefix sizing, and containment, generated fresh each round with a remembered streak. Runs entirely in your browser.
subnetDrill.name: Subnetting drill
subnetDrill.blurb: Randomized CIDR practice with difficulty tiers and a streak your browser remembers.
subnetDrill.why: This tool lives in the green room because it GENERATES exercises (randomness) and REMEMBERS your streak (state) - two things the catalogue's fixed input-to-output tools do not do. The math it quizzes is the site's own CIDR engine.
subnetDrill.about1: Pick a difficulty and each round deals ten fresh questions drawn from a new random seed, so no two rounds repeat. The subnet arithmetic is computed by the same engine behind the CIDR tool, so every canonical answer is exactly what that tool would produce.
subnetDrill.about2: Your current streak, best streak, and lifetime score are kept in your browser's local storage and never leave it. Clear them any time with Reset; nothing here is uploaded or tracked.
subnetDrill.runsLocally: Everything - the questions, the grading, the score - happens in your browser. Nothing is generated on or sent to a server.

q.hostToPrefix: Smallest prefix that fits at least {n} usable hosts?

subnetDrill.name: Subnetting drill
subnetDrill.blurb: Randomized CIDR practice with difficulty tiers and a streak your browser remembers - the math is the site's own engine.

pcapAnalyzer.name: PCAP analyzer
pcapAnalyzer.blurb: Drop a capture and read it locally - protocols, conversations, talkers, anomaly flags. Nothing is uploaded.

fingerprint.name: Self-fingerprint inspector
fingerprint.blurb: The signals a tracker could read to identify your browser - shown only to you, computed only here.

serialConsole.name: WebSerial console
serialConsole.blurb: A serial terminal in your browser - console into gear over a USB adapter, nothing installed or uploaded.

pcap.metaTitle: PCAP analyzer - read a capture in your browser
pcap.metaDescription: Drop a .pcap or .pcapng and get an L2-L4 summary, conversation and top-talker tables, and first-tier anomaly flags - computed entirely in your browser, nothing uploaded.
pcap.name: PCAP analyzer
pcap.blurb: Drop a capture and read it locally: protocols, conversations, talkers, and anomaly flags - nothing uploaded.
pcap.why: This tool lives in the green room because its input is a BINARY FILE, not the paste/form text the catalogue's tools take. That is an input contract the catalogue cannot hold yet; the day it can, this tool graduates to the main floor.
pcap.about1: Drop a capture file and it is read into memory and dissected in your browser: the container format (classic pcap in either byte order, including the nanosecond variant, and pcapng), then Ethernet through IPv4/IPv6 and TCP/UDP/ICMP - enough for the overview, the conversation and top-talker tables, and a first tier of anomaly flags stated as observations, not verdicts.
pcap.about2: This first version dissects layers 2 through 4. Application-payload parsing (DNS, TLS, HTTP details) is a deliberate future pass, not a hidden gap - the tool tells you the depth it reached. Files are capped at 50 MB because a browser is for triage, not multi-gigabyte forensics; larger captures are refused rather than freezing the tab.
pcap.dropHint: or click to choose a file - it never leaves your browser
pcap.runsLocally: The file is read into memory and dissected entirely in your browser. Nothing is uploaded; there is no server round-trip at all.
pcap.depthNote: This summary covers layers 2-4. Application-payload dissection (DNS/TLS/HTTP contents) is a future pass.

flags.synNoSynAck: {syn} SYN packets with no SYN-ACK in reply - connections that were attempted but never completed a handshake.
flags.synImbalance: SYN packets ({syn}) far outnumber SYN-ACKs ({synack}) - worth checking whether a host is scanning or a service is unreachable.
flags.highRst: {rst} RST packets, a high share of the capture - connections are being reset often.
flags.truncated: The file ended mid-record; the last packet was incomplete and parsing stopped there.
flags.singleConversation: The whole capture is a single conversation - a targeted capture, or a filter left applied.

errors.empty: That file is too small to be a capture.
errors.format: This does not look like a pcap or pcapng file (unrecognized magic).
errors.unsupported: That capture format is not supported yet.
errors.tooBig: That file is larger than {max}. A browser dissector is for triage; open very large captures in a desktop tool.
errors.read: The file could not be read.

fingerprint.metaTitle: Self-fingerprint inspector - what this page can read about you
fingerprint.metaDescription: See the browser and device signals a tracker could read to fingerprint you - user agent, screen, canvas and WebGL hashes, fonts, timezone and more - computed entirely in your browser and shown only to you.
fingerprint.name: Self-fingerprint inspector
fingerprint.blurb: The signals a tracker could read to identify your browser, shown only to you and computed only here.
fingerprint.why: This tool lives in the green room because its input is your BROWSER AND DEVICE - the output legitimately differs for every visitor, so there is no fixed correct answer for golden vectors to assert. It is the privacy thesis made visible.
fingerprint.intro: Click below and this page computes, in your browser, the signals a fingerprinting script would read. This tool sends none of it anywhere - the reading is shown only to you.
fingerprint.runsLocally: Every signal is computed in your browser and displayed here only. Nothing is uploaded, stored, or compared against anyone else.
fingerprint.entropyNote: About {n} of these signals are individually high-entropy - the more of them a tracker can read, the more uniquely they identify you. This is a rough indicator, not a precise bit count.
fingerprint.footer: Reload the page to discard this reading; the tool computed it in this tab and sent it nowhere.
fingerprint.about1: Fingerprinting does not need cookies. A script can combine your user agent, screen geometry, installed fonts, and the exact way your GPU renders a hidden canvas into an identifier that follows you across sites without any stored ID. This tool computes those same signals so you can see the surface for yourself.
fingerprint.about2: This tool runs locally and shows the reading only to you - it uploads and retains nothing. But that is a fact about the tool, not about the signals, and the difference is the real lesson. Your user agent and the other HTTP request headers, along with your TLS handshake (the basis of JA3 and JA4), are already sent to every server you connect to; passive fingerprinting is just servers reading what you send. Whether a given server keeps it is up to that server. The other signals here - the canvas and WebGL hashes, the font probe, the screen metrics - are not in the request at all; they sit in your browser until a script on a page you load reads them and sends them back. So the surface splits in two: what you broadcast on every connection, and what a script can collect if you let its page run.

webserial.metaTitle: WebSerial console - a browser terminal for console cables
webserial.metaDescription: A serial terminal in your browser for network gear: connect a USB console adapter, pick a baud rate, and read and send lines - all local, Chromium-only, nothing uploaded.
webserial.name: WebSerial console
webserial.blurb: A serial terminal in your browser - console into a switch or firewall over a USB adapter, nothing installed.
webserial.why: This tool lives in the green room because it uses the browser's serial hardware-permission API and holds a LIVE session - there is no fixed input and output to golden-vector. The catalogue's tools are pure functions; a live console is not.
webserial.runsLocally: This talks only between your browser and the connected device. Nothing is uploaded; the session lives in this tab and is gone on reload unless you download it.
webserial.unsupported: This browser does not support the Web Serial API.
webserial.unsupportedHint: Web Serial is available in Chromium-based browsers (Chrome, Edge, Opera) on desktop. Open this page there to use the console.
webserial.connectError: Could not open that port. Another program may be holding it, or the connection was cancelled.
webserial.consolePlaceholder: Session output will appear here once you connect to a port.
webserial.linePlaceholder: Type a command and press Enter
webserial.about1: Console access usually means installing a terminal program and hunting for the right COM port. The Web Serial API lets a browser talk to a USB-to-serial adapter directly, so you can console into a switch, router, or firewall from a locked-down machine with nothing installed - pick the baud rate, click connect, and the browser shows its own device picker.
webserial.about2: The console is Chromium-only and permission-gated by design: the browser, not this page, decides which device you grant access to. Nothing that scrolls by is stored or sent; the scrollback lives in memory and is discarded on reload unless you download it.
webserial.pasteHint: Paste a config block and send it line by line, with a delay so the console does not drop lines.
webserial.logStarted: Disk log started - every line is written to the chosen file as it arrives.
webserial.logStopped: Disk log stopped and file closed.
webserial.logUnsupported: Real-time disk logging needs the File System Access API (Chromium browsers - the same family Web Serial requires).
webserial.interactiveHint: Keys go to the device: Enter = line ending, Backspace = 0x7F, arrows = ANSI. Click outside to release.

devOut.metaDescription: The red room of ronutz.com: live lookups against the real internet - RDAP and friends - with the rules of engagement stated out loud. Your query goes browser-direct to the official registry; nothing is sent to ronutz.com servers.
devOut.title: /dev/out
devOut.intro: The main site's thesis is tools that compute, never guess: local, deterministic, zero egress. The tools in this room cannot keep that promise, because their whole job is to ask the live internet a question. So they live here, behind red walls, with the differences written on the door.

notice.title: How this room differs from the rest of the site
notice.p1: Tools here send your input out of the browser. It goes directly from your browser to the official service named on screen (the registry that answers your query) - never to ronutz.com servers, which see nothing, in this version. Nothing leaves until you explicitly press Ask.
notice.p2: Results are the live world, not a computation. They come from third parties, can change between two identical queries, can be rate-limited or unavailable, and carry no golden-vector guarantee - the tested, deterministic parts here are the input parsing, the registry routing, and the response parsing, and the tool says which registry it will ask before anything is sent.
notice.p3: Everything else still holds. No account, no tracking by us, no ads, open source. The third party you query will see your request (that is how asking works) - its own terms and privacy policy apply to that hop, and each tool links the exact endpoint so you can inspect it.
notice.p4: The deep-red background is the marker: same darkness as the rest of the site, different hue, different rules. When the walls are red, you are in the room where packets leave.

rdapLookup.name: RDAP lookup
rdapLookup.blurb: WHOIS, the modern way: domain, IP, or AS number in, the official registry's structured answer out - asked directly from your browser, with the registry named before anything is sent.

asnLookup.name: ASN lookup
asnLookup.blurb: Who holds an AS number: special-purpose ASNs answered locally with their RFC, real ones fetched browser-direct from the owning RIR - with the registry named before anything is sent.

rdap.metaTitle: RDAP lookup - WHOIS, the modern way
rdap.metaDescription: Query the official RDAP registries for a domain, IP address, or AS number, browser-direct. Deterministic routing from vendored IANA bootstrap snapshots; explicit-egress design: nothing leaves until you press Ask.
rdap.inputLabel: Domain, IP address, or AS number
rdap.nothingLeaves: Nothing leaves your browser until you press Ask. Typing only computes, locally, which registry would answer.
rdap.willAsk: This {kind} query would ask {registry} directly from your browser:
rdap.notFoundBody: The registry answered 404: no object by that name in its database. For a domain, that usually means it is not registered (or the registry does not publish it over RDAP).
rdap.httpErrorBody: That status comes from the registry, not from this site. It may be rate limiting, maintenance, or a query it will not serve. The same query from your own terminal:
rdap.netErrorTitle: The query could not complete from the browser
rdap.netErrorBody: Either the network failed or the registry does not allow browser-direct queries (no CORS header - some registries, RIPE among them as of 2026-07-08, do not send one). This is a known, honest limit of the browser-direct design: nothing was silently proxied. Run the identical query from your own terminal:
rdap.bootstrapNote: Registry routing is deterministic: it walks vendored snapshots of the four IANA RDAP bootstrap files (RFC 9224), vendored {vendored}; the dns table's own publication stamp is {dns}. The live answer, by design, is not deterministic - that is why this tool lives in the green room.

errors.empty: Enter a domain, an IP address, or an AS number.
errors.format: Could not read that as a domain, IP address, or AS number.
errors.noEndpoint: IANA's bootstrap registry publishes no RDAP endpoint for that - honest answer: this tool cannot ask anyone about it.

rdap.about1: RDAP is WHOIS rebuilt on HTTPS and JSON (RFCs 7480, 9082, 9083): the same registry data, structured, with consistent field names and honest redaction notices instead of free-form text. This tool resolves which registry is authoritative using vendored snapshots of IANA's bootstrap files (RFC 9224), builds the exact query URL, shows it, and only sends it when you press Ask - directly from your browser to the registry, never through ronutz.com.
rdap.about2: The deterministic layers (input classification, IDN punycode conversion, bootstrap routing, URL construction, response parsing) are golden-vector-tested like every tool on the main site. The one thing that cannot be pinned is the registry's live answer - which is exactly why this tool lives here and not at /tools.

asn.metaTitle: ASN lookup - who holds an AS number
asn.metaDescription: Type an AS number and see, before anything is sent, which regional registry would answer. Special-purpose ASNs (private, documentation, AS_TRANS, AS0) are explained locally with their RFC and never leave the browser; real ones are fetched browser-direct from the owning RIR on an explicit Ask.
asn.nothingLeaves: Nothing leaves the browser until you press Ask. Typing only computes, locally, what kind of number this is and which registry would be asked.
asn.willAsk: This would ask {registry} (browser-direct, never through ronutz.com):
asn.notFoundTitle: The registry has no object for this number
asn.notFoundBody: An HTTP 404 from the RIR means no autnum object is registered there for this number. That is the registry's honest answer, relayed unchanged.
asn.httpErrorBody: Shown verbatim rather than interpreted. You can run the same query from your own machine:
asn.netErrorTitle: The browser could not complete the request
asn.netErrorBody: This is either a network failure or a registry that does not send CORS headers to browsers (RIPE, as of 2026-07-08, does not). The page cannot tell the two apart by design. The same query from your own machine:
asn.redirectNote: The bootstrap registry redirected this number to {host} - a national registry answering on the RIR's behalf. The browser followed the redirect; the answer above is theirs.
asn.provenance: Routing is decided locally against a vendored copy of the IANA RDAP bootstrap (asn.json publication {asnPub}, vendored {vendored}); special-purpose classification against the IANA Special-Purpose AS Numbers registry (accessed {specialAccessed}). Live answers come from the registries themselves and carry no golden-vector guarantee.
asn.about1: An Autonomous System number identifies a routing domain on the internet, and five regional registries hold the records of who each one belongs to. This tool classifies your input locally, and only for globally-assignable numbers builds the one URL that would answer - the owning RIR's RDAP autnum endpoint - showing it to you before anything is sent.
asn.about2: Two whole classes of answer never touch the network: special-purpose numbers (AS0, AS112, AS_TRANS, documentation and private ranges, the two reserved ends of the space) are explained with their RFC right here, and numbers falling in bootstrap gaps are reported as unallocated. An honest tool refuses to pretend a lookup where none exists.

special.body: This is a special-purpose AS number, set aside by {rfc}. No regional registry holds a delegation for it, so there is nothing to ask and no query will be made.
special.noEgress: Answered entirely locally - zero bytes left the browser.

unallocated.title: not delegated to any registry
unallocated.body: This number falls in a gap of the IANA bootstrap file: no RIR currently serves RDAP for it, which usually means the block is unallocated or not yet delegated. That is the answer; no query will be made.

errors.format: Not an AS number - accepted forms: AS15169, as 15169, or the bare number.
errors.range: Beyond the 32-bit AS number space (the maximum is 4294967295).

vendorsHome.title: Vendor hubs
vendorsHome.lede: One home for the vendors this site is built around. Each hub gathers every tool and every article for that vendor in one place - tools that compute locally in your browser, articles grounded in the vendor's own documentation.
vendorsHome.comingSoon: Hub opens with its first tool
vendorsHome.note: Each hub is the single place that gathers everything the site holds for a vendor: the tools, grouped by product family, and the full article library for that vendor, on one page. The same hubs are reachable from the strip on the Tools and Learn listings.
vendorsHome.scopeNote: These four are the vendors whose training this site's author is authorized to deliver. Tools and articles may reference other vendors' technologies where relevant, but a hub here means an authorized-training relationship, not just coverage.
vendorsHome.metaDescription: The vendor hubs on ronutz.com: F5, Fortinet, Netskope, Extreme Networks, Zscaler, and Ping Identity, each gathering every tool and article for that vendor on one page.

blurbs.f5: BIG-IP and the wider F5 estate: traffic management, DNS, application security, access, and F5 Distributed Cloud. The deepest hub on the site.
blurbs.fortinet: The Fortinet Security Fabric: FortiGate and FortiOS, SASE, secure operations, application security, and the FortiGuard services.
blurbs.netskope: Netskope One and SSE: the next-gen secure web gateway, CASB, ZTNA, cloud firewall, and the data-protection and platform layers.
blurbs.extreme: Extreme Networks: Platform ONE, the ExtremeCloud IQ continuum, wired and wireless and fabric, access security, and analytics.
blurbs.zscaler: Zscaler and the Zero Trust Exchange: cloud secure web gateway and firewall (ZIA), zero trust access to private applications (ZPA), digital experience monitoring (ZDX), and data protection. Security delivered as a global cloud, not an appliance.
blurbs.ping: Ping Identity and ForgeRock: access management across PingOne, PingFederate, PingAccess, and the ForgeRock Identity Platform, with SSO, MFA, identity orchestration, and directory services at enterprise scale.

vendorsHome.redEduCallout: Why these vendors? Because they are the platforms Rodolfo is authorized to teach through <link>Red Education</link>, a global award-winning authorized training partner: F5, Fortinet, Netskope, Extreme Networks, Ping Identity, and Zscaler.

glossary.title: Glossary
glossary.tagline: The field's terms, acronyms, expressions, jargon, and lore - defined, sourced, and linked to the tools that compute them.
glossary.disputedNote: A popular version of this story is inaccurate - see the note above.

the-hacker-manifesto.def: A short 1986 essay, written after its author's arrest, that became the defining statement of hacker curiosity.
the-hacker-manifesto.context: Published in Phrack under the handle The Mentor, it frames hacking as a hunger to understand rather than to harm, and its line about being judged for curiosity resonated widely. It remains the most quoted text in hacker culture, for better and worse.

the-gnu-manifesto.def: Richard Stallman's 1985 call to build a free Unix-compatible operating system that anyone could share and change.
the-gnu-manifesto.context: It launched the GNU Project and articulated the free-software ethic - that users should be free to run, study, modify, and redistribute software. The tools it produced, combined years later with the Linux kernel, became the operating system running much of the internet.

the-cathedral-and-the-bazaar.def: Eric Raymond's 1997 essay contrasting closed, top-down development with open, community-driven development.
the-cathedral-and-the-bazaar.context: The 'cathedral' is software built quietly by a small anointed group; the 'bazaar' is the noisy, open Linux model where anyone can contribute and releases are frequent. It popularized open-source development to a business audience and coined Linus's Law about many eyes making bugs shallow.

kevin-mitnick.def: The 1990s hacker whose social-engineering exploits made him the era's most famous computer intruder.
kevin-mitnick.context: Mitnick broke into corporate networks largely by manipulating people rather than machines, became a fugitive, and was arrested in 1995 after a high-profile hunt. He later reinvented himself as a security consultant and author, making 'the human is the weakest link' a lasting lesson.

the-414s.def: A group of Milwaukee teenagers who broke into dozens of computer systems in 1983 and put hacking on front pages.
the-414s.context: Named after their area code, they accessed systems including a cancer center and Los Alamos National Laboratory, mostly through default and guessed passwords. Their case helped spur the first US computer-crime laws and fixed the young 'hacker' in the public imagination.

log4shell.def: The 2021 vulnerability in the ubiquitous Log4j logging library that let a logged string execute arbitrary code.
log4shell.context: Because Log4j is embedded in countless Java applications, a crafted string in something as innocent as a username or user agent could trigger remote code execution almost anywhere. Its reach and severity made it one of the most serious and widespread flaws ever disclosed.

heartbleed.def: The 2014 OpenSSL flaw that let attackers read chunks of a server's memory, including keys and passwords.
heartbleed.context: A missing bounds check in the TLS heartbeat extension let a client ask for more data than it sent and receive whatever happened to be adjacent in memory. It was one of the first bugs with a logo and a name, and it forced a global scramble to patch servers and rotate secrets.

shellshock.def: A 2014 family of Bash vulnerabilities that let attackers run commands through crafted environment variables.
shellshock.context: Bash mistakenly executed code trailing a function definition passed in an environment variable, and because web servers and other services set such variables from user input, this became remote code execution. Bash is so pervasive across Unix systems that the exposure was enormous.

stuxnet.def: The 2010 worm that sabotaged Iranian nuclear centrifuges, the first known cyberweapon to cause physical damage.
stuxnet.context: Stuxnet spread widely but activated only on specific Siemens industrial controllers, subtly altering centrifuge speeds while reporting normal readings to operators. Its use of multiple zero-days and its physical target marked the arrival of state-level cyber-sabotage.

wannacry.def: The 2017 ransomware worm that spread through a leaked Windows exploit and crippled systems worldwide.
wannacry.context: It used the EternalBlue SMB exploit to self-propagate, encrypting files and demanding Bitcoin, and hit hospitals, factories, and rail networks within hours. A researcher's accidental discovery of a kill-switch domain slowed it, but not before it caused billions in damage.

the-iloveyou-virus.def: The 2000 email worm whose 'ILOVEYOU' subject line tricked millions into infecting their own machines.
the-iloveyou-virus.context: Opening the attached 'love letter' ran a script that overwrote files and mailed itself to the victim's entire address book, spreading around the globe in hours. Its social hook, not any technical brilliance, is what made it one of the most damaging worms of its time.

the-turing-test.def: Alan Turing's 1950 proposal to judge machine intelligence by whether it can converse indistinguishably from a human.
the-turing-test.context: Rather than ask 'can machines think', Turing reframed it as an imitation game: if a human judge cannot reliably tell the machine from a person by text alone, the question is answered pragmatically. It set the terms for decades of debate about artificial intelligence.

ada-lovelace.def: The 19th-century mathematician often called the first programmer for her notes on Babbage's Analytical Engine.
ada-lovelace.context: In 1843 she translated a paper on Charles Babbage's proposed machine and added notes far longer than the original, including what is often described as the first published algorithm intended for a computer. She also grasped that such a machine could manipulate symbols, not just numbers - an insight ahead of its time.

the-eniac-programmers.def: The six women who programmed the ENIAC in the 1940s, largely uncredited for decades.
the-eniac-programmers.context: Working without programming languages or manuals, they configured the room-sized machine by physically rewiring it and setting switches, effectively inventing hands-on programming as they went. Their contribution went unrecognized for years, and their story reshaped the history of who built early computing.

the-streisand-effect.def: When an attempt to hide or suppress information ends up drawing far more attention to it.
the-streisand-effect.context: It is named for a 2003 lawsuit in which Barbra Streisand tried to remove an aerial photo of her home from an obscure coastal survey, which then went viral because of the suit. On the internet, trying to bury something is often the surest way to spread it.

moores-law.def: The observation that the number of transistors on a chip roughly doubles every couple of years.
moores-law.context: Gordon Moore noted the trend in 1965, and it held for decades as a self-fulfilling roadmap the whole industry planned around. It is slowing as physics intrudes, but it remains the reference point for talking about the pace of computing progress.

brooks-law.def: The principle that adding people to a late software project makes it later.
brooks-law.context: New team members need ramp-up and training, and every added person multiplies the communication paths, so late-stage staffing often slows things down instead of speeding them up. Fred Brooks drew it from managing IBM's OS/360, and it still punctures the myth that effort is fungible.

conways-law.def: The observation that systems end up mirroring the communication structure of the organizations that build them.
conways-law.context: If four teams build a compiler, you tend to get a four-pass compiler, because the software's seams form where the teams' conversations do. The modern corollary, the 'inverse Conway maneuver', is to shape teams deliberately so the desired architecture emerges.

hofstadters-law.def: The self-referential rule that things always take longer than you expect, even accounting for this rule.
hofstadters-law.context: Its recursive twist captures why software estimates are chronically optimistic: even when you pad the estimate, the unknowns you did not imagine still find you. It is quoted, ruefully, in nearly every planning meeting that has ever slipped.

hanlons-razor.def: The heuristic to never attribute to malice what is adequately explained by stupidity or mistake.
hanlons-razor.context: When something goes wrong, an honest error or oversight is usually more likely than a deliberate plot, and assuming malice poisons collaboration. In incident reviews it pairs naturally with blameless culture: look for the broken process, not the villain.

cunninghams-law.def: The observation that the best way to get a right answer online is to post a wrong one.
cunninghams-law.context: People who will not answer a question will rush to correct a mistake, so a confidently wrong post often draws the correction a plain question would not. It is named for Ward Cunningham, who created the wiki, and it explains a lot of internet comment threads.

godwins-law.def: The adage that as an online argument grows longer, a comparison to Nazis becomes inevitable.
godwins-law.context: Mike Godwin coined it in 1990 to push back on cheapened historical analogies, and by convention whoever first reaches for the comparison is taken to have lost the thread. It has become shorthand for the way heated threads decay.

leaky-abstraction.def: An abstraction that is supposed to hide complexity but lets details of what is underneath show through.
leaky-abstraction.context: TCP promises reliable delivery, but a cut cable still surfaces as a hang; an ORM hides SQL until a slow query forces you to think in SQL again. Joel Spolsky's 'law' is that all non-trivial abstractions leak to some degree, so you can never fully stop understanding the layer below.

golden-hammer.def: Over-relying on one familiar tool for every problem, whether or not it fits.
golden-hammer.context: From 'if all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail', it describes reaching for your favorite language, framework, or pattern reflexively instead of choosing what the problem needs. The cure is a bigger toolbox and the judgment to know when the hammer is wrong.

north-star.def: The single measure a team agrees best captures the value it delivers, used to align everyone's work.
north-star.context: Rather than chase a dozen dashboards, a team picks one guiding metric - active users, jobs completed, time to value - and steers by it. Chosen well it focuses effort; chosen badly it becomes a number people game while the real goal drifts.

mandelbug.def: A bug whose causes are so complex and tangled that its behavior looks chaotic or nondeterministic.
mandelbug.context: Named after the infinitely intricate Mandelbrot set, it is the bug you cannot pin down because it seems to depend on everything at once. It sits in the family with the heisenbug, which vanishes when observed, and the bohrbug, which is boringly reproducible.

schroedinbug.def: A bug that only manifests after someone reads the code and realizes it should never have worked.
schroedinbug.context: The joke, riffing on Schrodinger's cat, is that the program ran fine until an engineer noticed it logically could not, at which point it promptly broke. It captures the eerie feeling that some code was only working because nobody was looking too hard.

crud.def: The four basic operations of persistent storage: create, read, update, and delete.
crud.context: Almost every data-backed application is, at bottom, a way to CRUD records, and REST verbs and SQL statements map onto these four actions. A 'CRUD app' is shorthand for straightforward software that mostly moves records in and out of a database.

acid.def: The four guarantees a reliable database transaction should provide.
acid.context: Atomicity means all-or-nothing, consistency keeps the data valid, isolation keeps concurrent transactions from corrupting each other, and durability means committed data survives a crash. ACID is the classic contract of relational databases, contrasted with the looser BASE model many distributed stores adopt.

the-blue-box.def: The tone-generating box the young Steve Wozniak built, and sold with Steve Jobs, to make free long-distance calls.
the-blue-box.context: Inspired by the 1971 Esquire piece on phreaking, Wozniak engineered a digital blue box that emitted the control tones phone switches trusted, and the pair sold them around Berkeley. Jobs later said that without the blue box there would have been no Apple - it proved two kids could build something the phone company could not stop.

the-first-webpage.def: The plain page Tim Berners-Lee published at CERN in 1991 to explain the World Wide Web itself.
the-first-webpage.context: Served from a NeXT machine at info.cern.ch, it described what the web was, how to make a browser, and how to set up a server - the web documenting its own instructions. CERN restored the page at its original address, so the first thing the web ever said is still online.

halt-and-catch-fire.def: A jokingly named machine instruction said to make a CPU seize up - and, on some chips, a real undocumented opcode that does.
halt-and-catch-fire.context: HCF began as programmer humor for an instruction that would do something catastrophic like set the processor on fire, but the Motorola 6800 really had undocumented opcodes that locked the CPU into a runaway read cycle, and the nickname stuck to them. The name is folklore; the lock-up behavior on those chips is real.

the-konami-code.def: The button sequence up up down down left right left right B A, a cheat that became a cultural in-joke.
the-konami-code.context: Kazuhisa Hashimoto added it while porting Gradius to the NES in 1986 because he found the game too hard to test, and it later became famous through Contra. Decades on, it is still hidden as an Easter egg in countless websites and apps that reward anyone who types it.

all-your-base.def: A garbled line from a badly translated 1991 video game that became one of the first viral internet memes.
all-your-base.context: The Mega Drive port of Zero Wing opened with 'All your base are belong to us', a mistranslation so awkward it became beloved, exploding across forums and image edits around 2000 and 2001. It is a landmark in early meme culture and a standing joke about machine and rushed translation.

the-y2k-bug.def: The fear that systems storing years as two digits would misread 2000 as 1900 at the century rollover.
the-y2k-bug.context: Decades of code and data used two-digit years to save space, so '00' was ambiguous, prompting a massive global remediation effort in the late 1990s. The rollover passed with few incidents - which sparked a lasting debate over whether the fix was overkill or precisely why nothing broke.

the-year-2038-problem.def: The moment in 2038 when systems counting seconds in a signed 32-bit integer will overflow and wrap to 1901.
the-year-2038-problem.context: Unix time is seconds since 1970, and a signed 32-bit counter runs out at 03:14:07 UTC on 19 January 2038, flipping to a negative value read as December 1901. Modern systems use 64-bit time, but embedded and legacy devices that still use 32 bits are the Y2K of the next generation.

the-pentium-fdiv-bug.def: A 1994 flaw in the Intel Pentium that returned slightly wrong answers for certain floating-point divisions.
the-pentium-fdiv-bug.context: Mathematician Thomas Nicely spotted the error in a lookup table, and Intel's initial offer to replace chips only for those who could prove they were affected caused a public-relations disaster. Intel reversed course, took a charge of roughly 475 million dollars, and the episode reset how the industry handles hardware errata.

the-therac-25.def: A 1980s radiation-therapy machine whose software defects delivered massive overdoses to patients.
