bados.def: A defense that detects denial-of-service by learning normal traffic behavior.
bados.context: Behavioral Denial of Service protection builds a baseline of normal traffic and mitigates deviations automatically, catching application-layer attacks that signature-based defenses miss.

c3d.def: An F5 feature that re-signs client certificates for backend mutual TLS.
c3d.context: Client Certificate Constrained Delegation lets BIG-IP terminate a client's TLS and then present a freshly generated certificate representing that client to the backend, preserving identity across a re-encryption boundary.

ehf.def: An out-of-band fix F5 issues for a specific urgent problem.
ehf.context: An Engineering Hotfix is a targeted patch delivered outside the normal release cadence to address a specific defect, later rolled into a maintenance release.

df.def: An F5 hardware designation for a dual-fan platform variant.
df.context: DF marks a dual-fan variant of an F5 appliance - a platform-specific hardware detail relevant when checking which models a software release supports.

ja3.def: A fingerprint of a TLS client built from the fields in its ClientHello.
ja3.context: JA3 hashes a set of ClientHello fields - TLS version, cipher list, extensions - into a short signature (an MD5 hash) that often identifies the client software or malware family, regardless of IP. JA4 is its more robust successor.

ja4.def: A newer, more robust family of TLS and network fingerprints.
ja4.context: JA4 (part of the JA4+ suite) improves on JA3 with a more structured, harder-to-evade fingerprint of the ClientHello, and adds companion fingerprints for other protocols. It resists the ordering tricks that let clients dodge JA3.

spbm.def: An Ethernet fabric technology that builds shortest-path forwarding using IS-IS.
spbm.context: Shortest Path Bridging MAC mode (IEEE 802.1aq) creates a loop-free, shortest-path Ethernet fabric controlled by the IS-IS routing protocol, replacing spanning tree with true multipath forwarding across a provider or campus backbone.

is-is.def: A link-state routing protocol, used in large networks and Ethernet fabrics.
is-is.context: Intermediate System to Intermediate System (ISO/IEC 10589) is a link-state interior gateway protocol, a peer to OSPF, favored in large service-provider cores and used as the control plane for SPBM fabrics.

exos.context: Extreme Operating System (ExtremeXOS) is the modular network OS that runs on Extreme Networks switches, with a process-isolated architecture and a scripting/automation surface for configuration and management.

rip.def: One of the oldest routing protocols, using hop count as its metric.
rip.context: Routing Information Protocol (RFC 2453) picks routes by fewest hops, capped at 15, which makes it simple but unsuitable for large networks. It survives mostly in small or legacy deployments, long superseded by OSPF and BGP.

beb.def: An edge device in an Ethernet fabric that encapsulates traffic into the backbone.
beb.context: A Backbone Edge Bridge (IEEE 802.1ah) sits at the boundary of a provider-backbone or SPBM fabric, wrapping customer frames in backbone MAC headers so the core forwards on backbone addresses - the entry and exit points of the fabric.

set-us-up-the-bomb.def: Another garbled line from the same 1991 game's opening as "all your base", now shorthand for a system suddenly and catastrophically compromised.
set-us-up-the-bomb.context: From Zero Wing's notoriously mistranslated intro (the captain is told "Somebody set up us the bomb"), the phrase spread with the "all your base are belong to us" meme in the early 2000s. In ops culture it is affectionate shorthand for the moment an incident begins - the alarm that says something has just gone very wrong.

oidc.def: An identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 for authenticating users.
oidc.context: OpenID Connect adds authentication to the OAuth 2.0 authorization framework, issuing signed ID tokens (JWTs) so an application can verify who a user is. It is the modern successor to much of what SAML did for web single sign-on.

jwk.def: A JSON format for representing a cryptographic key.
jwk.context: JSON Web Key (RFC 7517) encodes a public or private key as a JSON object, the format signing keys are published in for JWT verification. A collection of them is a JWK Set (JWKS).

jwks.def: A published set of keys used to verify signed tokens.
jwks.context: JSON Web Key Set is a JSON document listing the public keys (as JWKs) a service uses to sign its tokens. Relying parties fetch the JWKS to verify JWT signatures and to follow key rotation without manual reconfiguration.

pss.def: A randomized RSA signature scheme, more robust than the older deterministic padding.
pss.context: Probabilistic Signature Scheme (RSASSA-PSS, RFC 8017) adds randomized salt to RSA signatures, giving stronger security proofs than the legacy PKCS#1 v1.5 padding. It is the recommended RSA signature padding in modern protocols.

hqc.def: A code-based post-quantum key-encapsulation algorithm selected by NIST as a backup.
hqc.context: Hamming Quasi-Cyclic is a code-based key-encapsulation mechanism NIST selected in 2025 as a backup to the lattice-based ML-KEM, so that post-quantum security does not rest on a single mathematical assumption.

ecdh.def: Elliptic-curve key exchange - the non-ephemeral form lacks forward secrecy.
ecdh.context: Elliptic-Curve Diffie-Hellman derives a shared secret over elliptic curves. Without the ephemeral 'E' (as in ECDHE) the keys are long-lived, so a leaked key exposes past sessions - which is why the ephemeral variant is preferred.

rs256.def: A JWT signing algorithm: RSA with SHA-256.
rs256.context: RS256 is a JSON Web Algorithm identifier (RFC 7518) for an RSA signature using SHA-256. It is one of the most common JWT signing algorithms, paired with a public key published in a JWKS.

es256.def: A JWT signing algorithm: ECDSA with SHA-256.
es256.context: ES256 is a JSON Web Algorithm identifier (RFC 7518) for an ECDSA signature over the P-256 curve using SHA-256. It offers the same security as RS256 with much smaller keys and signatures.

acme.def: A protocol that automates getting and renewing TLS certificates.
acme.context: Automatic Certificate Management Environment (RFC 8555) is the protocol behind Let's Encrypt and similar CAs. It lets a client prove domain control and obtain certificates without human interaction, which is why free, auto-renewing TLS became universal.

fqdn.def: A complete domain name that specifies a host's exact location in DNS.
fqdn.context: A Fully Qualified Domain Name gives the full path from host to root, like `www.example.com.`, leaving no ambiguity about which domain is meant - as opposed to a short or relative name that depends on a search suffix.

san.def: The certificate field listing all hostnames the certificate is valid for.
san.context: Subject Alternative Name (RFC 5280) is the X.509 extension that carries the domains a certificate covers. Modern browsers ignore the old Common Name and validate against the SAN list, so a certificate must name every hostname it serves there.

isrg.def: The nonprofit that runs Let's Encrypt.
isrg.context: Internet Security Research Group is the nonprofit behind Let's Encrypt, whose ISRG Root X1 certificate anchors the free, automated certificates that a large share of the web now uses.

ios.def: Cisco's long-standing operating system for routers and switches.
ios.context: Internetwork Operating System is Cisco's classic network OS and command-line style. Its conventions (`enable`, `configure terminal`) are so widespread that many other vendors offer an IOS-like CLI mode for familiarity. Not to be confused with Apple's iOS.

fa.def: A protocol that auto-attaches edge switches to an Ethernet fabric.
fa.context: Fabric Attach lets an edge switch join a fabric and signal VLAN-to-service mappings automatically over LLDP, so the edge does not have to run the fabric's control protocol (IS-IS/SPBM) itself.

sike.def: A post-quantum candidate that was dramatically broken in 2022.
sike.context: Supersingular Isogeny Key Encapsulation was a promising isogeny-based post-quantum scheme until a 2022 attack broke it on a laptop in about an hour - a cautionary tale in why the NIST process hedges across different mathematical families.

totp.def: A one-time code that changes every 30 seconds, used for two-factor auth.
totp.context: Time-based One-Time Password (RFC 6238) derives a short code from a shared secret and the current time using HMAC, so authenticator apps and servers compute the same value independently. It is the basis of most app-based 2FA.

hotp.def: A one-time code advanced by a counter rather than a clock.
hotp.context: HMAC-based One-Time Password (RFC 4226) derives codes from a shared secret and an incrementing counter. TOTP is HOTP with the counter replaced by time; HOTP itself is used where a counter is easier to synchronize than a clock.

qr.def: A 2D barcode that phones scan to read data quickly.
qr.context: Quick Response code (ISO/IEC 18004) is a matrix barcode that encodes text or URLs, widely used to provision authenticator apps by encoding the otpauth URI so a phone camera can enroll a secret in one scan.

dnssec.def: Extensions that let DNS answers be cryptographically verified.
dnssec.context: Domain Name System Security Extensions (RFC 4033) add signatures to DNS records so a resolver can prove an answer came from the real zone and was not tampered with in transit. It builds a chain of trust from the root down through each delegation.

dnskey.def: The DNS record that publishes a zone's public signing key.
dnskey.context: A DNSKEY record (RFC 4034) holds the public key a resolver uses to verify a zone's DNSSEC signatures. Its hash is published as a DS record in the parent zone, linking the chain of trust.

nsec.def: A DNSSEC record that proves a name does not exist.
nsec.context: Next Secure records (RFC 4034) chain the existing names in a zone so a resolver can prove authenticated denial - that a queried name genuinely is absent - rather than trusting an unsigned 'not found'.

nsec3.def: A hashed version of NSEC that hides a zone's contents.
nsec3.context: Next Secure version 3 (RFC 5155) provides the same authenticated denial as NSEC but over hashed names, so an attacker cannot walk the chain to enumerate every name in the zone.

tld.def: The last label of a domain name, like com or org.
tld.context: A top-level domain is the rightmost part of a domain, sitting directly under the DNS root. Registries operate each TLD, and the DS record at this level is where a DNSSEC chain of trust from the root continues downward.

pac.def: A JavaScript file that tells a browser which proxy to use per URL.
pac.context: A Proxy Auto-Config file defines a `FindProxyForURL` function the browser calls for each request, returning which proxy (if any) to use. It allows fine-grained, per-destination proxy rules without hardcoding a single proxy.

wpad.def: A method for browsers to find a PAC file automatically.
wpad.context: Web Proxy Auto-Discovery lets a browser locate a proxy config (PAC) file via DHCP or DNS without manual setup. Convenient but a classic attack surface, since a rogue WPAD response can redirect traffic through an attacker's proxy.

mime.def: A standard for labeling the type of a piece of content.
mime.context: Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions (RFC 2045) defined content types like `text/html` and `application/json`, originally for email attachments and now the media-type system the whole web uses to say what a byte stream is.

mdn.def: Mozilla's widely used web-technology documentation site.
mdn.context: Mozilla Developer Network is the reference documentation many developers treat as the canonical description of HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and HTTP behavior in browsers.

hs256.def: A JWT signing algorithm: HMAC with SHA-256.
hs256.context: HS256 is a JSON Web Algorithm identifier (RFC 7518) for a symmetric HMAC signature using SHA-256. Because signing and verifying use the same shared secret, mixing it up with an asymmetric algorithm like RS256 is the root of a well-known JWT attack.

kdf.def: A function that turns a secret into one or more cryptographic keys.
kdf.context: A key derivation function stretches or transforms input keying material into keys. Password-oriented KDFs (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2, PBKDF2) are deliberately slow and tunable so that guessing a password stays expensive.

der.def: A strict binary encoding for X.509 certificates and keys.
der.context: Distinguished Encoding Rules (ITU-T X.690) is a canonical binary form of ASN.1 structures. Certificate signatures are computed over the DER bytes, and PEM is just DER wrapped in Base64 with header lines.

crl.def: A published list of certificates that have been revoked.
crl.context: Certificate Revocation List (RFC 5280) is a signed list a CA publishes of certificates no longer valid before their expiry. A client may download the CRL, or use the lighter-weight OCSP, to check whether a certificate has been revoked.

pem.def: A Base64 text wrapper for certificates and keys, with BEGIN/END lines.
pem.context: Privacy-Enhanced Mail (RFC 7468) is the familiar `-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----` text format. Despite the mail-era name, it is now the standard way to store and paste DER-encoded certificates and keys as ASCII.

sid.def: An identifier that maps a service to a fabric-wide virtual network.
sid.context: In Shortest Path Bridging, a Service Identifier (I-SID) tags traffic so the fabric knows which virtual network a frame belongs to, decoupling the service from any specific VLAN and letting the edge signal membership without running the core protocol.

rpz.def: A DNS mechanism for rewriting or blocking answers by policy.
rpz.context: A Response Policy Zone lets a resolver override DNS answers - returning NXDOMAIN, a walled-garden address, or a custom record - for domains on a threat feed. It turns DNS into an enforcement point using ordinary zone-transfer machinery.

cert.def: A team that coordinates response to security incidents.
cert.context: A Computer Emergency Response Team (also CSIRT) handles and coordinates the response to cybersecurity incidents for an organization or nation. National CERTs publish threat feeds and advisories that downstream defenders consume.

pva.def: F5 hardware that accelerates simple Layer 4 traffic.
pva.context: Packet Velocity ASIC is BIG-IP's hardware fast path. In full or assisted mode it offloads established, simple flows to silicon, freeing the software TMM for connections that need Layer 7 processing.

icap.def: A protocol for handing HTTP content to an external service for adaptation.
icap.context: Internet Content Adaptation Protocol (RFC 3507) lets a proxy pass request or response bodies to a separate server - for antivirus scanning, DLP, or content rewriting - and splice the result back into the flow.

eui.def: An IEEE identifier format used to build IPv6 interface IDs.
eui.context: Extended Unique Identifier, in its EUI-64 form, derives a 64-bit interface identifier from a device's MAC address. IPv6 SLAAC historically used it to auto-generate the host part of an address.

dad.def: An IPv6 check that a chosen address is not already in use.
dad.context: Duplicate Address Detection (RFC 4862) has a node send a neighbor solicitation for its own tentative address before using it; if anyone answers, the address is a duplicate and cannot be claimed.

ldns.def: The recursive resolver a client actually queries.
ldns.context: The local DNS resolver is the recursive server a stub client sends queries to, and whose IP a service like GTM/DNS sees, not the end user's. Because the LDNS location approximates the user's, it drives geographic load-balancing decisions.

isp.def: A company that provides internet access.
isp.context: An internet service provider connects subscribers to the internet. In geographic DNS and topology decisions, the resolving network's ISP can be one of the attributes used to steer a client toward a nearby data center.

bind.def: The most widely deployed DNS server software.
bind.context: Berkeley Internet Name Domain is the reference DNS server from ISC. On BIG-IP DNS, a 'fall through to BIND' action hands a query to the local BIND instance when no wide IP or other object answers it.

ips.def: A control that detects and blocks attacks inline.
ips.context: An intrusion prevention system inspects traffic in the data path and can drop or reset connections matching known attack signatures or anomalies, sitting inline unlike a passive detection-only sensor.

wad.def: The FortiGate process that handles proxy-based inspection.
wad.context: The Web Application Daemon is FortiGate's user-space proxy process. In proxy-based SSL inspection and web filtering, WAD terminates and re-originates the session so security profiles can see the full content.

ntlm.def: A legacy Microsoft challenge-response authentication protocol.
ntlm.context: NT LAN Manager authenticates a user via a challenge-response exchange without sending the password. Still seen in Windows environments, it is weaker than Kerberos and a frequent relay-attack target.

spn.def: The name a Kerberos client uses to request a service ticket.
spn.context: A Service Principal Name uniquely identifies a service instance in Kerberos, tied to the account running it. A misregistered SPN is a classic cause of single sign-on failures.

esp.def: The IPsec protocol that encrypts and authenticates packet payloads.
esp.context: Encapsulating Security Payload (RFC 4303) is the IPsec component that provides confidentiality and integrity for tunneled traffic. A capture taken outside the tunnel sees only ESP, not the protected inner packets.

vip.def: The address clients connect to on a load balancer.
vip.context: A virtual IP is the front-door address a load balancer or virtual server listens on. Traffic completing at the VIP but never reaching a pool member points to a problem on the server-side of the proxy.

first.def: The global body of incident-response teams behind CVSS and EPSS.
first.context: The Forum of Incident Response and Security Teams is the international association that maintains standards like CVSS and EPSS. Its work gives defenders a common language for scoring and prioritizing vulnerabilities.

epss.def: A data-driven score estimating the odds a vulnerability gets exploited.
epss.context: Exploit Prediction Scoring System estimates the probability that a given vulnerability will be exploited in the wild in the near term. It complements CVSS severity by adding a likelihood dimension to prioritization.

pkce.def: An OAuth extension that protects the authorization-code flow.
pkce.context: Proof Key for Code Exchange (RFC 7636) binds an authorization code to the client that requested it, using a one-time secret. It stops an intercepted code from being redeemed by an attacker and is now standard for public OAuth clients.

eigrp.def: A Cisco routing protocol known for fast convergence.
eigrp.context: Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol is a Cisco distance-vector protocol with fast reconvergence via its DUAL algorithm. It can summarize routes on nearly any interface, which is why it appears in route-summarization discussions.

as.def: A network under one administrative routing policy, identified by an ASN.
as.context: An autonomous system is a collection of IP prefixes under a single routing policy, identified by an autonomous system number. BGP exchanges reachability between autonomous systems, and AS boundaries are natural places to summarize routes.

ca.def: The trusted issuer that signs digital certificates.
ca.context: A certificate authority is the entity whose signature makes a TLS certificate trusted. Browsers ship a set of trusted CAs; a certificate is valid only if it chains to one of them, and the CA is also who revokes certificates via CRL or OCSP.

pin.def: A short numeric secret used to authenticate a person.
pin.context: A personal identification number is a short numeric code used to prove identity, as on a bank card. Being short, a PIN has a tiny keyspace, so it relies on limited attempts or a hardware secure element rather than resistance to offline guessing.

otp.def: A password valid for a single login or a short window.
otp.context: A one-time password is used once and then invalid, defeating replay of a captured code. TOTP (time-based) and HOTP (counter-based) are the two common ways to generate them for two-factor authentication.

acs.def: The endpoint where a SAML response is delivered to the application.
acs.context: The Assertion Consumer Service is the URL at the service provider that receives and processes a SAML assertion from the identity provider. A response must be sent to the exact registered ACS, which is part of what stops assertion redirection attacks.

dcv.def: Proving you control a domain before a certificate is issued.
dcv.context: Domain Control Validation is how a CA confirms the requester controls the domain, via an HTTP, DNS, or email challenge. Baseline Requirements keep shrinking how long a DCV result can be reused, tightening certificate issuance.

csr.def: A signed request containing the public key and details for a certificate.
csr.context: A Certificate Signing Request (PKCS #10) bundles the public key and subject information, signed by the requester's private key, and is submitted to a CA to obtain a certificate. ACME automates what once meant pasting a CSR into a web form.

adc.def: A device that load-balances and optimizes application traffic.
adc.context: An application delivery controller sits in front of servers to distribute load, terminate TLS, and apply health checks and traffic policies. BIG-IP LTM is a canonical ADC; an AS3 'ADC-only' declaration configures just those functions.

sii.def: Reusable subscriber-identity data in the certificate issuance process.
sii.context: Subscriber Identity Information refers to previously validated details about a certificate subscriber that a CA may reuse for a limited time. Like DCV, the window for reusing SII has been tightened by industry baseline rules.

xxe.def: An attack that abuses XML external entities to read files or reach internal systems.
xxe.context: XML External Entity injection tricks an XML parser into resolving an external entity, letting an attacker read local files, reach internal services (SSRF), or cause denial of service. The fix is to disable DOCTYPE/external-entity processing in the parser.

posix.def: A standard defining a portable interface for Unix-like operating systems.
posix.context: Portable Operating System Interface (IEEE 1003.1) specifies APIs, shells, and utilities so software written to it runs across Unix-like systems. Its definition of a day as exactly 86,400 seconds is why POSIX time ignores leap seconds.

tai.def: A continuous atomic timescale with no leap seconds.
tai.context: International Atomic Time is a uniform timescale kept by atomic clocks that never inserts leap seconds. UTC is derived from TAI but adds leap seconds to stay aligned with the Earth's rotation, so the two drift apart by whole seconds.

icann.def: The body that coordinates the internet's domain-name and number systems.
icann.context: Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers oversees the DNS root, top-level domain policy, and IP-number allocation coordination. In the Public Suffix List, the ICANN section holds registry-controlled suffixes as opposed to privately submitted ones.

si.def: The modern metric system of measurement units.
si.context: The International System of Units defines the second and other base units. A true SI second is the physical unit against which timescales like TAI are measured, distinct from the calendar-day accounting that POSIX time uses.

utc.def: The primary civil time standard the world sets clocks by.
utc.context: Coordinated Universal Time is the basis for civil time worldwide. It tracks atomic time (TAI) but inserts occasional leap seconds to stay within a second of solar time, and time-zone offsets are expressed relative to it.

iana.def: The function that manages global IP, port, and protocol-number assignments.
iana.context: Internet Assigned Numbers Authority maintains the registries of IP address blocks, port numbers, protocol parameters, and the DNS root zone, and publishes the time-zone database that most systems rely on.

ce.def: A node that connects a customer site into F5 Distributed Cloud.
ce.context: A Customer Edge node is the software appliance that registers into F5 Distributed Cloud and terminates traffic at the customer's site or cloud. On startup it must reach specific control endpoints, DNS, and NTP, which is why its egress allowlist matters.

opt.def: A pseudo-record that carries EDNS options in a DNS message.
opt.context: The OPT record (RFC 6891) is not a real resource record but a container in the additional section that carries EDNS metadata like buffer size and flags. dig shows it as the OPT pseudo-section.

edns.def: An extension that adds capabilities to the original DNS message format.
edns.context: Extension Mechanisms for DNS (RFC 6891) let DNS carry larger responses, signal DNSSEC support, and pass options, all via the OPT pseudo-record, without breaking the legacy message format.

sirt.def: F5's team that handles security incidents and advisories.
sirt.context: The Security Incident Response Team at F5 investigates and coordinates responses to security issues in F5 products, publishing advisories and responding to reports, often in the same week a vulnerability is disclosed.

adsp.def: F5's unified platform branding for application delivery and security.
adsp.context: Application Delivery and Security Platform is F5's umbrella name for its converged delivery and security offering. It signals the direction customers are pointed toward when planning around F5's evolving product portfolio.

ad.def: Microsoft's directory service for identity and authentication.
ad.context: Active Directory stores users, groups, and computers and authenticates them in Windows environments, typically via Kerberos or NTLM. Access policies often query an AD agent to resolve who a user is and what they may reach.

l4.def: The transport layer, handling ports and connections like TCP/UDP.
l4.context: Layer 4 of the OSI model is the transport layer, where TCP and UDP live. L4 load balancing forwards based on IP and port without inspecting application content, which is faster but blind to Layer 7 details like hostnames or URLs.

yaml.def: A human-readable data format often used for configuration.
yaml.context: YAML Ain't Markup Language is a whitespace-based serialization format popular for config files and CI pipelines. Its readability comes with sharp edges, like type-coercion surprises and significant indentation.

jws.def: A standard for signing data represented as JSON.
jws.context: JSON Web Signature (RFC 7515) defines how to sign a payload and encode the signature compactly. It underlies signed JWTs; notably its 'none' algorithm, meant for unsecured tokens, is a classic source of verification bugs.

pdf.context: Portable Document Format (ISO 32000) preserves a document's exact layout across devices. In security contexts, server-side PDF or screenshot generators are a common SSRF vector because they fetch URLs.

bsd.def: A Unix lineage; also the name of the original syslog format.
bsd.context: Berkeley Software Distribution is a family of Unix systems from UC Berkeley. In logging, 'BSD syslog' (RFC 3164) refers to the original, loosely specified message format that RFC 5424 later replaced.

pri.def: The leading field in a syslog message encoding facility and severity.
pri.context: The priority value is the number in angle brackets at the start of a syslog line. It packs the facility and severity into one integer, from which both can be recovered.

y2038.def: The moment 32-bit Unix time overflows, in January 2038.
y2038.context: The Year 2038 problem is when a signed 32-bit time_t counter, measuring seconds since 1970, overflows and wraps to a negative number. Unlike Y2K's formatting issue, this is a hard representational limit fixed by moving to 64-bit time.

snat.def: Rewriting the client source address as traffic passes through a device.
snat.context: Source network address translation makes connections appear to originate from the device's address rather than the client's. On BIG-IP it ensures return traffic comes back through the proxy, at the cost of hiding the original client IP from the server.

msrdp.def: Microsoft's protocol for remote graphical desktop sessions.
msrdp.context: Microsoft Remote Desktop Protocol carries remote desktop sessions. MSRDP persistence on a load balancer uses the session's routing token to keep a reconnecting client on the same backend server.

wccp.def: A protocol for transparently redirecting traffic to a cache or proxy.
wccp.context: Web Cache Communication Protocol lets a router redirect selected traffic to a cache or security appliance without the client being configured for it. It is one way transparent proxy interception is set up.

hsts.def: A header that forces browsers to use HTTPS for a site.
hsts.context: HTTP Strict Transport Security (RFC 6797) tells a browser to only ever connect to a site over HTTPS, refusing plaintext and blocking click-through on certificate warnings. It defends against downgrade and stripping attacks.

lb.def: A device or service that spreads traffic across multiple servers.
lb.context: A load balancer distributes client requests across a pool of backends for scale and availability. In F5 Distributed Cloud the load balancer object also anchors routing, security, and origin-pool configuration.

l3.def: The network layer, where IP routing happens.
l3.context: Layer 3 of the OSI model is the network layer, home to IP addressing and routing. An L3 proxy or forwarding decision operates on IP addresses, one layer below the transport (L4) and application (L7) layers.

asn.def: A number identifying a network under one routing policy.
asn.context: An autonomous system number uniquely identifies an autonomous system in BGP. Policies can match on a specific ASN to permit or deny routes from a given network, such as blocking a known-bad provider.

rng.def: A source of random values, ideally unpredictable.
rng.context: A random number generator produces the randomness that security depends on. A weak or predictable RNG, not a flaw in a scheme like UUIDv4, is usually the real cause when supposedly unique or secret values collide or are guessed.

ulid.def: A sortable, unique identifier alternative to UUID.
ulid.context: Universally Unique Lexicographically Sortable Identifier encodes a timestamp plus randomness so IDs sort roughly by creation time while staying unique. It was a popular answer to UUIDv4's lack of ordering before UUIDv7 arrived.

ecma.def: A standards body behind JavaScript and JSON specifications.
ecma.context: European Computer Manufacturers Association, now Ecma International, publishes standards including ECMAScript (JavaScript) and ECMA-404, one of the two specifications that define JSON.

cdata.def: An XML section whose content is not parsed as markup.
cdata.context: A character data section lets an XML document include raw text - like code or markup - without the parser interpreting angle brackets and ampersands. It is written as a CDATA block so special characters need no escaping.

lcs.def: The longest sequence common to two inputs, keeping order.
lcs.context: The longest common subsequence is the basis of most diff tools: the longest ordered (but not necessarily contiguous) run of elements shared by two files. What is left over becomes the additions and deletions a diff shows.

nvd.def: The US government's public database of known vulnerabilities.
nvd.context: The National Vulnerability Database, run by NIST, enriches CVE entries with CVSS scores and other metadata. Reproducing its published score for a vulnerability is a good check that a CVSS calculation matches the authoritative source.

ajax.def: A technique for updating a web page without a full reload.
ajax.context: Asynchronous JavaScript and XML refers to making background HTTP requests from a page and updating it in place. The name predates JSON, which has largely replaced XML as the payload, but the term stuck for in-page dynamic requests.

sec1.def: A standard format for encoding elliptic-curve private keys.
sec1.context: Standards for Efficient Cryptography 1 defines an elliptic-curve key encoding, distinct from the PKCS#1 (RSA) and PKCS#8 (generic) formats. A private key file may be in any of these, wrapped in either PEM or DER.

captcha.def: A challenge meant to tell humans and bots apart.
captcha.context: Completely Automated Public Turing test to tell Computers and Humans Apart is a challenge, like distorted text or image selection, that is easy for people and hard for bots. It is a last-resort defense because it adds real user friction.

gui.def: A visual, point-and-click interface to software.
gui.context: A graphical user interface lets a user operate software through windows, buttons, and menus rather than typed commands. On BIG-IP some tasks are only practical through the GUI, while others are faster from the CLI.

dh.def: A method for two parties to agree on a shared secret.
dh.context: Diffie-Hellman is a key-exchange method letting two sides derive a shared secret over a public channel. Its ephemeral form gives forward secrecy; the parameters, including the DH group, determine the strength of the exchange.

iis.def: Microsoft's web server software for Windows.
iis.context: Internet Information Services is the web server built into Windows Server. Its URL and path handling quirks, like accepting backslashes as separators, are a common source of WAF evasion tricks that attackers use to slip past signature matching.

beast.def: A 2011 attack decrypting TLS 1.0 traffic by exploiting predictable CBC initialization vectors.
beast.context: Browser Exploit Against SSL/TLS chained a chosen-plaintext trick with CBC's predictable IVs to recover secrets like cookies. It pushed the industry toward TLS 1.1+ and AEAD ciphers, and is why RC4 was briefly favored before its own weaknesses surfaced.

poodle.def: A 2014 attack that forces a downgrade to SSL 3.0 to exploit its broken CBC padding.
poodle.context: Padding Oracle On Downgraded Legacy Encryption abused the fact that a client would fall back to SSL 3.0, whose padding could be probed byte by byte to recover data. It effectively ended SSL 3.0 in the field.

crime.def: A 2012 attack recovering secrets by observing the size of compressed TLS requests.
crime.context: Compression Ratio Info-leak Made Easy exploited TLS-level compression: injecting guesses and watching the compressed length reveal when a guess matched secret content like a session cookie. The fix was to disable TLS compression.

breach.def: A 2013 variant of CRIME that targets HTTP response compression instead of TLS.
breach.context: Browser Reconnaissance and Exfiltration via Adaptive Compression of Hypertext works even without TLS compression by attacking gzip at the HTTP layer, inferring secrets reflected in compressed responses. Mitigations include masking tokens and disabling reflection of user input.

grease.def: A mechanism that sends random dummy values to keep TLS implementations tolerant of unknown extensions.
grease.context: Generate Random Extensions And Sustain Extensibility (RFC 8701) deliberately injects reserved, meaningless values into TLS handshakes so that servers and middleboxes learn to ignore what they do not recognize, preventing future extensions from breaking. It also shows up as a distinctive marker in TLS fingerprints.

logjam.def: A 2015 attack downgrading Diffie-Hellman key exchange to weak 512-bit export parameters.
logjam.context: Logjam tricked servers into using export-grade DH groups small enough to break, then computed the shared secret. It revealed how many servers reused the same well-known primes, making precomputation attacks practical.

freak.def: A 2015 attack forcing servers to use weak, factorable export-grade RSA keys.
freak.context: Factoring RSA Export Keys exploited leftover 1990s export ciphers: an attacker could force a downgrade to 512-bit RSA, factor the key, and impersonate the server. It was a direct legacy of old US crypto export restrictions.

drown.def: A 2016 attack that breaks TLS by exploiting a server's support for obsolete SSLv2.
drown.context: Decrypting RSA with Obsolete and Weakened eNcryption used a still-enabled SSLv2 endpoint sharing the same RSA key as a cross-protocol oracle to decrypt modern TLS sessions. The lesson was to fully disable SSLv2 everywhere a key is used.

lucky13.def: A 2013 timing attack against CBC padding in TLS.
lucky13.context: Lucky Thirteen measured tiny timing differences in how TLS processed CBC padding to recover plaintext byte by byte. It reinforced the case for moving away from CBC mode toward AEAD ciphers.

sweet32.def: A 2016 attack exploiting birthday bounds on 64-bit block ciphers like 3DES.
sweet32.context: Sweet32 showed that ciphers with a 64-bit block, such as 3DES and Blowfish, leak data once enough traffic is encrypted under one key, due to block collisions. It drove deprecation of 3DES in TLS.

robot.def: A 2017 return of a classic RSA padding oracle affecting many TLS stacks.
robot.context: Return Of Bleichenbacher's Oracle Threat showed that a 1998 RSA PKCS#1 v1.5 padding attack still worked against numerous modern implementations. It underscored how hard it is to fully retire a flawed cryptographic construction.

raccoon-attack.def: A 2020 timing attack on TLS-DH that can recover the session key in rare cases.
raccoon-attack.context: The Raccoon attack exploited how TLS derived the premaster secret from Diffie-Hellman, where leading zero bytes and timing could leak enough to reconstruct the key. It is another reason ephemeral DH parameters and constant-time code matter.

spectre.def: A 2018 class of attacks abusing CPU speculative execution to leak memory.
spectre.context: Spectre tricks a processor into speculatively accessing data it should not, then reads it through cache side channels. It affected nearly all modern CPUs and forced deep changes in browsers, operating systems, and hardware design.

meltdown.def: A 2018 attack letting user programs read protected kernel memory via speculative execution.
meltdown.context: Meltdown broke the isolation between user space and the kernel on affected CPUs, allowing arbitrary kernel memory to be read. The main mitigation, kernel page-table isolation, carried a measurable performance cost.

roca.def: A 2017 flaw in RSA key generation in certain chips that made keys factorable.
roca.context: Return of Coppersmith's Attack exploited a weak RSA keygen routine in a widely used cryptographic library, so affected public keys could be factored. It forced reissuance of many smartcards, TPMs, and identity certificates.

sloth.def: A 2016 set of attacks abusing obsolete hashes like MD5 in TLS negotiation.
sloth.context: Security Losses from Obsolete and Truncated Transcript Hashes showed that allowing weak hashes such as MD5 in TLS signatures enabled transcript-collision and downgrade attacks. It pushed removal of MD5-based signatures from TLS.

terrapin.def: A 2023 attack that can weaken or truncate the SSH handshake.
terrapin.context: Terrapin manipulated sequence numbers during the SSH handshake to strip messages and downgrade security in specific configurations. It prompted updates to OpenSSH and other SSH implementations.

alpaca.def: A 2021 cross-protocol attack that confuses TLS services sharing a certificate.
alpaca.context: Application Layer Protocol Confusion Analyzing Cross-protocol Attacks redirected a TLS connection meant for one service to another that shared the certificate, exploiting mismatched application-layer expectations. Strict SNI and ALPN checking mitigate it.

krack.def: A 2017 attack on WPA2 Wi-Fi that resets encryption keys to replay traffic.
krack.context: Key Reinstallation Attack abused the WPA2 four-way handshake by forcing reinstallation of an already-used key, resetting nonces and enabling replay or decryption. It affected essentially every Wi-Fi client and drove firmware patches worldwide.

rowhammer.def: A hardware attack that flips memory bits by rapidly accessing neighboring rows.
rowhammer.context: Rowhammer exploits electrical interference in dense DRAM: hammering one row can flip bits in an adjacent row, corrupting memory and enabling privilege escalation. It blurs the line between software and hardware security.

bluekeep.def: A 2019 wormable flaw in Microsoft Remote Desktop Services.
bluekeep.context: BlueKeep was a pre-authentication remote code execution bug in RDP that could self-propagate like WannaCry. Its severity prompted rare patches even for out-of-support Windows versions.

zerologon.def: A 2020 flaw letting attackers take over a Windows domain controller.
zerologon.context: Zerologon exploited a cryptographic weakness in the Netlogon protocol so that an attacker on the network could reset a domain controller's machine password and seize control. It was rated maximum severity.

printnightmare.def: A 2021 set of flaws in the Windows Print Spooler enabling remote code execution.
printnightmare.context: PrintNightmare abused the Print Spooler service, which runs with high privileges and is enabled by default, to run attacker code and escalate to system. It led to emergency patches and guidance to disable the spooler where not needed.

proxylogon.def: A 2021 chain of Microsoft Exchange flaws exploited widely in the wild.
proxylogon.context: ProxyLogon combined a server-side request forgery with further bugs to achieve unauthenticated remote code execution on on-premises Exchange servers. It was mass-exploited before patches were widely applied.

proxyshell.def: A 2021 Exchange exploit chain related to ProxyLogon.
proxyshell.context: ProxyShell chained three Exchange vulnerabilities to reach remote code execution, and like ProxyLogon it was heavily exploited by multiple threat actors. Together they made Exchange patching an urgent priority that year.

mirai.def: A 2016 botnet that hijacked IoT devices to launch record DDoS attacks.
mirai.context: Mirai scanned for internet-of-things devices with default credentials and enlisted them into a botnet that took down major services through massive traffic floods. Its source code was released publicly, spawning many variants.

notpetya.def: A 2017 destructive malware outbreak disguised as ransomware.
notpetya.context: NotPetya spread using the same EternalBlue exploit as WannaCry but was designed to destroy data rather than collect ransom. It caused billions in damage to global companies and is widely attributed to a nation-state.

spring4shell.def: A 2022 remote code execution flaw in the Spring Framework for Java.
spring4shell.context: Spring4Shell let attackers achieve remote code execution through data binding in certain Spring configurations. Coming shortly after Log4Shell, it renewed attention on the risk carried by ubiquitous Java dependencies.

flapping.def: An interface or route that keeps going down and up, down and up - never quite dead, never quite stable.
flapping.context: A flapping link resets protocol adjacencies on every transition, so one bad SFP or loose patch cable can ripple through OSPF and BGP far beyond the port itself. BGP even grew a defense, route-flap damping, because a single flapping route can churn tables across the wider internet.

fat-finger.def: To cause an outage with a typo - the wrong command, the wrong device, or one digit off in a config line.
fat-finger.context: Half the war stories in networking start with a fat-fingered prefix, VLAN, or a reload typed into the wrong window. Change windows, peer review, and commit-confirm exist not because engineers are careless, but because everyone's fingers slip eventually.

cruft.def: The accumulated leftover junk in a system - old configs, dead code, objects nobody dares delete.
cruft.context: Every long-lived firewall policy and codebase grows cruft: rules for servers decommissioned years ago, objects named test2-DO-NOT-DELETE. Cleaning it is unglamorous and essential, because cruft is where surprises hide.

bit-bucket.def: The imaginary place discarded data goes - the destination of everything sent to /dev/null.
bit-bucket.context: Dropped packets, silenced logs, and ignored alerts all land in the bit bucket. On Unix it has a real address, /dev/null: the device that accepts everything and stores nothing.

thundering-herd.def: What happens when a crowd of clients all wake up, retry, or reconnect at exactly the same moment.
thundering-herd.context: A cache entry expires and a thousand requests hit the origin at once; a switch reboots and every device re-authenticates together. The fixes are jitter, backoff, and request coalescing - anything that turns a stampede back into a queue.

hairpin-nat.def: Reaching your own public address from inside your own network - the traffic goes out to the NAT and hairpins straight back in.
hairpin-nat.context: Without hairpin (loopback) NAT support, an internal client asking for the company's public IP gets nowhere, which is why split-horizon DNS often answers with the internal address instead. The name is the shape the traffic draws: out, U-turn, back.

eyeball-network.def: An ISP whose customers are people watching screens - the networks where the eyeballs are.
eyeball-network.context: Peering economics splits the internet into eyeball networks (residential ISPs, mobile carriers) and content networks (CDNs, clouds). Content wants to reach eyeballs, eyeballs want content close, and interconnection disputes between the two are where most peering drama lives.

five-nines.def: 99.999% availability - about five minutes of downtime allowed per year.
five-nines.context: The classic telecom gold standard. Each added nine costs roughly ten times more than the last: four nines is under an hour of downtime a year, five is minutes, six is seconds. The honest question is never whether you can hit five nines, but which failures the users actually feel.

war-room.def: The room, physical or virtual, where an incident's responders gather until the thing is fixed.
war-room.context: A war room concentrates the people, the timeline, and the decision-making in one place during a major outage. The modern version is a bridge call plus a dedicated chat channel; the ritual matters more than the walls.

snowflake-server.def: A server so hand-crafted and unique that nobody could rebuild it from scratch.
snowflake-server.context: Years of manual tweaks make a snowflake: beautiful, fragile, and unreproducible. The remedy is the cattle model - configuration as code, rebuildable from a definition - so that no machine is precious.

configuration-drift.def: The slow divergence between what a system is supposed to look like and what it actually runs.
configuration-drift.context: One emergency hotfix here, one manual tweak there, and six months later no two of the 'identical' devices match. Drift is why audits surprise people, and why declarative tooling keeps re-asserting the desired state instead of trusting the last change.

crash-cart.def: The wheeled cart with a monitor, keyboard, and cables that gets pushed to a server that stopped answering the network.
crash-cart.context: When SSH is dead and the out-of-band card was never configured, the crash cart is the last resort: console access at walking speed. Datacenters keep one per aisle for exactly that day.

tarpit.def: A defensive trick that answers attackers extremely slowly, wasting their time instead of refusing them.
tarpit.context: A tarpit accepts the connection and then drips out bytes, holding scanners and spam bots for minutes on what should take milliseconds. The German original, Teergrube, gave the technique its name during the spam wars.

script-kiddie.def: An attacker who runs other people's exploit tools without understanding how they work.
script-kiddie.context: Dismissive but useful: script kiddies are the background noise of the internet, scanning everything with downloaded kits. The insult hides a serious point - automation means even unskilled attackers find unpatched systems, so 'nobody would bother attacking us' has never been true.

shoulder-surfing.def: Stealing information the analog way: reading someone's screen or keyboard over their shoulder.
shoulder-surfing.context: Airport lounges, trains, and open-plan offices are the classic venues. Privacy screens, screen-lock habits, and not typing passwords during a projected demo are the countermeasures - the oldest attack in the book still works.

dumpster-diving.def: Recovering sensitive information from the trash - printouts, sticky notes, unwiped drives.
dumpster-diving.context: Old-school reconnaissance that still delivers: org charts, invoices, and password notes leave the building through the bin. Shredders and disk-wipe policies exist because the perimeter includes the dumpster.

juice-jacking.def: The attack theory that a public USB charging port could push malware or pull data while it charges your phone.
juice-jacking.context: USB carries data and power on the same connector, which makes the attack plausible; confirmed in-the-wild cases are rare, but the fix is cheap - a power-only cable, a data blocker, or simply your own charger.

bricked.def: Rendered as functional as a brick - a device so broken it will not even boot.
bricked.context: A failed firmware update is the classic cause: half-written flash, no bootloader, no recovery. Soft-bricked means a rescue path exists (safe mode, TFTP boot, console); hard-bricked means it is now a paperweight with ports.

borked.def: Broken, in the resigned voice of someone who has to fix it - as in 'the upgrade borked the standby unit'.
borked.context: Less formal than 'failed', more printable than the alternatives. A word for the state between working and diagnosed.

nuke-and-pave.def: To stop debugging a sick machine and simply wipe it and reinstall from scratch.
nuke-and-pave.context: At some point the honest cost-benefit says rebuilding beats untangling. Cheap and safe when configs are code and data is backed up; terrifying exactly when they are not - which is the real lesson.

dependency-hell.def: The state where software cannot be installed or upgraded because its dependencies' versions conflict.
dependency-hell.context: Package A needs libfoo 1.x, package B needs 2.x, and you need both. Windows called it DLL hell; every language ecosystem reinvents it. Lockfiles, vendoring, and containers are all, at heart, escapes from dependency hell.

braindump.def: A verbatim collection of leaked real exam questions, sold or shared so candidates can memorize their way to a certification.
braindump.context: The dark economy of the certification world. Using dumps violates every vendor's exam agreement, gets certifications revoked, and produces paper cert-holders who collapse at the first real device. The word also has an innocent sense - writing down everything you know before leaving a project - so context matters.

alert-fatigue.def: The numbness that sets in when monitoring pages so often that real alerts drown among the false ones.
alert-fatigue.context: Every ignored alarm trains the on-call brain that alarms do not matter - right up until one does. The cure is ruthless: every alert must be actionable, or it gets tuned, aggregated, or deleted.

alarm-storm.def: One real failure detonating into thousands of secondary alarms across the monitoring system.
alarm-storm.context: A core link dies and every device behind it screams at once, burying the root cause under its own symptoms. Event correlation and topology-aware suppression exist to turn the storm back into a single actionable page.

gray-failure.def: A failure that is real but not clean - the system is degraded in a way the health checks do not see.
gray-failure.context: The disk that is slow but not dead, the link dropping one packet in a thousand. Gray failures are nastier than outages because everything reports green while users suffer; catching them means measuring what users experience, not just whether the process is up.

goodput.def: The throughput that actually matters: useful application data delivered, after all the overhead and retransmissions.
goodput.context: A link can show high throughput while goodput is terrible - retransmissions, headers, and duplicate data all count as traffic but not as progress. It is the difference between the speedometer and actually arriving.

bufferbloat.def: Latency caused by oversized buffers: routers queue so much that packets arrive very late instead of being usefully dropped.
bufferbloat.context: Big buffers were meant to prevent loss, but a full deep buffer means every packet waits in a long line - which is why one big upload can make video calls stutter. Smart queue management (CoDel, FQ-CoDel, CAKE) drains the bloat.

microburst.def: A traffic spike so brief that it overflows a switch buffer and drops packets, yet vanishes inside the utilization averages.
microburst.context: The five-minute graph shows 30% while a two-millisecond burst overruns the port and TCP quietly retransmits. Classic in storage and trading networks; catching one takes high-resolution counters or buffer-drop statistics, not prettier averages.

elephant-flow.def: A single long-lived flow so large it dominates a link - one elephant among thousands of mice.
elephant-flow.context: Backups, replication, and bulk transfers are the elephants; interactive traffic is the mice. One elephant can fill a queue and trample the mice's latency, which is why QoS, ECMP hashing, and flow-aware scheduling all care about telling them apart.

living-off-the-land.def: Attacking with the tools already installed on the victim's systems, so nothing malicious ever has to be downloaded.
living-off-the-land.context: PowerShell, WMI, certutil, PsExec: all legitimate, all signed, all abusable. It defeats naive antivirus because there is no malware file to find; defenders answer with behavioral detection and with logging what the built-in tools are doing.

magic-smoke.def: The smoke inside electronics that, once released, can never be put back - the joke explanation for why fried hardware stays dead.
magic-smoke.context: 'You let the magic smoke out' is how engineers pronounce a component dead after a miswire or a power surge. The humor encodes a truth: electronics fail visibly exactly once.

tribal-knowledge.def: The critical operational knowledge that lives only in certain people's heads, never in the documentation.
tribal-knowledge.context: Why the failover script must run twice, which port is really connected where - the things you learn by asking the right veteran. It is the raw material of the bus factor, and writing it down is how teams stop being hostage to it.

flag-day.def: A cutover where everything must change at once, with no coexistence between old and new.
flag-day.context: The name honors the original: January 1, 1983, when the ARPANET switched from NCP to TCP/IP in a single day. Protocol designers have avoided flag days ever since - it is why IPv6 was built to run alongside IPv4 rather than replace it overnight.

forklift-upgrade.def: An upgrade so total that the old equipment leaves on a forklift - nothing is reused, everything is replaced.
forklift-upgrade.context: The opposite of an incremental migration: new chassis, new architecture, new cabling. Sometimes it genuinely beats nursing the installed base; vendors, naturally, tend to agree.

sev1.def: The highest incident severity: the service is down, customers feel it, and everything else can wait.
sev1.context: Declaring a SEV1 is a deliberate act - it summons the war room, wakes the escalation chain, and starts the clock on communications. Most organizations run SEV1 through SEV4; arguing about the number mid-incident is a smell of its own.

its-always-dns.def: The troubleshooting proverb that whatever the symptom, the root cause will turn out to be DNS.
its-always-dns.context: Half joke, half statistics: name resolution touches everything, fails in creative ways, and hides behind symptoms that look like anything else. The haiku version is canon: it's not DNS - there's no way it's DNS - it was DNS.

works-on-my-machine.def: The developer's classic defense when code fails everywhere except their own laptop.
works-on-my-machine.context: The phrase that launched a thousand containers. The gap between 'my machine' and production - versions, paths, environment variables, data - is precisely what reproducible builds, CI, and Docker exist to close.

s-in-iot.def: The joke that the S in IoT stands for security - there is no S, and too often no security either.
s-in-iot.context: A one-line audit of an industry: default passwords, unpatchable firmware, devices that outlive their vendors. Botnets like Mirai turned the punchline into an operational fact.

trust-but-verify.def: The old proverb of cooperative suspicion - accept the claim, then check it anyway.
trust-but-verify.context: A Russian proverb that Reagan made famous in arms control and IT made a habit. Zero trust sharpened it into 'never trust, always verify': identity and posture are checked continuously, not once at the door.

assume-breach.def: The security posture that plans as if attackers are already inside, because someday they will be.
assume-breach.context: It reframes the job from building a perfect wall to limiting what a successful intruder can reach: segmentation, least privilege, detection, rehearsed response. The grim industry version: there are two kinds of companies - those that have been breached, and those that do not know it yet.

never-touch-a-running-system.def: The conservative operator's creed: if it works, leave it alone.
never-touch-a-running-system.context: Born from hard experience with fragile systems, and half-obsolete in the patch-or-be-breached era: not touching a running system also means not patching it. The modern compromise is making change safe enough to do routinely rather than avoiding it.

read-only-friday.def: The ops tradition of making no production changes on Friday, because weekends exist.
read-only-friday.context: A deploy at five on Friday has a way of becoming a Saturday incident with half the team unreachable. Mature CI/CD teams argue the rule away - if deploys are that risky, fix the deploys - but the instinct it encodes is respect for blast radius and for people's time off.

hope-is-not-a-strategy.def: The SRE reminder that wishing a system stays up is not the same as engineering it to.
hope-is-not-a-strategy.context: Popularized by Google's SRE culture: reliability comes from measured objectives, error budgets, and rehearsed failure, not optimism. If the plan for a risk is 'it probably won't happen', there is no plan.

you-build-it-you-run-it.def: The DevOps principle that the team that writes a service also operates it in production.
you-build-it-you-run-it.context: Werner Vogels' phrase from Amazon, and the cultural end of throwing code over the wall: nothing teaches operability like being the one who gets paged. Ownership closes the loop between design decisions and 3 a.m. reality.

single-pane-of-glass.def: The perennial vendor promise of one dashboard that shows everything - and the engineer's shorthand for doubting it.
single-pane-of-glass.context: Every management product pitches one; most environments end up with a dozen single panes of glass. Useful as an aspiration, ironic as a description - usually said with one eyebrow raised.

two-is-one-one-is-none.def: The redundancy proverb: with two you can lose one; with one, a single failure leaves you with nothing.
two-is-one-one-is-none.context: Military in origin and native to network design: dual power feeds, dual supervisors, HA pairs, two of everything on the critical path. The corollary every operator learns: untested redundancy counts as one.

fail-open-vs-fail-closed.def: The design choice of what a control does when it breaks: allow everything through, or block everything.
fail-open-vs-fail-closed.context: A fire door fails open because life beats security; a vault fails closed for the opposite reason. Every inline control faces the same choice - an IPS that fails open keeps the business running but unprotected, while failing closed is safe and is also an outage. The right answer is a decision, never a default.

crunchy-outside-chewy-center.def: The classic critique of perimeter-only security: a hard shell around a soft, defenseless inside.
crunchy-outside-chewy-center.context: Bill Cheswick's 1990 description of firewalled networks - a sort of crunchy shell around a soft, chewy center - is still the sharpest one-liner about why one perimeter is not a strategy. Everything from segmentation to zero trust is an answer to it.

castle-and-moat.def: The perimeter security model: strong walls at the edge, broad trust for anyone already inside.
castle-and-moat.context: It matched the world where everything lived in one building behind one firewall. Cloud, SaaS, and remote work dissolved the moat - the resources left the castle - which is the whole argument for zero trust's per-request verification.

pcap-or-it-didnt-happen.def: The network engineer's evidence standard: claims about traffic are settled by a packet capture, not by opinion.
pcap-or-it-didnt-happen.context: Applications blame the network, the network blames the application, and the pcap ends the argument, because packets do not lie. It is the profession's version of 'citation needed'.

check-layer-1.def: The first commandment of troubleshooting: before anything clever, confirm the cable, the port, the power.
check-layer-1.context: The OSI model doubles as a debugging ladder, and an embarrassing share of 'routing problems' end at an unplugged patch lead or a dead SFP. Start at the bottom; the physical layer has no sense of humor.

schroedingers-backup.def: The state of any backup that has never been restored: simultaneously good and bad until someone opens the box.
schroedingers-backup.context: Backup jobs succeed for years while quietly producing nothing restorable - wrong scope, silent corruption, expired credentials. The operational form of the proverb: a backup does not exist until a restore has been tested.

three-two-one-backup.def: The backup rule of thumb: three copies of the data, on two different media, with one copy off-site.
three-two-one-backup.context: Simple enough to remember mid-disaster, and it survives most single failures: device death, site loss, even ransomware if the off-site copy is offline or immutable. The modern extension is 3-2-1-1-0: one copy offline, zero restore errors.

raid-is-not-a-backup.def: The storage truth that redundancy protects against disk failure, not against deletion, corruption, or ransomware.
raid-is-not-a-backup.context: RAID keeps a volume alive when a drive dies - and faithfully mirrors every mistake to all disks at once. Deleted the file? It is gone, redundantly. Availability and recoverability are different problems; a backup solves the second one.

security-is-a-process.def: Bruce Schneier's reminder that security is a process, not a product - nothing you buy makes you done.
security-is-a-process.context: Products help, but threats move, configurations drift, and people change; what endures is the loop of assessing, patching, monitoring, and responding. Anyone selling total security in a box is selling the noun without the verb.

dont-roll-your-own-crypto.def: The first commandment of applied cryptography: use the vetted algorithms and libraries, never your homemade ones.
dont-roll-your-own-crypto.context: Designing crypto that others cannot break is one of the hardest problems in the field, and every homemade cipher looks unbreakable to its author - Schneier's law in action. Rolling your own means your mistakes ship without ever meeting the years of public attack that hardened AES and TLS.

key-management-is-hard.def: The crypto veteran's summary: encrypting data is the easy part; protecting, rotating, and not losing the keys is the hard part.
key-management-is-hard.context: The algorithms are free and vetted; what fails in practice is the key left in a repository, the certificate nobody renewed, the HSM nobody can operate. It is the two-hard-things joke of the crypto world, and the reason key-management systems exist.

rough-consensus-and-running-code.def: The IETF's working philosophy: standards emerge from broad agreement plus implementations that actually work.
rough-consensus-and-running-code.context: David Clark's 1992 formulation - we reject kings, presidents and voting; we believe in rough consensus and running code - is why internet protocols are proven in interop before they are finished on paper. It is the cultural DNA behind every RFC this site teaches.

turtles-all-the-way-down.def: The recursion joke applied to trust: every layer rests on another layer, and eventually you must simply stand somewhere.
turtles-all-the-way-down.context: PKI ends at self-signed root CAs, DNS at the root servers, secure boot at a burned-in key - every chain of trust bottoms out in an anchor taken on faith. Naming the turtles is how you find out what you are actually trusting.

second-system-effect.def: Fred Brooks's warning that the successor to a successful system tends to be overengineered with every idea saved up from the first.
second-system-effect.context: The first system was kept humble by ignorance; the second gets all the features its designers wished for, and sinks under them. Protocols, products, and rewrites all fall for it - restraint is a second-system skill.

patch-tuesday-exploit-wednesday.def: The rhythm of Windows security: fixes ship on the second Tuesday, and reverse-engineered attacks follow within days.
patch-tuesday-exploit-wednesday.context: Microsoft's predictable cadence gave defenders a schedule - and gave attackers a diff to reverse into working exploits before slow organizations patch. The window between patch release and patch deployment is the whole game.

demo-gods.def: The capricious deities who decide whether a live demo works - having behaved perfectly in every rehearsal.
demo-gods.context: Every instructor and presenter knows the liturgy: the lab that ran flawlessly all week fails the moment an audience appears. Offerings include pre-recorded fallbacks, snapshots, and never typing live what a script can type for you.

two-generals.def: The classic proof that two parties on an unreliable channel can never be perfectly certain they agree.
two-generals.context: Each general needs the other's confirmation, the confirmation needs its own confirmation, forever - no finite number of messengers yields certainty. It is why exactly-once delivery over a lossy network is impossible in the strict sense, and why TCP settles for reliable-enough with acknowledgments and retries.

greenfield-vs-brownfield.def: Building on an empty field versus building inside what already exists - the two starting conditions of every project.
greenfield-vs-brownfield.context: Greenfield means freedom and no users; brownfield means constraints, migrations, and the installed base paying the bills. Most real work is brownfield, which is why 'how do we get there from here' matters more than the target architecture.

hot-warm-cold-standby.def: The three temperatures of a backup system: running and synchronized, running but behind, or powered off until needed.
hot-warm-cold-standby.context: The temperature buys recovery time: a hot standby takes over in seconds, a cold one after hours of restore. Cost scales the same way, which makes the choice a business decision wearing a technical costume.

active-active-vs-active-passive.def: The two shapes of high availability: both nodes serving traffic, or one serving while the other waits.
active-active-vs-active-passive.context: Active-passive is simpler to reason about and idles half the hardware; active-active uses everything and doubles the failure modes - notably the risk that each node runs fine alone but the pair cannot agree who owns what. BIG-IP HA pairs, firewall clusters, and databases all wrestle with the same trade.

baby-giant.def: A frame just slightly over the classic 1518-byte Ethernet maximum - grown by tags and labels, not by payload.
baby-giant.context: One 802.1Q VLAN tag makes 1522, QinQ makes 1526, a couple of MPLS labels push further. The payload is still a normal 1500 bytes; only the header grew. Switch platforms accept them by carrying frame headroom, which is why system MTUs read 9216 while the IP MTU stays put.

brownout.def: A service that is up but deliberately or visibly degraded - dimmed, like the power-grid original, rather than dark.
brownout.context: Feature brownouts are also a communication tool: briefly degrading a deprecated API warns integrators that the real shutdown is coming, in a way release notes never manage. The involuntary kind is just a gray failure with better branding.

dark-launch.def: Shipping a feature to production silently, running it on real traffic before any user can see it.
dark-launch.context: The code path executes, metrics and error rates accumulate, and the audience is zero - the feature flag stays off. It converts the scariest moment of a launch into a rehearsal with production-grade data.

tiger-team.def: A small, hand-picked group pulled out of normal structure to attack one hard problem until it yields.
tiger-team.context: The name comes from 1960s aerospace and earned immortality with Apollo 13's rescue effort. In security the term overlaps with red teams, but a tiger team is defined by its mission and temporariness, not by playing the adversary.

cowboy-coding.def: Development with no process between the idea and production: no review, no tests, no branch, just push.
cowboy-coding.context: Thrilling in a prototype, lethal in anything shared. The cowboy is not always wrong - speed has value - but the debt lands on whoever maintains the code, which is why teams fence the pasture with CI and review.

firefighting.def: Operating in permanent emergency mode: all effort goes to the incident of the day, none to preventing the next one.
firefighting.context: The trap is self-sustaining - time spent extinguishing leaves no time for fireproofing, which guarantees more fires. Escaping requires deliberately protecting non-urgent work, which is exactly what burning teams feel they cannot afford.

top-talkers.def: The hosts, flows, or applications consuming the most bandwidth on a link - the first list every traffic tool shows.
top-talkers.context: NetFlow, sFlow, and every monitoring dashboard lead with top talkers because the distribution is brutally unequal: a handful of conversations usually own the link. It answers the eternal question 'what is eating the WAN' in one sorted column.

clean-pipe.def: Internet transit that arrives already scrubbed of DDoS traffic - the provider filters the flood upstream and delivers only the clean flow.
clean-pipe.context: Sold by carriers and mitigation providers as a service: attack traffic is absorbed in their scrubbing centers, close to its sources, instead of saturating the customer's last mile. The metaphor is exact - the pipe is the same, the water is filtered before it reaches you.

swivel-chair.def: Integrating two systems with a human who swivels between screens, reading from one and retyping into the other.
swivel-chair.context: The oldest middleware in the world, and still everywhere: ticket data copied into the change system, alarms retyped into spreadsheets. Every swivel is latency, toil, and a transcription error waiting to happen - which is why 'swivel-chair integration' is usually said while writing the automation ticket.

shelfware.def: Software an organization bought, licensed, and never actually deployed - value sitting on a shelf.
shelfware.context: Born from bundle deals, over-optimistic projects, and compliance purchases. Security suffers a special variant: the tool is installed but never tuned, generating a comforting line item and no protection. License audits exist largely to find it.

vaporware.def: A product announced with fanfare that never ships - or ships so late and so diminished that the announcement was the product.
vaporware.context: A vintage industry word from the era of pre-announcements designed to freeze competitors' sales. The defensive reflex it taught still applies to roadmap slides: ship dates are facts, announcements are marketing.

bit-banging.def: Driving a hardware protocol entirely in software, toggling a pin bit by bit instead of using a dedicated controller.
bit-banging.context: How you speak SPI or serial on a chip that lacks the peripheral: the CPU personally raises and lowers the line with careful timing. Inefficient, fragile at speed, and occasionally the only way - a rite of passage in embedded work.

crown-jewels.def: The small set of assets whose compromise would actually hurt: the data, keys, and systems the business cannot lose.
crown-jewels.context: Security budgets are finite, so the crown-jewels exercise forces the uncomfortable ranking: which systems justify the strongest controls. The related phrase 'keys to the kingdom' names the credentials - domain admin, root CA, break-glass accounts - that unlock all of it at once.

lift-and-shift.def: Migrating a workload to the cloud exactly as it is - same VM, same architecture, new landlord.
lift-and-shift.context: The fastest migration and the least transformative: the application gains none of the elasticity it is now paying cloud prices for. Sometimes that is the right call (deadlines, datacenter exits), but the bill eventually argues for re-architecting.

god-box.def: A single appliance that does everything - routing, firewalling, VPN, IPS, load balancing - one box to rule them all.
god-box.context: Beloved by budgets and branch offices, feared by architects: the god box is a single point of failure with a very long blast radius, and its jack-of-all-trades feature set rarely matches best-of-breed depth. The eternal trade against complexity and cost.

pizza-box.def: A flat 1U rack-mount server or switch - the form factor of a large pizza, stacked by the dozen.
pizza-box.context: The visual vocabulary of every datacenter aisle: racks of pizza boxes, occasionally interrupted by a chassis. The shape won because it slices compute into rack-unit-sized, individually replaceable pieces.

frobnicate.def: To fiddle with a setting or object, adjusting it without deep intent - classic hacker-dictionary vocabulary.
frobnicate.context: From the Jargon File, with a documented scale of intent: you frob a knob aimlessly, twiddle it with mild purpose, and tweak it with precision. Surviving in the wild mostly as 'frob', it signals fluency in the culture's oldest dialect.

keep-the-lights-on.def: The baseline operational work that keeps existing systems running - maintenance, patching, renewals, the unglamorous constant.
keep-the-lights-on.context: KTLO is the budget line every transformation initiative resents and depends on. When it consumes the whole team, nothing new ever ships; when it is starved, the lights literally go out. Mature organizations measure the ratio.

incast.def: The many-to-one traffic collapse: dozens of servers answer one request simultaneously and their combined burst overruns the receiver's switch port.
incast.context: The signature pathology of distributed storage and big-data queries - each sender behaves, the sum does not. The microburst overflows shallow buffers, TCP timeouts synchronize, and throughput craters. Fixes live in buffer tuning, ECN, and application-level request pacing.

lit-fiber.def: Fiber with the electronics attached and light flowing - a working service, as opposed to dark fiber you light yourself.
lit-fiber.context: The buy-versus-build of the physical layer: lit service means the provider owns the optics and the wavelengths, dark fiber means you do. 'Lit building' is the sales version - the fiber is already there, turn-up is fast.

middle-mile.def: The network segment between the local access network and the internet's core - the haul from the neighborhood to the exchange.
middle-mile.context: The last mile gets the attention, but rural broadband economics usually die in the middle mile: backhauling a small town's traffic hundreds of kilometers to the nearest interconnection point. Subsidy programs and open-access networks target exactly this segment.

meet-me-room.def: The room in a carrier-neutral facility where networks physically interconnect - the cross-connect floor where the internet is literally patched together.
meet-me-room.context: An MMR is racks of demarcation panels and a spider web of fiber jumpers: your router, their router, one cable. The room's neutrality is the product - any tenant can reach any carrier without touching the street.

carrier-hotel.def: A building whose tenants are networks: dozens of carriers and providers colocated where fiber routes converge.
carrier-hotel.context: The great interconnection buildings - the famous downtown addresses where transatlantic cables, backbones, and exchanges meet - concentrate staggering shares of a region's traffic. Their meet-me rooms are why 'where is the internet' has surprisingly literal answers.

looking-glass.def: A public web interface into a network's router, letting outsiders run ping, traceroute, and BGP queries from inside that network.
looking-glass.context: The courtesy tool of the ISP world: when routing looks wrong, you check how the routes appear from the other side. Debugging asymmetric paths and hijacks without looking glasses would be guesswork - they are peer review for the routing table.

default-free-zone.def: The part of the internet whose routers carry the full routing table and no default route - nowhere to forward blindly.
default-free-zone.context: In the DFZ, every destination must be explicitly known or it does not exist - close to a million IPv4 prefixes and growing. Being default-free is what separates the core from the customers; everyone else can shrug and point a default at their provider.

full-table.def: The complete set of internet routes, taken over BGP from a transit provider - the whole map, not a summary.
full-table.context: 'Taking full tables' is the moment a network graduates: with the complete table from two or more providers, the router can pick the genuinely best exit per prefix instead of trusting a default. The price is memory, convergence time, and operating like an adult in the DFZ.

gold-plating.def: Adding polish and features nobody asked for - effort spent past the point where the requirement was met.
gold-plating.context: The engineer's version of over-delivering: beautiful abstractions for one caller, configurability no one will use. It feels like quality and behaves like scope creep with better manners; YAGNI is the standing antidote.

burn-rate.def: How fast a finite budget is being consumed - cash in a startup, error budget in SRE practice.
burn-rate.context: The SRE borrowing is precise: if the month's reliability allowance is burning at three times the sustainable rate, alerts should fire long before the budget is gone. Same instinct as the startup runway - the number matters less than the slope.

abandonware.def: Software whose vendor is gone or has walked away: no patches, no support, no legal way to buy it - and often no way to run it safely.
