abandonware.context: Museums and emulation communities preserve the culture; enterprises meet the darker variant when a critical system's vendor evaporates and the unpatched binary becomes a permanent risk. Escrow clauses and open-source exits exist for exactly that ending.

bloatware.def: Unwanted software that arrives pre-installed - trials, toolbars, vendor utilities - consuming resources nobody offered.
bloatware.context: The economics are simple: manufacturers are paid to preload it. The first ritual of a new corporate laptop image is scraping it off, which is why golden images and clean OS installs are IT hygiene, not preference.

drinking-from-the-firehose.def: Absorbing information far faster than anyone could - the universal description of onboarding into a complex system.
drinking-from-the-firehose.context: New hires, new codebases, new incident bridges: the volume is the hazing. The useful response is triage, not heroics - decide what to actually swallow, and where the rest is written down for later.

if-it-hurts-do-it-more-often.def: The counterintuitive delivery principle: painful, risky activities become safe through frequency, not avoidance.
if-it-hurts-do-it-more-often.context: Popularized by Martin Fowler about deployment and integration: rare releases accumulate risk and atrophy the muscles; frequent ones force automation and shrink each change's blast radius. The direct philosophical rival of read-only Friday - and the argument that eventually wins.

you-cant-secure-what-you-cant-see.def: The visibility maxim: security begins with an accurate inventory of what exists, because unknown assets get no controls.
you-cant-secure-what-you-cant-see.context: Why asset management leads every serious controls framework, unglamorous as it is. Shadow IT, forgotten subdomains, and orphaned cloud accounts are breaches waiting patiently for their moment; discovery is the first control.

no-plan-survives-contact.def: Moltke's maxim: the plan is what you have until reality responds, then adaptation is the actual skill.
no-plan-survives-contact.context: Field marshal Helmuth von Moltke's original point was not to skip planning but to plan for deviation - which is exactly the argument for incident runbooks that rehearse decision-making rather than script it, and for DR tests that expect surprises.

make-it-work-make-it-right.def: Kent Beck's ordering of concerns: first correctness, then design, then - and only then - speed.
make-it-work-make-it-right.context: The discipline is in the sequence: resisting cleverness before the thing works, and resisting optimization before the design is honest. Most premature-optimization sins are a violation of step three's position in the queue.

canary-in-the-coal-mine.def: A sacrificial early-warning signal: something small that suffers first, so the rest can react in time.
canary-in-the-coal-mine.context: Miners' literal canaries became the vocabulary of modern operations: canary deployments send a slice of traffic to the new version and watch it before the fleet follows; canary tokens are honeytoken credentials whose only job is to scream when touched.

weakest-link.def: The chain proverb applied to security: the system's real strength is its most neglected component, not its best one.
weakest-link.context: Attackers do not duel your strongest control; they route around it - through the unpatched box, the reused password, the helpful human. The corollary shapes strategy: uniform decent coverage beats one magnificent wall with a gap.

attackers-only-need-to-be-right-once.def: The defender's dilemma: attackers can fail repeatedly at no cost, while defense must hold everywhere, always.
attackers-only-need-to-be-right-once.context: True as economics, and worth the modern counterpoint: a defender only needs to detect once, and an intruder must stay invisible through every step of a long kill chain. Assume-breach thinking exists to turn that second asymmetry into practice.

street-finds-its-own-uses.def: William Gibson's observation that technology's real uses are invented by its users, not its designers.
street-finds-its-own-uses.context: SMS, spreadsheets as databases, GPS as a fleet-discipline tool, DNS as a covert channel: every deployed system develops a second, unplanned life. Security consequences included - the street's creativity works both directions.

nobody-knows-youre-a-dog.def: The 1993 New Yorker cartoon that named the internet's founding identity condition: online, nobody can tell who or what you are.
nobody-knows-youre-a-dog.context: Peter Steiner's two dogs at a computer became the most-reproduced summary of online anonymity ever drawn. Thirty years of identity engineering - PKI, MFA, KYC, verified badges - is one long argument with that caption, and the dog is still winning more often than the industry admits.

routes-around-damage.def: John Gilmore's line that the Net treats blockage as a fault to route around - resilience as a design temperament.
routes-around-damage.context: The full 1993 quote names censorship as the damage; the engineering half is simply true (dynamic routing exists to survive failures), while the political half has aged into an open argument. Both halves get invoked, often without noticing they differ.

swiss-cheese-model.def: James Reason's accident model: every defense layer has holes, and disasters happen when the holes line up.
swiss-cheese-model.context: Each slice - firewall, review, monitoring, training - is imperfect, which is fine; the design goal is ensuring the holes never align. It is the theory underneath defense in depth, and the honest answer to 'but we had a control for that': you had one slice.

testing-in-production.def: The practice - reckless or disciplined, depending entirely on tooling - of validating changes on the live system.
testing-in-production.context: As a punchline it mocks missing test environments; as a discipline (feature flags, canaries, dark launches, chaos engineering) it acknowledges that production is the only environment that tells the whole truth. The joke and the strategy are separated by observability and a rollback path.

perfect-is-the-enemy-of-good.def: Voltaire's warning that pursuing the flawless blocks shipping the sufficient.
perfect-is-the-enemy-of-good.context: In engineering it pairs with the ninety-ninety rule: the last polish costs the most and pays the least. The judgment call it demands - which imperfections are acceptable - is the actual job; the proverb just forbids answering 'none'.

slow-is-smooth-smooth-is-fast.def: The special-operations cadence rule: hurrying breeds errors, and errors cost more time than care ever did.
slow-is-smooth-smooth-is-fast.context: During incidents it is the antidote to panic typing: the deliberate command executed once beats the fast one executed three times with a rollback in between. Change windows run on the same physics.

belt-and-suspenders.def: Wearing two independent safeguards for the same failure - if one lets go, the trousers stay up.
belt-and-suspenders.context: The idiom for deliberate redundancy of controls: backup and replication, MFA and conditional access, dual power feeds. The word 'independent' carries the weight; two straps anchored to the same failing point are one strap with extra steps.

only-secure-computer.def: Gene Spafford's reductio: the only truly secure computer is off, encased in concrete, guarded - and useless.
only-secure-computer.context: The point is not despair but framing: security is not a reachable end state, it is a trade against usefulness, managed as risk. Every real system lives somewhere on the line between the concrete block and the open door.

all-models-are-wrong.def: George Box's statistics proverb: every model simplifies falsely - the question is whether it is useful anyway.
all-models-are-wrong.context: Monitoring dashboards, capacity forecasts, risk matrices, network diagrams: all wrong, some useful. The operational discipline is remembering which simplifications each model made, because that is exactly where it will mislead you.

map-is-not-the-territory.def: Korzybski's warning that representations are not the reality they describe - and confusing them has consequences.
map-is-not-the-territory.context: The network diagram, the CMDB, the runbook: maps, drawn once, aging quietly while the territory drifts. Incidents love the gap - the documented topology says the link exists, the packets disagree. Trust the territory; audit the maps.

everything-fails-all-the-time.def: Werner Vogels' cloud axiom: at sufficient scale, failure is not an event but a permanent background condition.
everything-fails-all-the-time.context: With enough disks, links, and instances, something is always broken - so architecture must assume it: redundancy, graceful degradation, retries with backoff. The mindset shift is from preventing failure to making it boring.

no-solutions-only-tradeoffs.def: Thomas Sowell's economics dictum, adopted whole by engineering: every choice purchases its benefits with costs elsewhere.
no-solutions-only-tradeoffs.context: Consistency versus availability, security versus usability, flexibility versus simplicity - the mature design conversation is not 'which option is right' but 'which costs can we live with'. Anyone selling a pure solution has hidden the invoice.

choose-boring-technology.def: Dan McKinley's principle: prefer proven, well-understood tools, and spend your few innovation tokens deliberately.
choose-boring-technology.context: Boring means known failure modes, deep operational lore, and answers on the first search-results page. The essay's framing - each team can afford maybe three genuinely novel choices - turned technology selection from taste into budgeting.

security-theater.def: Bruce Schneier's term for measures that perform protection visibly while providing little of it.
security-theater.context: The value of the term is the test it implies: does this control change an attacker's calculus, or an observer's feelings? Some theater is even defensible - visible deterrence has effects - but only if someone, somewhere, knows which category the measure is in.

compliance-is-not-security.def: The audit-season reminder that passing a checklist proves documentation, not protection.
compliance-is-not-security.context: Frameworks set floors and attackers read the same checklists everyone else does. Breached organizations with clean audits are an industry constant. The mature stance keeps both books: compliance as the reporting layer, security as the engineering it is supposed to describe.

zawinskis-law.def: Every program attempts to expand until it can read mail; those which cannot are replaced by ones which can.
zawinskis-law.context: Jamie Zawinski's satire of software envelopment, aging beautifully: substitute 'embed a browser', 'add a chat', or 'ship an AI assistant' and the law still compiles. The underlying force is real - products grow toward platforms until something leaner eats them.

segals-law.def: A man with a watch knows what time it is; a man with two watches is never sure.
segals-law.context: The proverb of conflicting authorities, and networking's favorite in-joke: it is literally why NTP exists and why serious timekeeping uses three or more sources with an algorithm to detect the liar. Two monitoring systems disagreeing about an outage is Segal's law with paging enabled.

free-as-in-beer.def: The disambiguation of 'free software': gratis (free beer) versus libre (free speech) - price versus liberty.
free-as-in-beer.context: English overloads one word where other languages have two, so the movement coined the beer test. The distinction has teeth: freeware costs nothing and grants nothing, while open source may cost money and still grant everything that matters - the source, the rights, the exit.

xy-problem.def: Asking for help with your attempted solution (Y) instead of your actual problem (X) - and burning everyone's time on the wrong question.
xy-problem.context: The support-forum classic: 'how do I extract the last 3 characters of a filename' when the real goal is the extension. The cure is on both sides: askers state the underlying goal; helpers ask 'what are you actually trying to do' before optimizing the wrong path.

single-source-of-truth.def: The principle that each fact should have exactly one authoritative home, with everything else derived from it.
single-source-of-truth.context: Two authoritative spreadsheets is how organizations institutionalize Segal's law. In practice SSOT means declaring which system wins - the IPAM, the repo, the CMDB - and making every copy provably downstream. Configuration drift is what happens when nobody declared.

safe-method.def: An HTTP method whose defined semantics request no state change on the server - reading, not writing.
safe-method.context: RFC 9110's safe set is GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, and TRACE; RFC 10008 added QUERY in 2026. The designation is a registered promise about intent that caches, prefetchers, and crawlers act on mechanically - and it says nothing about the payload, so a safe method's body still deserves full security inspection.

http-query-method.def: The HTTP method standardized in June 2026 (RFC 10008) for read-only queries that carry a request body: safe and idempotent like GET, body like POST, cacheable with the body in the cache key.
http-query-method.context: The first new HTTP method since PATCH in 2010, it exists because GET forces queries into the URL and POST-as-search hides its read-only nature from every cache and proxy in the path. Adoption is a per-layer audit: WAF method allowlists written before mid-2026 block or blind-pass it, CORS requires a preflight, HTML forms fall back to GET, and any cache must key on the full body or invite poisoning.

cors-preflight.def: The OPTIONS request a browser sends first, asking the server's permission, before it is willing to send certain cross-origin requests at all.
cors-preflight.context: A cross-origin request only skips the preflight when it is "simple": method GET, HEAD, or POST, with safelisted headers and content types. Anything else - PUT, DELETE, PATCH, the new QUERY, or a custom header like Authorization - makes the browser first send OPTIONS with Access-Control-Request-Method and -Headers; only if the server answers with matching Access-Control-Allow-* headers does the real request follow. Most "blocked by CORS" errors are actually a preflight the server answered badly or not at all, and Access-Control-Max-Age is how servers let browsers cache the permission instead of asking before every request.

scim.def: System for Cross-domain Identity Management: the open standard for provisioning - creating, updating, grouping, and deactivating user accounts across systems over a JSON REST API, with schemas from RFC 7643 and the protocol from RFC 7644.
scim.context: SAML and OIDC sign an account in; SCIM is how the account got there and how it is switched off the day the person leaves.

kerberos.def: The ticket-based network authentication protocol behind enterprise silent sign-on: a Key Distribution Center issues time-limited tickets that services can verify offline, so passwords travel once at login and never again.
kerberos.context: In Active Directory environments Kerberos is the default authentication fabric, and web SSO products ride it through SPNEGO for prompt-free desktop login.

spnego.def: Simple and Protected GSS-API Negotiation Mechanism: the wrapper that carries a Kerberos service ticket inside HTTP's Negotiate authentication scheme, letting a browser authenticate to a website silently from the user's existing domain session.
spnego.context: The server challenges with WWW-Authenticate: Negotiate; a browser configured to trust the site answers with a ticket - the machinery marketed as Integrated Windows Authentication.

kdc.def: Key Distribution Center: the Kerberos authority - Authentication Service plus Ticket-Granting Service - that verifies principals and issues the TGT and service tickets; in Active Directory it runs on the domain controllers.
kdc.context: Clients talk to the KDC at login and when first reaching each service; after that, tickets verify offline, which is why KDC placement and time sync matter more than KDC throughput.

keytab.def: A file holding a service account's long-term Kerberos keys, letting a service - often on Linux, outside the domain - decrypt the tickets clients present without interactive domain membership.
keytab.context: A keytab is a credential in file form: generated against the account that owns the SPN, protected like a password, and regenerated whenever that account's password changes.

password-credential-validator.def: PingFederate's reusable password-checking component: given a username and password, a PCV answers valid or invalid against a source such as an LDAP directory - and adapters consume it rather than users meeting it directly.
password-credential-validator.context: Factoring validation out of the adapters means the login form and the HTTP Basic challenge can share one PCV, and changing the password source touches one definition.

authentication-adapter.def: The PingFederate component that actually authenticates a user - or receives one authenticated elsewhere: HTML Form, HTTP Basic, Kerberos, OpenToken, and Reference ID each embody a different integration pattern.
authentication-adapter.context: Adapters are the leaves of authentication policy trees; the policy decides which adapter a given user meets, in what order, with what fallbacks.

authentication-policy-contract.def: The normalized attribute set an authentication policy fulfills on every successful path: whatever adapter or IdP connection authenticated the user, downstream configuration consumes the same contract.
authentication-policy-contract.context: The APC is the boundary where 'how the user authenticated' stops mattering - SP connections and OAuth mappings bind to the contract, never to individual sources.

authentication-policy-tree.def: PingFederate's runtime decision tree for authentication: nodes are authentication sources and selectors, Fail and Success branches express fallbacks, reusable fragments capture common sequences, and every successful path ends by fulfilling a policy contract.
authentication-policy-tree.context: One tree routes different populations differently - corporate network tries Kerberos and falls back to the form, sensitive applications add a second factor.

opentoken.def: Ping Identity's encrypted token format for integration kits: the authenticated identity travels between PingFederate and an application as an OpenToken carried by cookie, query parameter, or POST.
opentoken.context: The OpenToken adapter runs both directions - handing a user to an agent-equipped application after SSO, or accepting one the application already authenticated.

data-store.def: A reusable connection definition to an external identity source - an LDAP directory with failover hosts and a service bind, or a JDBC database with its driver and validation query - configured once and referenced by validators, attribute lookups, and provisioning.
data-store.context: The indirection is the point: rotate the service credential or move the directory, and every consumer follows one changed definition.

identity-provider.def: The party that authenticates the user and asserts the result: in SAML the IdP issues assertions, in OIDC the same role is the OpenID Provider issuing ID tokens.
identity-provider.context: Every federation names one side the identity provider and the other the service provider - who authenticates, and who trusts.

service-provider.def: The application side of a federation: the party that consumes assertions or tokens and grants access based on them - SAML's SP, OIDC's relying party.
service-provider.context: SP-initiated sign-on starts here - the user arrives at the application first, and it sends them to the identity provider with a request to authenticate.

certGuides.title: Certification study guides
certGuides.lede: Blueprint-guided study maps for vendor certification exams. Every objective from the published exam blueprint is linked to the articles, tools, and official manual pages that teach it, so you study the right things in the right order.
certGuides.philosophyBody: The exam blueprint is the spine. Each objective points to deterministic, grounded resources already on this site plus the vendor's own documentation. No filler, and no guessing which topics matter.
certGuides.ethicsBody: These are independent study aids built from the public exam blueprint. They contain no exam questions, answers, or brain dumps, which violate every vendor's certification agreement. They help you learn the material, not shortcut the exam.
certGuides.credentialsPointer: Looking for Rodolfo Nützmann's own certifications? They live under About
certGuides.requiresAll: Earned by passing all {count} exams, in any order.
certGuides.objectivesCount: {count, plural, =0 {Blueprint pending} one {# objective} other {# objectives}}
certGuides.versionUnspecified: Product version not specified in the blueprint
certGuides.disclaimer: Independent study aid built from the public exam blueprint. Not affiliated with, authorized by, or endorsed by {vendor}. Exam objectives, versions, and availability change, so always verify against the official blueprint.
certGuides.preparingBody: The official blueprint for this exam is being mapped to the site's articles, tools, and manual pages. In the meantime, the exam's official page is linked above.

trainingCta.body: Prefer to learn with an instructor? Rodolfo Nützmann and other authorized instructors teach these courses live at <redu>Red Education</redu>, a global Authorized Training Center.

redEducation.metaTitle: Red Education - Global Authorized Training Center
redEducation.metaDescription: Red Education is a global Authorized Training Center for the world's leading network and security vendors, with more than 100,000 students trained across 132 countries. Rodolfo Nützmann teaches there as a Senior Instructor.
redEducation.title: Red Education
redEducation.lede: A global Authorized Training Center for the world's leading network and security vendors - and the home of Rodolfo Nützmann's classroom since 2021.
redEducation.whoBody1: Founded in 2005, Red Education is a specialist IT and cyber security training company that has trained more than 100,000 students across 132 countries, holding a 4.9-star average rating from more than 5,000 reviews.
redEducation.whoBody2: Training is delivered by more than 50 instructors, many certified across multiple vendors, in classroom, virtual, and on-site formats across five global regions: the Americas, Australasia, SAARC, ASEAN, and EMEA.
redEducation.visionBody1: We are passionate about empowering every student with the necessary skills and knowledge by providing the ultimate technical training experience.
redEducation.visionBody2: Delivered in an environment that supports quality adoption and understanding.
redEducation.visionBody3: We successfully enable and deploy IT training solutions across our valued customer ecosystems.

values.v3: We show enthusiasm in serving our clients.
values.v5: Harmony, we all share one voice.

redEducation.authIntro: Red Education delivers vendor-accredited training as an authorized partner of the world's leading network and security vendors, including:
redEducation.awardF5: Best F5 ATC Award 2020/21, with a 98% customer satisfaction rating on F5 courses.
redEducation.awardFortinet: Fortinet Training Institute ATC Partner of the Year for APAC (2026 ATC Awards).
redEducation.rodolfoBody: Rodolfo Nützmann has taught through Red Education since 2021, and full-time as a Senior Instructor since 2023. Through Red Education, he delivers authorized instructor-led training for F5, Fortinet, Netskope, and Extreme Networks to students around the world - with Ping Identity and Zscaler, the authorizations cleared in 2026, joining the roster through the same house.
redEducation.caseStudyNote: Red Education's own case study on upskilling 43 engineers at a global IT firm names Rodolfo among the instructors who delivered the training.
redEducation.linkTraining: See the courses taught by Rodolfo
redEducation.tribute: I began teaching through Red Education in 2021 and joined full-time as a Senior Instructor in 2023, which turned out to be one of the best professional decisions of my career. Behind every class I deliver stands an operations team spanning five regions that makes complex logistics look effortless, account managers who genuinely care about outcomes, and a standard of quality that pushes me to be better every single session. This page is my public thank-you to the people of Red Education, for trusting me with their classrooms and their students.
redEducation.ctaBody: Courses taught by Rodolfo Nützmann and other authorized instructors can be booked directly at Red Education, worldwide, in classroom, virtual, or on-site formats.

toolDocs.metaDescription: How the {tool} tool works: what it computes, how to use it, and the sources it is grounded in.
toolDocs.lede: How the {tool} tool works, what it computes, and the sources behind it.

lineages.title: Vendor lineages
lineages.lede: The enterprise-networking and security industry consolidated relentlessly. These are the corporate genealogies behind the vendors - who acquired whom, and what each purchase became. Every figure is cross-checked against primary filings.
lineages.intro: Start with F5, whose platform was assembled largely by acquisition. More vendors follow.
lineages.f5CardDesc: From one load balancer (1996) to an app delivery and security platform, via 23+ acquisitions.
lineages.moreSoon: More vendor lineages are being researched and added - each verified against primary sources before it ships.

partnerVendors.disclaimerText: Rodolfo does not deliver {vendor} training. His own authorized instruction covers F5, Fortinet, Netskope, Extreme Networks, Ping Identity, and Zscaler. {vendor} is taught by Red Education's instructor team - among the most awarded in the industry, with recognition granted by multiple vendors.
partnerVendors.reduSectionNote: Vendors Red Education is authorized to deliver training for. These are not part of Rodolfo's own authorized instruction - his covers F5, Fortinet, Netskope, Extreme Networks, Ping Identity, and Zscaler.
partnerVendors.otherSectionTitle: Other vendors - lineages of the pioneers
partnerVendors.otherSectionNote: Corporate genealogies of the companies that shaped networking, telecom, and cybersecurity - who founded them, what they built, and where their IP and people live today. History pages only: no training association is implied.
partnerVendors.contemporarySectionTitle: The contemporaries - still writing their chapters
partnerVendors.contemporarySectionNote: Companies of the modern era whose stories are still unfolding - no training association; profiled for what they mean to the industry today.

industry.metaTitle: Industry - corporate lineages of networking and security
industry.metaDescription: Deep-research corporate genealogies of the networking and security industry: foundings, acquisitions, and where every bloodline ended up. Written from primary sources.
industry.title: The industry, from the inside
industry.lede: The corporate genealogies of networking and security: who founded each company, what they built, who bought whom, and where every bloodline ended up. Written from primary sources - SEC filings, inquiry reports, and the trade press of each era.
industry.careerNote: Worked with directly between 1996 and 2020, in chronological order of first engagement.
industry.careerStripNote: These fourteen are also chapters of the author's own record - each timeline carries a Rodolfo's chapter marker. The full career telling lives at the career record.

sitemap.metaDescription: A human-readable map of ronutz.com: tools, Learn articles, the glossary, vendor hubs, training, the industry lineages, and the career record.
sitemap.title: Sitemap
sitemap.lede: Every section of this site on one page. The machine-readable version lives at sitemap.xml; this is the human one.

studyGuidesIndex.title: Study guides
studyGuidesIndex.lede: Two ways to study here, both free. Curated reading paths walk the Learn library in teaching order - each one an ordered sequence of articles with the tools to practice on. Certification study guides map official exam blueprints objective by objective to articles, tools, and the vendor's own manuals.
studyGuidesIndex.pathsLede: Each path is a syllabus without an exam: read in order, practice with the tools as you go. Everything computes locally and nothing is paywalled.
studyGuidesIndex.certLede: Blueprint-mapped guides for the exams behind the credentials - every objective tied to the articles that teach it, the tools that exercise it, and the official manual pages that remain the source of truth.

bigip-fundamentals.title: F5 BIG-IP end to end
bigip-fundamentals.lede: Everything the retired 101, 201, 301A, and 301B blueprints listed, as this site's articles in teaching order: the networking fundamentals the modern track assumes (OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) to VPN (virtual private network)), the full administration arc from interfaces to high availability to the support workflows the new exams dropped, and the LTM (Local Traffic Manager) specialist craft - iRules, analytics, captures, SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) - closing with the two articles that compare the retired blueprints against the current ones.

modern-identity.title: Modern identity, from the token up
modern-identity.lede: The open standards behind single sign-on, in dependency order: the JWT (JSON Web Token) itself, the signatures and JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) that make it verifiable, the OAuth 2.0 and PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) flows that mint it, OIDC (OpenID Connect) on top, and TOTP/HOTP (time-based and HMAC-based one-time passwords) as the second factor.

tls-from-zero.title: TLS from zero
tls-from-zero.lede: TLS (Transport Layer Security) from the cipher suite string upward: what each part of a suite name means, what TLS 1.3 removed and why, how hybrid post-quantum key exchange works, and how TLS 1.2, TLS 1.3, DTLS (Datagram TLS), and QUIC relate as one family.

http-evolution.title: HTTP, told forward
http-evolution.lede: HTTP (the Hypertext Transfer Protocol) across its five versions - from the one-line GET of 1991 to HTTP/3 on QUIC - then the operational layer: forward and reverse proxies, HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security), and curl beyond the browser.

regex-mastery.title: Regular expressions, properly
regex-mastery.lede: The craft skill under log analysis and every parser: quantifiers and classes, groups and backreferences, anchors and boundaries, flags and modes - ending on catastrophic backtracking, the failure mode every production regex must avoid.

pingfederate-administration.title: PingFederate administration, end to end
pingfederate-administration.lede: The blueprint's assumed fundamentals first - the directory model and the Kerberos ticket machinery - then the ten product articles in teaching order, SCIM beside the provisioning stop, and the SSO flows to close. The reading spine behind the PFP-001 certification guide.

catDistribution.metaDescription: The tracking console for the internet's most reliable logistics network: stray cats assigning themselves to humans. Enter your name; the System does the rest. Consent was never part of the protocol.
catDistribution.title: The Global Cat Distribution System
catDistribution.tagline: You do not choose the cat. The cat has already chosen.
catDistribution.intro: The Cat Distribution System is the internet's name for a well-documented phenomenon: you do not acquire a stray cat, a stray cat acquires you. This console tracks your assignment. Enter your name (city optional, the System knows anyway) and read the delivery manifest. Assignments are deterministic: the same name always receives the same cat. That is not a bug. That is destiny.
catDistribution.emptyHint: Enter a name. The System is patient. The System is always patient.
catDistribution.stampSub: Resistance is not filed under available options. Prepare a warm surface and lower your expectations of furniture.
catDistribution.creditNote: The Cat Distribution System is genuine internet folklore: the observation, popularized in the early 2020s, that stray cats appear precisely when and where they decide to. No cats were algorithmically generated; all cats are real and currently on their way.
coats: orange tabby (one shared braincell, currently checked out)
void (a black cat; absorbs light and attention)
siamese (equipped with voice module, volume knob missing)
white with one spot (the spot is load-bearing)
ages: unknown (the System does not ask; the cat does not tell)
vectors: engine bay of a parked car (warm-start protocol)
falls in behind you on the sidewalk and escorts you home
follows you home from the bus stop
appears at an open window, already halfway in
appears on the balcony; provides no explanation
cardboard box in a parking lot, pre-labeled 'free'
office parking lot, staring directly into your soul
steps: Application auto-filed on your behalf. The System requires no consent.
Application approved. It was approved before you finished typing.
Unit assigned from regional stray pool.
Unit is observing you. Assessment: adequate.
Delivery vector locked. Weather conditions being arranged.
Delivery pending. Do not resist. Acquire treats.
